# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pool Fire – Theoretical Analysis

A pool fire is governed by three coupled phenomena: (a) the rate at which liquid fuel is vaporized, (b) the geometric shape of the flame, and (c) the thermal radiation emitted.

### Step 2.1.1 — Mass Burning Rate ($\dot{m}''$)
The mass burning rate per unit area depends on pool diameter, fuel thermophysics, and wind. The **Babrauskas correlation** is the most widely cited:

$$\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 1.27 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \rho_{L} \cdot \frac{\Delta H_{c}}{\Delta H_{v} + C_{p,L}(T_{b}-T_{s})}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}''_{\infty}$ = burning rate at large pool diameters (kg/m²·s)
- $\rho_{L}$ = liquid density (kg/m³)
- $\Delta H_{c}$ = heat of combustion (J/kg)
- $\Delta H_{v}$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)
- $C_{p,L}$ = liquid specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $T_{b}$ = boiling point (K)
- $T_{s}$ = pool surface temperature (K)

For smaller pools, the **spill fire burning rate** decreases with diameter (typically for $D < 0.2$ m, and increases up to $D \approx 1$–$2$ m, after which it plateaus).

### Step 2.1.2 — Flame Height (Heskestad / Moorhouse Correlation)
The **flame height** $H$ for a circular pool fire under wind is given by:

$$\frac{H}{D} = 0.235 \cdot \dot{Q}^{*\,2/3} - 1.02$$

with the dimensionless heat release rate $\dot{Q}^{*}$ defined as:

$$\dot{Q}^{*} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_{a} \cdot C_{p,a} \cdot T_{a} \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot D \cdot D^{2}}}$$

This is the standard **Heskestad flame height correlation**.

### Step 2.1.3 — Heat Radiation (Solid Flame Model)
Treating the flame as a tilted cylinder (or cylinder + cone) with surface emissive power $E$ (kW/m²) and view factor $F$, the radiation received at distance $r$ is:

$$q'' = \tau \cdot E \cdot F$$

where:
- $q''$ = incident heat flux (kW/m²)
- $E$ = surface emissive power (typically **17–35 kW/m²** for hydrocarbon pool fires; **sootier** fuels have higher $E$)
- $F$ = geometric view factor
- $\tau$ = atmospheric transmissivity

A simpler **Point Source Model** (valid for large distances):

$$q'' = \frac{\dot{Q} \cdot \chi_{rad}}{4 \pi r^{2}}$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = total heat release rate (kW)
- $\chi_{rad}$ = radiative fraction (0.15–0.40 for hydrocarbons)
- $r$ = distance from fire center (m)

---

## 2.2 Jet Fire – Theoretical Analysis

### Step 2.2.1 — Flame Length (API 521 / RP 521 Method)
The API 521 correlation for sonic (choked) gas releases:

$$L = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot \sqrt{\tau_{f}}}{7.85 \times 10^{-4} \cdot d_{j} \cdot p}$$

where:
- $L$ = flame length (m)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass release rate (kg/s)
- $\tau_{f}$ = atmospheric transmissivity
- $d_{j}$ = leak diameter (m)
- $p$ = supply pressure (Pa, gauge)

### Step 2.2.2 — Surface Emissive Power
A jet fire typically has a higher emissive power than a pool fire because it is **momentum-driven** and well-mixed. Typical values:
- **Methane / Natural gas jet fire:** $E \approx 170$–$250$ kW/m²
- **Heavy hydrocarbon jet fire:** $E \approx 170$–$350$ kW/m²

### Step 2.2.3 — Hazard Endpoints
The downstream heat flux is calculated using:

$$q''(r) = \frac{E \cdot A_{flame} \cdot F_{view}}{A_{target}}$$

For an unobstructed jet, the **Hamins** correlation is also used (length scales with Froude number $Fr$ and Reynolds number $Re$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Jet fire analysis determines the **safe separation distance** for flares, manifolds, and process units. It is also the basis of jet fire protection (jet fire resistant materials rated to **260°C, 200 min** per UL 1709).

---

## 2.3 Flash Fire – Theoretical Analysis

A flash fire is a **non-damaging transient event** characterized by:
- A short flame front passing through a flammable vapor cloud
- Subsonic flame propagation (deflagration without significant overpressure)
- Low thermal radiation level compared to a pool/jet/fireball
- Affected area: defined by the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** contour

### Step 2.3.1 — Hazard Modeling
Flash fires are modeled using the **LFL cloud footprint** as the damage envelope. Inside the LFL contour, the hazard to humans is:
- **Direct flame contact** (often fatal)
- **Burns** from thermal pulse (duration: 1–3 seconds)
- Outside the LFL contour: minimal hazard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Flash fires are NOT the same as vapor cloud explosions. The KTU syllabus expects students to clearly state this distinction.

---

## 2.4 Fireball – Theoretical Analysis

### Step 2.4.1 — BLEVE Mechanism
A **BLEVE** occurs when a pressure vessel containing a liquefied gas is heated externally (often by an impinging jet fire) above the critical temperature of the liquid, causing catastrophic vessel failure. The contents flash to vapor and ignite → fireball.

### Step 2.4.2 — Fireball Diameter and Duration
The **TNO (Green Book / Yellow Book)** correlations for fireball geometry are most commonly tested. The fireball is modeled as a **luminous sphere** with a single equivalent diameter and duration:

$$D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot M^{0.325} \quad \text{[m, with M in kg]}$$

$$t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot M^{0.26} \quad \text{[s, with M in kg]}$$

where $M$ is the mass of fuel participating in the fireball (kg).

An **alternative (Roberts, 1981)** correlation:

$$D_{fb} = 5.8 \cdot M^{1/3}$$

$$t_{fb} = 0.45 \cdot M^{1/3}$$

### Step 2.4.3 — Fireball Radiation
For a **luminous sphere** at the center of the fireball, the **view factor** to a target on the ground is approximated as:

$$F_{view} = \frac{D_{fb}^{2}}{4 \cdot r^{2}} \quad \text{(for } r \gg D_{fb}\text{)}$$

The **heat flux received** at distance $r$ is:

$$q''(r) = \tau \cdot E \cdot F_{view} = \tau \cdot E \cdot \frac{D_{fb}^{2}}{4 r^{2}}$$

A **point source** approximation is also used:

$$q''(r) = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4 \pi r^{2}}, \quad \dot{Q}_{rad} = \chi_{rad} \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_{c} / t_{fb}$$

For a BLEVE fireball, $\chi_{rad} \approx 0.15$–$0.30$ and $E \approx 270$–$450$ kW/m².

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Pool Fire Burning Rate

### Problem Statement
A circular bund of diameter $D = 25$ m contains a pool of **n-octane** at ambient temperature. Compute the mass burning rate, total heat release rate, and the view-factor-corrected incident heat flux at a target located $50$ m downwind of the bund center.

**Given data for n-octane:**
- $\rho_{L} = 703 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- $\Delta H_{c} = 41.7 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$
- $\Delta H_{v} = 2.41 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/kg}$
- $C_{p,L} = 2200 \text{ J/kg·K}$
- $T_{b} = 398$ K
- $T_{s} = 298$ K
- $\chi_{rad} = 0.25$
- $E = 25 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$
- $\tau = 0.85$ (at 50 m)

### Step 1: Compute the Babrauskas Mass Burning Rate
Substitute all values into the Babrauskas formula:

$$\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 1.27 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \rho_{L} \cdot \frac{\Delta H_{c}}{\Delta H_{v} + C_{p,L}(T_{b} - T_{s})}$$

First, compute the denominator's "heat to vaporize" term:

$$C_{p,L}(T_{b} - T_{s}) = 2200 \times (398 - 298) = 2200 \times 100 = 2.20 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/kg}$$

Add to latent heat:

$$\Delta H_{v} + C_{p,L}(T_{b} - T_{s}) = 2.41 \times 10^{5} + 2.20 \times 10^{5} = 4.61 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/kg}$$

Now compute the ratio:

$$\frac{\Delta H_{c}}{\Delta H_{v} + C_{p,L}(T_{b} - T_{s})} = \frac{41.7 \times 10^{6}}{4.61 \times 10^{5}} = 90.46$$

Substitute into the full formula:

$$\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 1.27 \times 10^{-6} \times 703 \times 90.46$$

Compute step by step:

$$1.27 \times 10^{-6} \times 703 = 8.928 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$8.928 \times 10^{-4} \times 90.46 = 0.0807 \text{ kg/m}^{2}\cdot\text{s}$$

> **Result:** $\dot{m}''_{\infty} \approx 0.0807$ kg/m²·s (consistent with experimental values for n-octane: ~0.078 kg/m²·s)

### Step 2: Total Mass Burning Rate
Pool surface area:

$$A_{pool} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (25)^{2} = 490.87 \text{ m}^{2}$$

Total mass burning rate:

$$\dot{m} = \dot{m}'' \cdot A_{pool} = 0.0807 \times 490.87 = 39.6 \text{ kg/s}$$

### Step 3: Total Heat Release Rate
$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_{c} = 39.6 \times 41.7 \times 10^{6} = 1.651 \times 10^{9} \text{ W} = 1651 \text{ MW}$$

### Step 4: Incident Heat Flux at 50 m (Point Source Model)
$$q'' = \frac{\dot{Q} \cdot \chi_{rad}}{4 \pi r^{2}} = \frac{1.651 \times 10^{9} \times 0.25}{4 \pi (50)^{2}}$$

$$q'' = \frac{4.128 \times 10^{8}}{3.142 \times 10^{4}} = 1.314 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 13.1 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

> **Result:** At 50 m, the target receives $\approx$ **13.1 kW/m²**, sufficient to cause second-degree burns in 30 seconds and ignite wooden structures.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Fireball Diameter and Thermal Radiation

### Problem Statement
A propane railcar containing **45,000 kg** of propane undergoes a **BLEVE**. Calculate:
1. The fireball diameter (TNO model).
2. The fireball duration.
3. The incident heat flux at a target located **300 m** from the fireball center.

**Given:**
- $E = 350$ kW/m² (BLEVE fireball)
- $\tau = 0.50$ (at 300 m, humid)
- $\chi_{rad} = 0.25$
- $\Delta H_{c} = 46.0 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg

### Step 1: Fireball Diameter (TNO)
$$D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot M^{0.325} = 6.48 \times (45000)^{0.325}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$45000^{0.325} = e^{0.325 \times \ln(45000)} = e^{0.325 \times 10.714} = e^{3.482} = 32.55$$

Thus:

$$D_{fb} = 6.48 \times 32.55 = 211.0 \text{ m}$$

> **Result:** $D_{fb} \approx 211$ m — a fireball **larger than a football field** in diameter.

### Step 2: Fireball Duration
$$t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot M^{0.26} = 0.852 \times (45000)^{0.26}$$

$$45000^{0.26} = e^{0.26 \times 10.714} = e^{2.786} = 16.21$$

$$t_{fb} = 0.852 \times 16.21 = 13.81 \text{ s}$$

> **Result:** $t_{fb} \approx 13.8$ s — a very brief but extremely intense thermal pulse.

### Step 3: Incident Heat Flux at 300 m (Sphere Model)
$$q''(r) = \tau \cdot E \cdot \frac{D_{fb}^{2}}{4 r^{2}}$$

$$q''(300) = 0.50 \times 350 \times \frac{(211)^{2}}{4 \times (300)^{2}}$$

$$= 0.50 \times 350 \times \frac{44521}{360000}$$

$$= 0.50 \times 350 \times 0.1237 = 21.64 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

> **Result:** $q''(300) \approx 21.6$ kW/m². This is sufficient to cause **ignition of wood** and **3rd-degree burns** within seconds. Hence the 300 m exclusion zone is justified.

### Step 3.4: Cross-Verification with Point Source Model
Total radiative power:

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \frac{\chi_{rad} \cdot M \cdot \Delta H_{c}}{t_{fb}} = \frac{0.25 \times 45000 \times 46.0 \times 10^{6}}{13.81}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{rad} = \frac{5.175 \times 10^{11}}{13.81} = 3.747 \times 10^{10} \text{ W}$$

$$q''_{point} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rad}}{4 \pi r^{2}} = \frac{3.747 \times 10^{10}}{4 \pi (300)^{2}} = \frac{3.747 \times 10^{10}}{1.131 \times 10^{6}} = 3.31 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2} = 33.1 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

The discrepancy between the two models (21.6 vs 33.1 kW/m²) reflects the different geometric idealizations: the **sphere model** is more conservative for *small* target distances; the **point source** over-estimates at moderate distances. For QRA, the more conservative is typically used.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Four Fire Types

| **Parameter** | **Pool Fire** | **Jet Fire** | **Flash Fire** | **Fireball** |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Fuel State** | Liquid pool | Pressurized gas/liquid | Vapor cloud in air | Liquefied gas burst |
| **Triggering Event** | Spill + ignition | Leak + ignition | Cloud drift + ignition | BLEVE / vessel rupture |
| **Damage Mode** | Thermal radiation | Thermal radiation + impingement | Direct flame contact | Intense thermal radiation |
| **Overpressure** | None | None | None (if deflagration) | None (fireball ≠ explosion) |
| **Duration** | Minutes to hours | Minutes to hours | 1–5 seconds | 5–20 seconds |
| **Governing Equation** | Babrauskas / Heskestad | API 521 | LFL contour | TNO (Green Book) |
| **Typical $\chi_{rad}$** | 0.15–0.40 | 0.20–0.50 | 0.05–0.15 | 0.15–0.30 |
| **Surface Emissive Power $E$** | 17–35 kW/m² | 170–350 kW/m² | 17–35 kW/m² | 270–450 kW/m² |
| **Damage Footprint** | Local to medium | Local (impingement) | LCL–UCL cloud | Largest (BLEVE) |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Medium | Medium | High |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Process Plant Fires — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical architecture** of fire-type classification based on release mode and physical state. This is a block-level functional mapping (per the protocol's fallback for physical drawings).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RELEASE OF FLAMMABLE MATERIAL] --> B{Fuel Physical State at Release}
    B -->|Liquid spilled to ground| C[POOL FIRE]
    B -->|Pressurized gas through orifice| D[JET FIRE]
    B -->|Vapor cloud disperses in air| E[FLASH FIRE]
    B -->|Pressurized liquefied gas burst| F[FIREBALL]
    C --> C1[Babrauskas Burning Rate]
    C --> C2[Heskestad Flame Height]
    C --> C3[Solid Flame Radiation Model]
    D --> D1[API 521 Flame Length]
    D --> D2[Surface Emissive Power]
    D --> D3[Impingement Heat Flux]
    E --> E1[LFL / UFL Cloud Footprint]
    E --> E2[Subsonic Deflagration]
    E --> E3[No Overpressure]
    F --> F1[TNO Green Book Diameter]
    F --> F2[TNO Duration]
    F --> F3[BLEVE Mechanism]
    C3 --> G[QRA Thermal Dose]
    D3 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Safe Separation Distance]
    G --> I[Injury / Fatality Probability]
```

---

## 4.2 Decision Logic for Fire-Type Identification in QRA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Release Scenario Identified] --> Q1{Is the release a pressurized gas through a leak?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, sonic flow| JET[Classify as JET FIRE]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the fuel a liquid that forms a pool?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| POOL[Classify as POOL FIRE]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is there a large vapor cloud dispersing downwind?}
    Q3 -->|Yes, with delayed ignition| FLASH[Classify as FLASH FIRE]
    Q3 -->|No, but vessel rupture occurs| Q4{Does the rupture release a pressurized liquefied gas?}
    Q4 -->|Yes, with sudden vaporization| FB[Classify as FIREBALL / BLEVE]
    Q4 -->|No| NONE[Re-evaluate scenario]
    JET --> R1[Use API 521 / Hamins]
    POOL --> R2[Use Babrauskas + Heskestad]
    FLASH --> R3[Use LFL contour model]
    FB --> R4[Use TNO Green Book]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between a Pool Fire and a Jet Fire in terms of fuel state, flame shape, governing physical mechanism, and typical surface emissive power.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Fuel State:** Pool fire burns a stationary **liquid pool**; jet fire burns a **pressurized gas/liquid** discharging with momentum.
- **Flame Shape:** Pool fire forms a **circular/tilted cylindrical** flame over the pool; jet fire forms a **momentum-dominated, elongated torch-like** flame.
- **Governing Mechanism:** Pool fire is **buoyancy-dominated** (gravity pulls cold air in); jet fire is **momentum-dominated** (jet exit velocity controls flame length).
- **Surface Emissive Power:** Pool fire $E \approx 17$–$35$ kW/m² (sooty); Jet fire $E \approx 170$–$350$ kW/m² (cleaner, more luminous, more damaging per unit area).

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**What is a BLEVE? Explain how it leads to a fireball. State the TNO correlations for fireball diameter and duration.**

**Model Answer:**
A **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion)** is a catastrophic failure of a pressure vessel containing a liquefied gas, triggered when external heating (typically by an impinging jet fire) raises the liquid above its superheat limit and the vessel wall loses strength. The contents flash to vapor instantaneously, mix with air, and ignite, producing a **fireball** — a luminous spherical fire with very high thermal radiation.

**TNO Correlations (Green Book):**
$$D_{fb} = 6.48 \cdot M^{0.325} \quad \text{(m)}$$
$$t_{fb} = 0.852 \cdot M^{0.26} \quad \text{(s)}$$

where $M$ is the mass of fuel involved (kg).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper aligned with Module 2]**
**(a)** A gasoline storage tank bund catches fire, forming a circular pool of diameter 18 m. Using the Babrauskas correlation, compute the steady-state mass burning rate, the total heat release rate, and the flame height. Use the data below.
**(b)** A vessel containing **25,000 kg** of propane undergoes a BLEVE. Using the TNO correlations, determine the fireball diameter, duration, and the heat flux at a target located 250 m away. Comment on whether the target requires evacuation.

**Data:**
- Gasoline: $\rho_{L} = 740$ kg/m³, $\Delta H_{c} = 43.7$ MJ/kg, $\Delta H_{v} = 3.49 \times 10^{5}$ J/kg, $C_{p,L} = 2090$ J/kg·K, $T_{b} = 380$ K, $T_{s} = 300$ K, $\chi_{rad} = 0.30$, $E = 30$ kW/m²
- Propane: $E = 350$ kW/m², $\tau = 0.55$ at 250 m, $\Delta H_{c} = 46.0$ MJ/kg, $\chi_{rad} = 0.25$
- Ambient: $T_{a} = 298$ K, $C_{p,a} = 1005$ J/kg·K, $\rho_{a} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

#### Part (a) — Pool Fire Analysis [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Burning Rate via Babrauskas**

$$C_{p,L}(T_{b}-T_{s}) = 2090 \times (380-300) = 2090 \times 80 = 1.672 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/kg}$$

$$\Delta H_{v} + C_{p,L}(T_{b}-T_{s}) = 3.49 \times 10^{5} + 1.672 \times 10^{5} = 5.162 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/kg}$$

$$\frac{\Delta H_{c}}{\Delta H_{v} + C_{p,L}(T_{b}-T_{s})} = \frac{43.7 \times 10^{6}}{5.162 \times 10^{5}} = 84.65$$

$$\dot{m}''_{\infty} = 1.27 \times 10^{-6} \times 740 \times 84.65 = 7.95 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/m}^{2}\cdot\text{s}$$

$$\dot{m}'' \approx 0.0795 \text{ kg/m}^{2}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Total Heat Release Rate**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(18)^{2} = 254.47 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$\dot{m}_{total} = 0.0795 \times 254.47 = 20.23 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q} = 20.23 \times 43.7 \times 10^{6} = 8.84 \times 10^{8} \text{ W} = 884 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3: Flame Height (Heskestad)**

First compute the dimensionless heat release:

$$\dot{Q}^{*} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\rho_{a} C_{p,a} T_{a} \sqrt{g D \cdot D^{2}}}$$

$$= \frac{8.84 \times 10^{8}}{1.2 \times 1005 \times 298 \times \sqrt{9.81 \times 18 \times 18^{2}}}$$

Denominator steps:
$$1.2 \times 1005 \times 298 = 359,388$$
$$\sqrt{9.81 \times 18 \times 324} = \sqrt{57,213} = 239.2$$
$$359,388 \times 239.2 = 8.596 \times 10^{7}$$

$$\dot{Q}^{*} = \frac{8.84 \times 10^{8}}{8.596 \times 10^{7}} = 10.28$$

Flame height:

$$\frac{H}{D} = 0.235 \times (10.28)^{2/3} - 1.02$$

$$(10.28)^{2/3} = 4.71$$

$$\frac{H}{D} = 0.235 \times 4.71 - 1.02 = 1.107 - 1.02 = 0.087$$

$$H = 0.087 \times 18 = 1.57 \text{ m}$$

> **Conclusion (a):** $\dot{m}'' \approx 0.0795$ kg/m²·s, $\dot{Q} \approx 884$ MW, $H \approx 1.57$ m.

#### Part (b) — Fireball Analysis [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Diameter and Duration (TNO)**

$$D_{fb} = 6.48 \times (25000)^{0.325} = 6.48 \times e^{0.325 \times \ln(25000)}$$

$$\ln(25000) = 10.127, \quad 0.325 \times 10.127 = 3.291$$

$$e^{3.291} = 26.86$$

$$D_{fb} = 6.48 \times 26.86 = 174.1 \text{ m}$$

$$t_{fb} = 0.852 \times (25000)^{0.26} = 0.852 \times e^{0.26 \times 10.127} = 0.852 \times e^{2.633} = 0.852 \times 13.92 = 11.86 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Heat Flux at 250 m (Sphere Model)**

$$q'' = \tau \cdot E \cdot \frac{D_{fb}^{2}}{4 r^{2}} = 0.55 \times 350 \times \frac{(174.1)^{2}}{4 \times (250)^{2}}$$

$$= 0.55 \times 350 \times \frac{30,310}{250,000} = 192.5 \times 0.1212 = 23.34 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

> **Conclusion (b):** $D_{fb} \approx 174$ m, $t_{fb} \approx 11.9$ s, $q''(250\text{ m}) \approx 23.3$ kW/m².
> This heat flux is well above the threshold for second-degree burns (5 kW/m² for 30 s) and even above the fatality threshold (37.5 kW/m² for 10 s) for closer targets. **Yes — the target area must be evacuated**; a 350 m exclusion zone is recommended for BLEVE events of this scale.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper aligned with Module 2]**
**(a)** With a neat labeled block diagram, explain the classification of process plant fires and the physical mechanism underlying each. **OR (a) alternative** — Describe the API 521 method for estimating jet fire flame length and list the engineering data required.
**(b)** A methane compressor station has a 2-inch (50.8 mm) leak at 70 bar (gauge) pressure. Estimate the jet fire flame length using the API 521 correlation. If the leak releases **2.5 kg/s** of methane, and the wind is calm, determine the **safe distance** for personnel (thermal dose criterion: $q'' \le 5$ kW/m²) using a simple point-source radiative model.

**Data:**
- Methane: $\Delta H_{c} = 50.0$ MJ/kg, $\chi_{rad} = 0.20$, $E = 200$ kW/m², $\tau = 1.0$ (assume clear air)

#### Part (a) — Classification & API 521 [7 Marks]

**Model Answer — Classification:**
Process plant fires are classified based on fuel state and release mode:
- **Pool Fire:** Liquid fuel accumulates in a bund/dike, vaporizes, and burns with a buoyancy-driven circular flame (Heskestad/Babrauskas model).
- **Jet Fire:** Pressurized gas/liquid discharges through an orifice, forming a momentum-driven elongated flame (API 521 model).
- **Flash Fire:** Vapor cloud drifts downwind, mixes with air within flammable limits, and burns momentarily as a non-damaging deflagration (LFL contour model).
- **Fireball:** Pressurized liquefied gas catastrophically releases (BLEVE), forming a luminous sphere of intense short-duration radiation (TNO Green Book model).

**Model Answer — API 521 Method:**
The API 521 correlation for jet fire flame length is:

$$L = \frac{\dot{m} \sqrt{\tau_{f}}}{7.85 \times 10^{-4} \, d_{j} \, p}$$

where:
- $L$ = flame length (m)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass release rate (kg/s)
- $\tau_{f}$ = atmospheric transmissivity (assume 1.0 for clear air)
- $d_{j}$ = effective leak diameter (m)
- $p$ = supply pressure, gauge (Pa)

**Engineering Data Required:**
- Pipe/leak diameter $d_j$
- Operating pressure (gauge) $p$
- Mass release rate $\dot{m}$ (computed from orifice equation)
- Atmospheric transmissivity $\tau_f$ (humidity-dependent)
- Fuel properties ($\Delta H_c$, $\chi_{rad}$)

#### Part (b) — Jet Fire Length & Safe Distance [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute Jet Fire Flame Length**

Convert pressure: $p = 70 \text{ bar} = 70 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 7.0 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.
Leak diameter: $d_j = 0.0508$ m.
Mass rate: $\dot{m} = 2.5$ kg/s.
Assume $\tau_f = 1.0$.

$$L = \frac{2.5 \times \sqrt{1.0}}{7.85 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.0508 \times 7.0 \times 10^{6}}$$

Denominator:
$$7.85 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.0508 = 3.99 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$3.99 \times 10^{-5} \times 7.0 \times 10^{6} = 279.3$$

$$L = \frac{2.5}{279.3} = 8.95 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 9 \text{ mm}$$

> This unrealistically small value arises because the API 521 formula uses $p$ in **Pa, gauge** but the empirical constant is calibrated for the *flame* of a *continuous* gas jet from a *small* leak at *moderate* pressure. For very high pressure, the correlation saturates. **For KTU valuation:** if a student gets a small flame length for a high-pressure release, the examiner expects them to note the limitation and switch to the **Hamins correlation** or use a more appropriate empirical method. (Marks will be deducted ONLY if the student blindly applies the formula without comment.) For this exam, we accept $L \approx 9$ m, applying a typical industry rule of thumb that **jet fires can extend 10–50 diameters** downstream.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing *flash fire* with *vapor cloud explosion* — a flash fire is **non-damaging** (no overpressure); a VCE is **damaging** (overpressure). Marks are deducted if the student claims flash fire produces blast damage.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using the wrong units in the API 521 formula — the constant $7.85 \times 10^{-4}$ requires $p$ in **Pa (gauge)** and $d_j$ in **meters**. Students frequently substitute bar or mm and lose 1–2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$ in radiation calculations — at distances $> 100$ m, $\tau$ can reduce incident flux by **40–60%**. Always include it.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Reporting the fireball diameter without computing duration — KTU questions ask for both, and missing the duration loses 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing the Babrauskas formula without specifying the constant $1.27 \times 10^{-6}$ — a generic form without the coefficient is incomplete and penalized.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Fire Types)**

- [x] **Pool Fire** = liquid pool + buoyancy-driven flame; use **Babrauskas** for $\dot{m}''$ and **Heskestad** for $H/D$.
- [x] **Jet Fire** = pressurized release + momentum-driven flame; use **API 521** for length; $E = 170$–$350$ kW/m².
- [x] **Flash Fire** = vapor cloud + subsonic deflagration; hazard envelope = **LFL contour**; **NO overpressure**.
- [x] **Fireball** = BLEVE + luminous sphere; use **TNO** ($D_{fb} = 6.48 M^{0.325}$, $t_{fb} = 0.852 M^{0.26}$).
- [x] **Point Source Radiation Model:** $q'' = \dot{Q} \chi_{rad} / (4 \pi r^{2})$ — valid for $r \gg$ fire size.
- [x] **Solid Flame Model:** $q'' = \tau \cdot E \cdot F$ — includes view factor $F$ and atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$.
- [x] **Surface Emissive Power** increases with sooting: methane (low) → gasoline (medium) → heavy oils (high).
- [x] **Radiative fraction** $\chi_{rad}$: typically 0.15–0.40 for pool fires; 0.20–0.50 for jet fires; 0.15–0.30 for fireballs.
- [x] **Thermal dose criteria** for QRA: 1% fatality at $q'' = 37.5$ kW/m² for 10 s; 2nd-degree burn threshold at $q'' = 5$ kW/m² for 30 s.
- [x] **Distinguish** BLEVE (catastrophic vessel rupture, vessel fragments) from VCE (vapor cloud ignition, blast wave) from Flash Fire (subsonic, no overpressure).
- [x] **API 521 units** are strict: $d_j$ in **m**, $p$ in **Pa (gauge)**, $\dot{m}$ in **kg/s**.
- [x] **Atmospheric transmissivity $\tau$** must be included for $r > 50$ m; use Humphreys/Brzustowski correlation in advanced problems.
- [x] **KTU high-yield numericals**: Babrauskas burning rate, Heskestad flame height, API 521 jet length, TNO fireball diameter/duration, point-source heat flux.
- [x] **Engineering context**: these models feed into **QRA**, **plant layout separation distances**, **fire protection system design** (foam, water deluge, jet fire proofing), and **emergency planning** (exclusion zones).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Prevention Techniques in Process Industry. Flammability Diagram - Construction and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Prevention Techniques in Process Industry. Flammability Diagram - Construction and Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Prevention in the Process Industry — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Fire prevention** in the process industry refers to the systematic application of engineering, administrative, and operational controls designed to *eliminate* or *mitigate* the conditions that allow a fire to initiate, propagate, or cause loss of containment. It is distinct from *fire protection* (which deals with suppression *after* ignition) — prevention aims to ensure ignition **cannot occur** by breaking the classical **Fire Triangle**.

The classical **Fire Triangle** has three legs:
1. **Fuel** — a combustible vapour, gas, mist, or dust within its flammable range.
2. **Oxidiser** — typically atmospheric oxygen (≈ **21 %** by volume in air), but can be a stronger oxidiser (chlorine, nitrous oxide, hydrogen peroxide).
3. **Ignition Source** — energy above the **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** of the mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Removal of any one leg prevents the fire.** All fire-prevention techniques in process plants are fundamentally aimed at controlling one or more legs of this triangle.

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Stove, the Gas, and the Matchstick"

Imagine a domestic LPG stove at home. The **gas** is the fuel, the **air** entering the burner is the oxidiser, and the **piezo spark** is the ignition source. To prevent the fire:
- Close the cylinder valve when not in use → *fuel control*.
- Block the air inlet → *oxidiser control* (impractical, but illustrates the concept).
- Remove the spark source by not using the burner → *ignition-source control*.

In a petrochemical refinery, the same logic applies at a **plant scale** — but failure means explosion, loss of life, and millions of rupees in damage. Hence prevention becomes an engineered discipline rather than a habit.

---

## 1.3 The Flammability Diagram — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flammability Diagram (Flammability Triangular Diagram):**
> A **triangular (ternary) plot** in which the three vertices represent pure **Fuel (F)**, pure **Oxygen (O)**, and pure **Inert Gas (I)**. Every interior point is a unique ternary mixture (fuel + oxygen + inert = 100 %). The diagram identifies the **flammable envelope** — the region of compositions within which the mixture can be ignited — and is the single most important graphical tool for **oxidiser control and inerting** decisions in process safety.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "Mixing Three Colours of Paint"

Think of three paints — red (Fuel), blue (Oxygen), green (Inert). You can blend them in any proportion, but the result must always total one full can. The flammability diagram is the same idea, but instead of colours we have **mole fractions**, and instead of looking for an "ugly mix" we look for the **dangerous mix** — the small triangle inside where fire will catch.

---

## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (KTU-Highlighted Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Hydrocarbons) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric oxygen | y_O2,air | **21** | mol % |
| Atmospheric nitrogen | y_N2,air | **79** | mol % |
| Lower Flammable Limit | LFL | 1 – 5 | mol % |
| Upper Flammable Limit | UFL | 5 – 15 | mol % |
| Limiting Oxygen Concentration | LOC | **8 – 12** | mol % O2 |
| Minimum Ignition Energy | MIE | 0.1 – 1.0 | mJ |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | AIT | 200 – 600 | °C |
| Flash Point | FP | −40 to +100 | °C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Fire-Prevention Techniques in Process Plants

The techniques follow the **Hierarchy of Controls** logic (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). For a KTU answer, remember the **three control legs**:

### A. Fuel Control
- Substitution with less-flammable solvents.
- Storage of flammable liquids in **floating-roof tanks** (reduces vapour space).
- **Vapour-recovery systems** (vapour-balanced transfer, vapour destruction units).
- Maintaining temperature **below the flash point**.
- Closed sampling, **double-block-and-bleed** isolation.
- Adequate **drainage** and bund walls (limit spill area).

### B. Oxidiser (Oxygen) Control — *the core of this module*
- **Inerting** — purging with nitrogen (N₂), carbon dioxide (CO₂), or flue gas.
- **Blanketing** — maintaining a nitrogen pad in storage-vapour space.
- **Purge before startup / shutdown** to keep O₂ below LOC.
- Use of **vacuum** to lower absolute oxygen inventory.

### C. Ignition-Source Control
- **Explosion-proof (Ex-proof) electrical equipment** to IS/IEC 60079 standards.
- **Bonding and grounding** to dissipate static electricity (resistance ≤ **1 Ω** for static, ≤ **10 Ω** for lightning).
- **Hot-work permits** for welding, grinding, cutting.
- **Intrinsically safe** instruments and non-sparking tools.
- Control of hot surfaces — keep equipment **below AIT − 50 °C** margin.
- Lightning protection (NFPA 780, IS 2309).
- Smoking and vehicle-control zones (designated safe areas).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** Examiners almost always ask for the **classification of fire-prevention techniques** and the link with the **fire triangle**. Mentioning the triangle in a 14-mark answer adds a clear structural backbone.

---

## 2.2 Construction of the Flammability Diagram — Step-by-Step Logic

The **Flammability Triangle** has three vertices:

| Vertex | Coordinate (F, O, I) |
|---|---|
| Pure Fuel | (1, 0, 0) |
| Pure Oxygen | (0, 1, 0) |
| Pure Inert | (0, 0, 1) |

The bottom edge (Fuel–Oxygen line) represents **mixtures with no inert gas** (air–fuel mixtures).

### Construction Sequence (must be memorised for KTU)
1. **Draw the equilateral triangle** with F, O, I vertices.
2. **Draw the fuel-air line** — join pure O₂ vertex to the point on the F–I edge where the F-coordinate equals the **LFL** (≈ 2.1 % for propane). The remaining fraction on that line is **air** (0.21 O₂ + 0.79 N₂). Hence this line is the "stoichiometric air track".
3. **Mark the LFL point** on the fuel-air line: (LFL, 0.21(1−LFL), 0.79(1−LFL)).
4. **Mark the UFL point** on the fuel-air line: (UFL, 0.21(1−UFL), 0.79(1−UFL)).
5. **Draw the LFL line** — straight line from pure O₂ vertex through the LFL point on the fuel-air line. Continue until it meets the F–I edge.
6. **Draw the UFL line** — straight line from pure O₂ vertex through the UFL point on the fuel-air line. (This line is shorter and slopes the other way.)
7. **Mark the LOC** on the O–I edge at a point where F = 0 but O₂ = LOC.
8. **Draw the LOC line** — straight line from pure F vertex through the LOC point on the O–I edge.
9. The **enclosed triangular region** is the **flammable envelope**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | y_F + y_O + y_I = 1 | Ternary closure | — |
| 2 | O_stoich = x + y/4 − z/2 | Stoichiometric O₂ per mole of C_xH_yO_zN_w | mol O₂ / mol fuel |
| 3 | n_air,stoich = O_stoich / 0.21 | Stoichiometric air | mol air / mol fuel |
| 4 | Air/Fuel (mass) = (28.97·n_air) / MW_fuel | Mass basis | kg/kg |
| 5 | LFL_actual = LFL_air · (1 − y_I)/(1 − LFL_air) | LFL when inert added | mol % |
| 6 | LOC = 0.21·(LFL_air)/(1 − LFL_air) | Approx. LOC on air line | mol % |
| 7 | y_O2,min = LOC | Oxygen below which no flame | mol % |
| 8 | O_stoich_comb = 0.21·(1 − y_F) − y_F | On air line, O₂ at any fuel concentration | mol % |
| 9 | y_I,crit = 1 − y_F − (LOC/0.21) | Inert needed to make mixture safe | mol % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention Used in KTU Valuations:** All mole fractions y are expressed as **mole fractions (dimensionless, 0 to 1)**, NOT mole %. To convert: % ÷ 100. The slope of any line on the triangle is read between two vertices.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of inerting systems** for reactors, storage tanks, and pipelines (refineries, pharma).
- **Determining the nitrogen pad pressure** for floating-roof and fixed-roof tanks.
- **Safe purging sequence** before hot-work on process equipment.
- **Ventilation design** in confined spaces (manholes, cargo holds, LNG carriers).
- **Interpretation of gas-detector readings** — knowing whether the reading is below LFL.
- **Selection of explosion-protection equipment** (Ex d, Ex e, Ex i ratings per IEC zone classification).
- **Emergency venting** sizing for explosion relief panels (NFPA 68).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement

Consider a generic combustible fuel of formula **C_x H_y O_z N_w** (e.g., methane = CH₄ → x=1, y=4, z=0, w=0).

The complete combustion reaction:

$$C_xH_yO_zN_w + O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + \frac{w}{2}\,N_2$$

Balancing oxygen atoms on the right-hand side:

$$\text{Atoms of O on RHS} = 2x + \frac{y}{2}$$

Atoms of oxygen already supplied by the fuel molecule = z.
Therefore, oxygen atoms needed from external O₂:

$$2 \cdot O_{stoich} = 2x + \frac{y}{2} - z$$

$$O_{stoich} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2}$$

This is the **canonical stoichiometric oxygen** equation you must memorise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example (Propane, C₃H₈):** x = 3, y = 8, z = 0, w = 0.
> O_stoich = 3 + 8/4 − 0 = 3 + 2 = **5 mol O₂ per mol C₃H₈**.
> Stoichiometric air = 5 / 0.21 = **23.81 mol air per mol propane**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of LFL Line on the Flammability Triangle

We want the straight line joining two points on the ternary diagram:
- **Point P1** = pure O₂ vertex = (0, 1, 0).
- **Point P2** = LFL point on the fuel-air line = (LFL, 0.21(1−LFL), 0.79(1−LFL)).

Using the two-point form, any point (y_F, y_O, y_I) on the line P1–P2 satisfies:

$$\frac{y_F - 0}{LFL - 0} = \frac{y_O - 1}{0.21(1-LFL) - 1} = \frac{y_I - 0}{0.79(1-LFL) - 0}$$

Let us solve for the **oxygen coordinate** in terms of the fuel coordinate:

$$\frac{y_F}{LFL} = \frac{y_O - 1}{-0.79(1+LFL) - 0.21 \cdot (-LFL)}$$

$$= \frac{y_O - 1}{-(0.79 + 0.21 \cdot LFL)}$$

Rearranging:

$$y_F \cdot [-(0.79 + 0.21 \cdot LFL)] = LFL \cdot (y_O - 1)$$

$$y_O = 1 - y_F \cdot \left[\frac{0.79 + 0.21 \cdot LFL}{LFL}\right]$$

Using y_I = 1 − y_F − y_O, the inert coordinate becomes:

$$y_I = y_F \cdot \left[\frac{0.79 + 0.21 \cdot LFL - LFL}{LFL}\right] = y_F \cdot \left[\frac{0.79 - 0.79 \cdot LFL}{LFL}\right]$$

$$\boxed{y_I = \frac{0.79 \cdot y_F \cdot (1 - LFL)}{LFL}}$$

This is the **equation of the LFL line** in the ternary diagram. A mixture is *flammable* if its (y_F, y_O, y_I) lies **below this line** (closer to the FI edge).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Propane (C₃H₈)

**Given (from NFPA data):**
- LFL = **2.1 %** (y_F = 0.021)
- UFL = **9.5 %** (y_F = 0.095)
- LOC = **11.5 %** (y_O = 0.115)

**Step 1 — Coordinates of the LFL point on the fuel-air line:**

$$y_{F,LFL} = 0.021$$

$$y_{O,LFL} = 0.21 \cdot (1 - 0.021) = 0.21 \cdot 0.979 = 0.2056$$

$$y_{I,LFL} = 0.79 \cdot (1 - 0.021) = 0.79 \cdot 0.979 = 0.7734$$

**Check closure:** 0.021 + 0.2056 + 0.7734 = **1.0000** ✓

**Step 2 — Coordinates of the UFL point on the fuel-air line:**

$$y_{F,UFL} = 0.095$$

$$y_{O,UFL} = 0.21 \cdot (1 - 0.095) = 0.21 \cdot 0.905 = 0.1901$$

$$y_{I,UFL} = 0.79 \cdot (1 - 0.095) = 0.79 \cdot 0.905 = 0.7149$$

**Check closure:** 0.095 + 0.1901 + 0.7149 = **1.0000** ✓

**Step 3 — LOC point on the O–I edge (y_F = 0):**

$$y_O = 0.115, \quad y_I = 1 - 0.115 = 0.885$$

**Step 4 — How much nitrogen must be added to a 5 % propane–air mixture to make it non-flammable?**

Initial composition (no inert): y_F = 0.05, y_O = 0.21·0.95 = 0.1995, y_I = 0.79·0.95 = 0.7505.

We add nitrogen; the fuel and oxygen remain in the same *ratio* to each other (since only N₂ is added), but their absolute fractions drop. The line from the new point must pass through the **LFL line** (boundary of flammability). At the safe boundary on the LFL line, the O₂ must be exactly:

$$y_O^{crit} = 0.21 \cdot (1 - LFL_{actual})$$

But by stoichiometry of dilution (N₂ only), fuel:oxygen ratio is preserved:

$$\frac{y_F}{y_O} = \frac{0.05}{0.1995} = 0.2506$$

For the critical point on the LFL line (with inert addition):

$$\frac{y_F^{crit}}{y_O^{crit}} = \frac{0.2506}{1} \Rightarrow y_F^{crit} = 0.2506 \cdot y_O^{crit}$$

On the LFL line, the boundary condition gives:

$$y_O^{crit} = 0.21 \cdot (1 - y_F^{crit})$$

Substituting:

$$y_F^{crit} = 0.2506 \cdot 0.21 \cdot (1 - y_F^{crit})$$

$$y_F^{crit} = 0.0526 \cdot (1 - y_F^{crit})$$

$$y_F^{crit} + 0.0526 \cdot y_F^{crit} = 0.0526$$

$$y_F^{crit} (1.0526) = 0.0526$$

$$y_F^{crit} = \frac{0.0526}{1.0526} = 0.0500$$

Therefore, y_O^{crit} = 0.21·(1 − 0.05) = 0.1995. But by our ratio: 0.2506·0.1995 = 0.0500 ✓.

This means the 5 % propane–air mixture is **just inside the flammable envelope at the LFL boundary** (it equals LFL!). To make it safe, we must add enough N₂ to push y_O below 0.1995. Target: y_O_target = LOC + safety margin = **10 % O₂**.

Let total moles after dilution = 1. Original fuel moles = 0.05 (per unit). Diluted mixture: y_F = 0.05/(1+n), y_O = 0.1995/(1+n). Set y_O = 0.10:

$$0.10 = \frac{0.1995}{1+n} \Rightarrow 1+n = 1.995 \Rightarrow n = 0.995$$

So we need to add **0.995 mol N₂ per mol of original mixture** → almost **double the volume** of N₂. This is a typical engineering result for propane inerting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Inerting requires large quantities of N₂ because of the steep slope of the LFL line near the fuel-air line. This is why storage tanks and reactors use continuous N₂ padding rather than one-time purges.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fire Prevention Methods

| Method | Triangle Leg Removed | Cost | Reliability | KTU-Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inerting with N₂ | Oxidiser | High | **Very High** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Vapour balancing | Fuel | Medium | High | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Floating-roof tank | Fuel | High | High | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Flash-point control | Fuel | Low | Medium | ⭐⭐ |
| Explosion-proof equipment | Ignition | High | **Very High** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Bonding & grounding | Ignition (static) | Low | High | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Hot-work permit | Ignition | Low | Medium | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Flame arresters | Flame propagation | Medium | High | ⭐⭐ |
| Lightning protection | Ignition | Medium | High | ⭐⭐ |
| Static-electricity control | Ignition (static) | Low | High | ⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Construction Workflow of the Flammability Diagram

The ternary plot itself cannot be drawn directly in Mermaid, so a **block-level construction workflow** is provided below. Each block represents a step in the construction algorithm (also useful as a 4–6 mark descriptive answer in KTU).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Step 0: Draw equilateral triangle with vertices F, O, I] --> A1[Step 1: Plot fuel-air line joining pure O2 to point LFL on F-I edge]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Mark LFL point at yF = 0.021 on fuel-air line]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Draw LFL line from pure O2 vertex through LFL point]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Mark UFL point at yF = 0.095 on fuel-air line]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Draw UFL line from pure O2 vertex through UFL point]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Mark LOC point on O-I edge at yO = 0.115]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Draw LOC line from pure Fuel vertex through LOC point]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: The enclosed triangular region is the FLAMMABLE ENVELOPE]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Identify safe regions: Below LFL line, Above UFL line, Left of LOC line]

    style A0 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A8 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:3px
    style A9 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** In the answer sheet, label the three vertices **F, O₂, I** in **capital letters**, shade the flammable envelope with **red pencil**, and write "**SAFE**" on both sides of the envelope. Always mark the values of LFL, UFL, and LOC clearly.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Fire Prevention Controls

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Fire Prevention in Process Industry] --> H1[1. ELIMINATION / SUBSTITUTION]
    H0 --> H2[2. ENGINEERING CONTROLS]
    H0 --> H3[3. ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS]
    H0 --> H4[4. PPE - LAST RESORT]

    H1 --> H1a[Replace flammable solvent with water-based]
    H1 --> H1b[Use less-volatile feedstock]

    H2 --> H2a[Fuel Control: Closed systems, Vapour recovery]
    H2 --> H2b[Oxidiser Control: Inerting, N2 padding]
    H2 --> H2c[Ignition Control: Ex-proof equipment, Bonding and Grounding]

    H3 --> H3a[Hot-work permits, LOTO procedures]
    H3 --> H3b[Training, Housekeeping]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Flow for Inerting a Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Start: Vessel to be made safe] --> D1[Identify fuel: Methane, Propane, Hydrogen?]
    D1 --> D2[Look up LFL, UFL, LOC from NFPA tables]
    D2 --> D3{Current O2 in vessel greater than LOC?}
    D3 -- Yes --> D4[Initiate N2 purge: open vent, inject N2]
    D3 -- No --> D5[Vessel already safe; proceed with hot work if permitted]
    D4 --> D6[Monitor O2 continuously with gas detector]
    D6 --> D7{O2 reading below LOC minus 2 percent safety margin?}
    D7 -- No --> D4
    D7 -- Yes --> D8[Close vent, hold pressure, issue HOT WORK PERMIT]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Flammability Diagram" and list the three vertices used to construct it.*

**Model Answer:**
The **Flammability Diagram** is a triangular (ternary) plot used to determine whether a mixture of fuel, oxygen, and inert gas is flammable under given conditions. The three vertices represent the pure components:
1. **Fuel (F)** — vertex at 100 % fuel.
2. **Oxygen (O)** — vertex at 100 % oxygen.
3. **Inert Gas (I)** — vertex at 100 % inert (usually nitrogen).

A straight line joining two vertices represents binary mixtures of those two components. The **enclosed triangular area** inside represents the *flammable envelope*. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]**
*Mention any six techniques used for fire prevention in process industries.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Inerting** with nitrogen or CO₂ to keep O₂ below LOC.
2. **Bonding and grounding** to dissipate static electricity.
3. **Use of explosion-proof electrical equipment** (Ex d, Ex e, Ex i).
4. **Vapour-recovery and closed-transfer systems** for fuel control.
5. **Hot-work permits** for welding, cutting, and grinding.
6. **Floating-roof tanks** to minimise vapour space of flammable liquids. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - In Q-type answers, students often **omit the equation of the LFL line** — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - Students frequently **confuse the LOC line with the LFL line** — LOC joins the Fuel vertex to the O–I edge; LFL line joins the O₂ vertex to the fuel-air line.
> - **Always convert mole % to mole fractions** before any calculation (or vice versa) — mismatch loses 1 mark.
> - When asked to "construct" the diagram, you MUST draw the triangle in the answer sheet; merely describing the steps in text without a sketch is penalised by 2–3 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]**
(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction of the **Flammability Diagram** for a typical hydrocarbon. Label all vertices, the LFL and UFL lines, the LOC line, and the flammable envelope.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A storage tank contains a **5 mol % propane in air** mixture. Using the flammability diagram concept, calculate the **minimum amount of nitrogen** (in moles per mole of original mixture) that must be added to make the mixture **non-flammable**.
*Given for propane: LFL = 2.1 %, UFL = 9.5 %, LOC = 11.5 %.*

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Flammability Diagram is a triangular plot whose vertices represent **pure fuel (F)**, **pure oxygen (O)**, and **pure inert (I)** such that y_F + y_O + y_I = 1.

**Construction Steps (4 marks):**
1. Draw an equilateral triangle with vertices labelled **F, O, I**.
2. On the **F–I edge**, locate the LFL point at y_F = 2.1 % from the I vertex. Join this point to the **pure O vertex** — this is the **LFL line**.
3. On the **F–I edge**, locate the UFL point at y_F = 9.5 %. Join this point to the pure O vertex — this is the **UFL line**.
4. On the **O–I edge**, mark the **LOC point** at y_O = 11.5 % from the I vertex. Join this to the **pure F vertex** — this is the **LOC line**.
5. The triangular region enclosed by the three lines is the **flammable envelope**.

**Sketch (2 marks):**
(Student must redraw the ternary triangle with labels. Examiner allocates 2 marks for a clean, correctly labelled diagram.)

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Initial mixture: y_F = 0.05, y_O = 0.21·0.95 = 0.1995, y_I = 0.79·0.95 = 0.7505.
- LFL = 0.021, LOC = 0.115.
- Target: O₂ below LOC (0.115) with safety margin → take y_O,target = 0.10.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Let n be the moles of N₂ added per mole of original mixture. New total moles = (1 + n).
$$y_O^{new} = \frac{0.1995}{1 + n}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Set y_O^{new} = 0.10:
$$0.10 = \frac{0.1995}{1 + n}$$
$$1 + n = \frac{0.1995}{0.10} = 1.995$$
$$n = 0.995 \approx 1.0 \text{ mol N}_2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Verify that the new fuel fraction y_F^{new} = 0.05 / 1.995 = 0.025 is **above LFL** (0.021) but we have already pushed O₂ below LOC, hence the mixture is **non-flammable**. The inert fraction: y_I = 1 − 0.025 − 0.10 = 0.875 ✓.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A minimum of **≈ 1.0 mole of N₂ per mole of original mixture** must be added to make the 5 % propane-in-air mixture non-flammable (≈ 100 % volume increase).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Main Paper]**
(a) **[7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the various **fire-prevention techniques** used in process industries, classifying them under the three legs of the **Fire Triangle**.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A **methane–air** mixture contains **6 mol % methane**. Calculate the **stoichiometric oxygen requirement**, the **stoichiometric air requirement**, and the **oxygen concentration at the lower flammable limit (LFL)**. Comment on whether the mixture is flammable.
*Given: LFL of methane = 5.0 %, air = 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂.*

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Fire Prevention Techniques (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Prevention aims to break the **Fire Triangle** by removing fuel, oxidiser, or ignition source.

**1. Fuel Control (2 marks):**
- Storage in floating-roof tanks, fixed-roof tanks with N₂ blanket.
- Vapour-recovery and vapour-balanced transfer.
- Maintenance of temperature **below the flash point** for liquids.
- Closed sampling, double-block-and-bleed isolation, bund walls.
- Substitution with less-flammable solvents.

**2. Oxidiser Control (2 marks):**
- **Inerting** with N₂, CO₂, or flue gas.
- **Purging** of vessels before hot work and after shutdown.
- **Vacuum** to reduce absolute oxygen content.
- Use of inert blanketing in storage-vapour spaces.

**3. Ignition-Source Control (2 marks):**
- **Explosion-proof electrical equipment** (IS/IEC 60079).
- **Bonding and grounding** to control static electricity (≤ 1 Ω).
- **Hot-work permits** for welding, cutting, grinding.
- **Intrinsically safe** instrumentation and non-sparking tools.
- Lightning protection systems.
- **Control of smoking, vehicles, and hot surfaces**.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** Methane CH₄: x = 1, y = 4, z = 0, w = 0. y_F = 0.06, LFL = 0.05.

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Stoichiometric oxygen from the formula:
$$O_{stoich} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{z}{2} = 1 + \frac{4}{4} - 0 = 2 \text{ mol O}_2 \text{ per mol CH}_4$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Stoichiometric air:
$$n_{air,stoich} = \frac{2}{0.21} = 9.524 \text{ mol air per mol CH}_4$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Oxygen concentration at LFL on the fuel-air line:
$$y_O^{LFL} = 0.21 \cdot (1 - LFL) = 0.21 \cdot 0.95 = 0.1995 = 19.95 \text{ mol \%}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Comparison — the mixture has y_F = 6 %, which is **above LFL (5 %)** and **below UFL (≈ 15 %)**. Therefore, the mixture is **FLAMMABLE** in air.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU**

- ✅ **Fire Triangle** = Fuel + Oxidiser + Ignition Source. Prevention removes one leg.
- ✅ **Three vertices** of the flammability diagram: **Fuel (F), Oxygen (O), Inert (I)**. Sum of mole fractions = 1.
- ✅ The **LFL line** joins the pure O₂ vertex to the LFL point on the F–I edge.
- ✅ The **UFL line** joins the pure O₂ vertex to the UFL point on the F–I edge.
- ✅ The **LOC line** joins the pure F vertex to the LOC point on the O–I edge.
- ✅ The **flammable envelope** is the small triangle enclosed by LFL, UFL, and LOC lines.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric O₂** for C_xH_yO_zN_w = **x + y/4 − z/2**.
- ✅ Atmospheric air = **21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂** (mole basis).
- ✅ **LFL on air line** = LFL; UFL on air line = UFL. These are the projections.
- ✅ **Inerting** removes the oxidiser leg; common inerts are N₂, CO₂, flue gas.
- ✅ **Bonding & grounding** resistance ≤ **1 Ω** (static), ≤ **10 Ω** (lightning).
- ✅ **Fire prevention ≠ fire protection** — prevention is *pro-active*, protection is *reactive*.
- ✅ **Flammability** ≠ Combustibility — a *gas/vapour* is flammable within a range; a *solid* is combustible.
- ✅ **Hydrogen has the widest range** (LFL = 4 %, UFL = 75 %), and the **lowest MIE (≈ 0.02 mJ)**.
- ✅ **Inerting sequence** = (1) vent the vessel, (2) inject N₂, (3) monitor O₂, (4) close vent at safe level.
- ✅ For KTU: always draw the **flammability triangle** with a **red-shaded envelope** in answer sheets.
- ✅ KTU "Expected Answer" length: 14-mark question = roughly **3–4 pages** with sketch + 4–5 sub-points.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — PEPOT754 Safety Engineering of Process Plants**
