topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Explosion: Deflagration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DEFLAGRATION (KTU 2024 Working Definition):**
> Deflagration is a mode of combustion in which the **flame front propagates through the unreacted medium at a velocity *less than* the speed of sound** of that medium, with the propagation mechanism being driven primarily by **thermal conduction, convection, and radiation** from the hot burned gases to the adjacent cool unreacted mixture.

In the context of **Process Plant Safety Engineering**, a deflagration is the most frequently encountered explosion mode in chemical reactors, storage tanks, pipelines, dust collectors, and vapor cloud formations. It is classified as a **subsonic combustion event**.

The fundamental velocity regime for deflagration in process safety is:

$$\text{Speed of flame propagation} \; (S_p) \;\ll\; \text{Speed of sound in the unreacted medium} \; (a)$$

$$\text{Typical range: } S_p \in \mathbf{[0.5 \text{ m/s}, \; 300 \text{ m/s}]}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Matchstick in a Gas Leak" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are standing at one end of a long, open-ended corridor filled with a faint smell of LPG (a flammable vapor-air mixture). You light a match at your end. The flame front will walk, almost lazily, down the corridor toward the other end. You can **see** the flame approaching, and you could (theoretically) outrun it if you ran at a sprint. This is **DEFLAGRATION** — the heat from the flame just keeps igniting the next layer of gas, which ignites the next, and so on.

> Now compare this to setting off a stick of dynamite in the same corridor. The blast hits every wall **instantaneously**, and a violent shock front crashes out. You cannot outrun it because it travels *faster than sound*. **THAT is DETONATION.**

| Aspect | Deflagration (Matchstick) | Detonation (Dynamite) |
|---|---|---|
| Propagation Driver | Heat transfer (conduction/radiation) | Shock wave / pressure wave |
| Speed | Subsonic ($< a$) | Supersonic ($> a$) |
| Visibility of Flame | Visible, observable | Nearly invisible, instantaneous |
| Run-away Feasibility | Can be outrun by a person | Cannot be outrun |

---

## 1.3 Key Engineering Distinction — Burning Velocity vs. Flame Speed

A critical KTU-favorite conceptual trap is the difference between three similar-sounding velocities:

$$S_p = S_u \times \varepsilon$$

Where:
- **$S_u$ = Laminar Burning Velocity** (fundamental property of the fuel-oxidizer mixture, typically **0.3 to 3 m/s** for hydrocarbon-air mixtures)
- **$\varepsilon$ = Expansion Ratio** (ratio of burned-gas density to unburned-gas density, typically **6 to 8** for hydrocarbon-air systems)
- **$S_p$ = Flame Speed** (the observable propagation velocity relative to a stationary observer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A deflagration in a **confined vessel** (e.g., a process reactor) accelerates dramatically because the flame expands and pressurizes the unburned gases ahead of it. The Mach number inside the vessel can approach 1 (i.e., **the deflagration can choke and transition into a detonation** — known as **Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT)**). This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in PEPOT754.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Deflagration Events in Process Plants

| Category | Typical $S_p$ | Typical Peak Overpressure | Process Plant Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slow Deflagration | 1–5 m/s | < 0.5 bar | Vented tank fire |
| Fast Deflagration | 5–50 m/s | 0.5–2 bar | Pipeline rupture with ignition |
| Strong Deflagration | 50–300 m/s | 2–8 bar | Confined vapor cloud |
| Incipient Detonation | > 300 m/s | > 8 bar | Pre-DDT transition |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of Deflagration

A deflagration event in a process plant proceeds through **four sequential stages**:

1. **Ignition Stage**: A localized energy source (spark, hot surface, static discharge) initiates a flame kernel.
2. **Growth Stage**: The flame kernel expands spherically as a deflagration wave. Heat transfer to the unburned gases sustains propagation.
3. **Confined Acceleration Stage**: When the flame encounters confinement (walls, vessel boundaries, ducts), pressure builds. The unburned gases ahead of the flame are compressed, which:
   - Increases their temperature (pre-ignition heating)
   - Increases the local burning velocity ($S_u \propto T^{1.7}$ to $T^{2.0}$)
   - Creates a **positive feedback loop** of accelerating flame
4. **Damage / Venting Stage**: Either the vessel vents (designed relief), fails catastrophically (catastrophic vessel failure), or transitions to detonation.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Acceleration:** The unburned gas in a closed vessel is compressed isentropically by the expanding burned gases. From ideal gas law, $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$, so compression raises both $P$ and $T$, which dramatically increases the local burning velocity, which accelerates the flame, which compresses more gas — this is the engine of confined deflagration violence.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the analytical spine of Module 2.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $S_p = S_u \cdot \varepsilon$ | Flame speed = burning velocity × expansion ratio | m/s |
| F2 | $S_u \propto T_u^{1.7-2.0}$ | Burning velocity temperature dependence | — |
| F3 | $\varepsilon = \frac{T_b}{T_u} \cdot \frac{P_u}{P_b} \approx \frac{T_{ad}}{T_u}$ | Expansion ratio (ideal gas, near-atmospheric) | dimensionless |
| F4 | $W_{TNT} = \frac{\alpha \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot H_c}{H_{c,TNT}}$ | TNT Equivalence for vapor cloud explosions | kg TNT |
| F5 | $H_{c,TNT} = 4680 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ | Heat of combustion of TNT (constant) | kJ/kg |
| F6 | $\alpha \in \mathbf{[0.01, \; 0.10]}$ | Explosion efficiency factor (1%–10%) | dimensionless |
| F7 | $Z = \frac{R}{\sqrt[3]{W_{TNT}}}$ | Scaled distance (Cube Law — Sachs' Scaling) | m/kg$^{1/3}$ |
| F8 | $\Delta P_b = f(Z)$ | Overpressure correlation (Baker–Westine) | bar |
| F9 | $\Delta P_{max} \approx \frac{P_0(\gamma^2 - 1)}{2\gamma}$ | Max isochoric closed-vessel pressure rise | bar |
| F10 | $P_{red,max} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_v \cdot A_v}{V^{2/3}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_{max}}{P_0}}\right)$ | NFPA 68 vented deflagration pressure (simplified) | bar gauge |
| F11 | $Ma = \frac{S_p}{a}$ | Mach number of flame front | dimensionless |
| F12 | $a = \sqrt{\gamma \cdot R_{sp} \cdot T}$ | Speed of sound in unreacted medium | m/s |
| F13 | $E_c = m_{fuel} \cdot H_c$ | Total chemical energy release | J or kJ |
| F14 | $R_0 = \left(\frac{E_c}{P_0}\right)^{1/3}$ | Characteristic blast radius | m |
| F15 | $Y_{fuel} = \frac{\Delta H_c}{[\text{Fuel}]_{stoich}}$ | Energy per mole of fuel | J/mol |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Formatting Note:** Absolute-value and set-membership notations have been rendered using `\vert` and `\in` to avoid markdown parser breakage. Do **not** write raw `\vert` inside LaTeX macros during your own notes.

---

## 2.3 The TNT Equivalence Method — Detailed Walk-Through

The **TNT Equivalence Method** is the industry-standard (and KTU-board-favorite) technique for translating a deflagration into a quantifiable blast damage model.

The conversion logic is:

$$W_{TNT} = \eta_{eff} \cdot \frac{m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c,fuel}}{\Delta H_{c,TNT}}$$

Where:
- $m_{fuel}$ = mass of fuel vapor in the cloud (kg)
- $\Delta H_{c,fuel}$ = heat of combustion of the fuel (kJ/kg)
- $\Delta H_{c,TNT} = \mathbf{4680 \; kJ/kg}$ (reference TNT value)
- $\eta_{eff}$ = **explosion efficiency factor** — the fraction of chemical energy that converts into blast energy

Typical $\eta_{eff}$ values from the CCPS / TNO Purple Book guidelines:

| Fuel Class | Typical $\eta_{eff}$ |
|---|---|
| Methane | 0.05 |
| Propane / Butane (LPG) | 0.10 |
| Hydrogen | 0.10 |
| Hydrocarbon Vapors (general) | 0.03 – 0.05 |
| Dust clouds | 0.10 – 0.40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Once $W_{TNT}$ is known, an engineer uses the **Sachs Scaled Distance** to compute overpressure at any standoff distance, and feeds it into Probit equations for fatality / structural damage estimation. This is the standard workflow in **CCPS QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment)** studies.

---

## 2.4 The Cube Law (Hopkinson's Scaling)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hopkinson's Cube Law:** Two identical explosions of different fuel masses, in the same atmosphere, will produce the same overpressure at points where the **scaled distance** is identical.

The mathematical statement:

$$\Delta P(R) = f\!\left( \frac{R}{\sqrt[3]{W_{TNT}}} \right)$$

This means a **10 kg TNT** charge and a **1000 kg TNT** charge produce the same blast overpressure at distances scaled by $\sqrt[3]{10} \approx 2.154$.

$$\frac{R_{10kg}}{R_{1000kg}} = \frac{\sqrt[3]{10}}{\sqrt[3]{1000}} = \frac{2.154}{10} = 0.2154$$

This is the basis for every standard blast-damage nomogram used in process plant siting (API RP 752/753, CCPS Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Pressure in an Unvented Closed Vessel

> [!EXAM-TIP]
> This derivation is a **KTU Part B (14-mark) classic** — students who skip steps lose 4–6 marks routinely.

### Given Data
A rigid, closed vessel contains a stoichiometric propane-air mixture at initial conditions:
- Initial pressure: $P_1 = 1.013 \; \text{bar}$ (atmospheric)
- Initial temperature: $T_1 = 298 \; \text{K}$
- Ratio of specific heats for hot burned gas: $\gamma_b = 1.25$
- Ratio of specific heats for cold unburned gas: $\gamma_u = 1.35$

### Step 1 — Combustion Energy Released

For stoichiometric propane-air, the lower heating value produces adiabatic flame temperature:

$$T_{ad} \approx 2267 \; \text{K} \; (\text{propane-air stoichiometric})$$

### Step 2 — Apply Isentropic Compression of Unburned Gas

In an unvented closed-vessel deflagration, the unburned gas is compressed isentropically by the expanding burned gases. For isentropic compression:

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma_u - 1)/\gamma_u}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the "Equal-Volume" Constraint

In the limit where combustion is essentially instantaneous (high $\eta_{eff}$), the burned gas reaches its maximum pressure $P_{max}$ when the volumes are equal. The standard result:

$$P_{max} = P_1 \cdot \frac{(\gamma_b - 1) \cdot T_{ad} + \gamma_b \cdot T_1}{\gamma_b \cdot T_1}$$

But a more rigorous thermodynamic derivation treats the burned gas as reaching $T_{ad}$ and the unburned gas as being compressed to the same final pressure $P_{max}$. The simplest closed-form estimate:

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_1} = \frac{\varepsilon + (\gamma_b - 1)/\gamma_b}{\gamma_b/(\gamma_b - 1) - \varepsilon \cdot (\gamma_u - 1)/\gamma_u}$$

A commonly used simpler approximation (valid when the mixture is stoichiometric and combustion is complete):

$$\boxed{\,P_{max} \approx \frac{P_1 \cdot (\gamma_b - 1)}{\gamma_b} \cdot \frac{T_{ad}}{T_1}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_1} = \frac{(1.25 - 1)}{1.25} \cdot \frac{2267}{298}$$

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_1} = \frac{0.25}{1.25} \cdot 7.608 = 0.20 \cdot 7.608 = 1.522$$

$$P_{max} = 1.013 \cdot 1.522 = 1.542 \; \text{bar absolute}$$

But wait — this is the **thermodynamic maximum** for a non-instantaneous flame. For a true constant-volume deflagration with $\gamma_b = 1.25$ at stoichiometric, the **NFPA 68 standard** uses the empirical formula:

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_1} = \frac{\gamma_b}{\gamma_b + 1} \cdot \frac{T_{ad}}{T_1}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_1} = \frac{1.25}{2.25} \cdot \frac{2267}{298} = 0.556 \cdot 7.608 = 4.227$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{max} \approx 4.23 \; \text{bar gauge} \; (\text{i.e., } P_{max,abs} \approx 5.24 \; \text{bar})\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This ~8× pressure rise is the basis of NFPA 68 vent-area sizing tables.** If your vessel was only designed for atmospheric + process pressure (~2 bar), it will violently rupture.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TNT Equivalence for an LPG Vapor Cloud

> [!EXAM-TIP]
> This is the **most-asked numerical in KTU 2024 Scheme past papers** — expect variations.

### Problem Statement

A process plant releases **1000 kg of propane** that forms a vapor cloud. Ignition occurs, causing a deflagration. Estimate the TNT equivalent, given:
- $\Delta H_{c,\text{propane}} = 46,000 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $\Delta H_{c,TNT} = 4680 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $\eta_{eff} = 0.05$ (typical for hydrocarbon vapor cloud deflagration)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1** — Compute total chemical energy released:

$$E_{chem} = m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c,fuel}$$

$$E_{chem} = 1000 \; \text{kg} \times 46{,}000 \; \text{kJ/kg} = 4.6 \times 10^{7} \; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2** — Convert to TNT mass via the equivalence formula:

$$W_{TNT} = \eta_{eff} \cdot \frac{E_{chem}}{\Delta H_{c,TNT}}$$

$$W_{TNT} = 0.05 \cdot \frac{4.6 \times 10^{7} \; \text{kJ}}{4680 \; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

$$W_{TNT} = 0.05 \cdot 9829 \; \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{\,W_{TNT} \approx 491 \; \text{kg of TNT equivalent}\,}$$

**Step 3** — Compute scaled distance at a target of $R = 100 \; \text{m}$:

$$Z = \frac{R}{\sqrt[3]{W_{TNT}}} = \frac{100}{\sqrt[3]{491}} = \frac{100}{7.875}$$

$$Z = 12.70 \; \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 4** — Look up overpressure using Baker–Westine or Kinney–Graham curves at $Z = 12.70$:

From the **Kinney–Graham** chart:

| Scaled Distance $Z$ (m/kg$^{1/3}$) | Side-on Overpressure $\Delta P$ (bar) |
|---|---|
| 3 | ~1.0 |
| 6 | ~0.35 |
| 12.70 | ~0.10 |
| 30 | ~0.02 |

At $Z = 12.7$:

$$\Delta P_s \approx 0.10 \; \text{bar}$$

This is a **minor blast** — windows shatter, light injuries, but no major structural collapse. (For major structural damage, one would need $Z < 5$, requiring $W_{TNT} > 8000 \; \text{kg}$.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Deflagration Severity Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this matrix as your KTU viva-voce / short-answer backbone.

| Parameter | Slow Deflagration | Fast Deflagration | Strong Deflagration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flame speed $S_p$** | 0.5–5 m/s | 5–50 m/s | 50–300 m/s |
| **Mach number $Ma$** | $\ll 0.01$ | 0.01–0.15 | 0.15–0.85 |
| **Peak overpressure** | < 0.1 bar | 0.1–2 bar | 2–8 bar |
| **Damage** | Light damage / injuries | Window breakage, structural cracks | Wall collapse, fatalities |
| **Energy fraction $\eta_{eff}$** | < 1% | 1–3% | 3–10% |
| **Confinement needed** | None | Partial (turbulence) | Full |
| **Vapor cloud example** | Open atmosphere cloud | Semi-confined cloud | Confined cloud (DDT risk) |
| **Applicable QRA method** | TNT eq. with $\alpha = 0.01$ | TNT eq. with $\alpha = 0.03$ | TNT eq. with $\alpha = 0.10$ |

---

## 3.4 Deflagration-to-Detontion Transition (DDT) — Analytical Notes

DDT occurs when the local flame velocity inside confinement reaches the local speed of sound of the unburned gas mixture (which itself is rising due to pre-compression). Mathematically:

$$Ma_{flame} = \frac{S_p}{a_u(T_u^{local}, P_u^{local})} \to 1$$

Once $Ma \geq 1$, a **leading shock wave** detaches from the flame front. The shock auto-ignites the unreacted gases ahead — this is the onset of detonation.

> [!WARNING]
> DDT is **most likely** in long ducts ($L > 5 \cdot D$), hydrogen-rich mixtures, and highly reactive fuels. KTU frequently asks students to **list the conditions favoring DDT**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Deflagration Event

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Vapor Cloud Formation] --> B[Delayed Ignition Source]
    B --> C[Flame Kernel Growth]
    C --> D{Surface or Confinement Encountered?}
    D -- No Surface --> E[Free-Air Deflagration]
    D -- Confinement Present --> F[Pressure Build-up Loop]
    F --> G[Unburned Gas Compression]
    G --> H[Temperature Rise in Unburned Zone]
    H --> I[Local Burning Velocity Surge]
    I --> F
    F --> J{Pressure Exceeds Vent Threshold?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Pressure Relief via Vent]
    J -- No --> L{Mach Number Reaches 1.0?}
    L -- No --> M[Strong Deflagration]
    L -- Yes --> N[Deflagration to Detonation Transition DDT]
    K --> O[Structural Damage Assessment]
    M --> O
    N --> P[Catastrophic Vessel Failure]
    E --> O
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Combustion Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Combustion Events in Process Plants] --> B[Deflagration]
    A --> C[Detonation]
    A --> D[Fire without Explosion]
    B --> B1[Unconfined Cloud]
    B --> B2[Partially Confined]
    B --> B3[Fully Confined Vessel]
    B3 --> B3a[Slow Deflagration]
    B3 --> B3b[Fast Deflagration]
    B3 --> B3c[Strong Deflagration]
    B3c --> B3cX[DDT Risk Zone]
    C --> C1[Stable Detonation]
    C --> C2[Unstable Detonation]
    D --> D1[Pool Fire]
    D --> D2[Jet Fire]
    D --> D3[Flash Fire]
```

## 4.3 TNT Equivalence Calculation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Released Fuel Mass m_fuel] --> B[Select Heat of Combustion dH_c]
    B --> C[Select Efficiency Factor eta_eff from CCPS Tables]
    C --> D[Compute E_chem = m_fuel × dH_c]
    D --> E[Compute W_TNT = eta_eff × E_chem / 4680]
    E --> F[Select Target Standoff Distance R]
    F --> G[Compute Scaled Distance Z = R / cuberoot of W_TNT]
    G --> H[Read Overpressure dP from Baker-Westine Chart]
    H --> I[Apply Probit Equation for Fatality Probability]
    H --> J[Compare to API RP 752 Damage Criteria]
```

## 4.4 Process Plant Deflagration Zones — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Plant_Siting_Zones
        Zone1[Zone 1: Highest Risk - Within 30m of Cloud Center] --> Z1Impact[Catastrophic Failure Likely]
        Zone2[Zone 2: Severe Damage - 30 to 100m] --> Z2Impact[Wall Failure, Major Injuries]
        Zone3[Zone 3: Moderate Damage - 100 to 300m] --> Z3Impact[Window Shattering, Minor Injuries]
        Zone4[Zone 4: Safe Zone - Greater than 300m] --> Z4Impact[Negligible Damage]
    end
    Z1Impact --> Response[Emergency Response Activation]
    Z2Impact --> Response
    Z3Impact --> Response
    Z4Impact --> Response
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The zone boundaries are illustrative and depend on cloud mass, confinement, and fuel reactivity. In an actual KTU problem, you compute the boundaries via the Cube Law and the overpressure-damage thresholds from API RP 752.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate clearly between deflagration and detonation. Give one example of each from a process plant context.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Deflagration | Detonation |
|---|---|---|
| Flame speed | Subsonic ($Ma < 1$) | Supersonic ($Ma > 1$) |
| Propagation | Heat transfer (conduction, convection, radiation) | Shock wave compression |
| Peak overpressure | Moderate (typically < 10 bar) | Very high (> 20 bar) |
| Example | Vapor cloud explosion in an open atmosphere after LPG leak | Detonation in a gas-phase reactor with hydrogen-rich mixture |

**Vapor-cloud deflagration** is the most common process-plant explosion type. **Detonation** is rarer but far more destructive when it occurs.

*(3 marks: 1 for speed classification, 1 for propagation mechanism, 1 for example)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the TNT Equivalence Method? Why is an efficiency factor of only 1%–10% used even though all the fuel ultimately burns?**

**Model Answer:**

The **TNT Equivalence Method** converts the chemical energy of a vapor-cloud deflagration into an equivalent mass of TNT for blast-damage modeling:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta_{eff} \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_{c,fuel}}{\Delta H_{c,TNT}}$$

Only 1%–10% of the fuel's chemical energy is converted into **blast-pressure energy** because:
- A large fraction is lost as **heat of combustion in the fireball / pool fire** (radiative loss)
- Incomplete mixing between fuel and air pockets leaves unburned fuel
- Turbulent dissipation and acoustic losses
- Acoustic / seismic losses to the ground

Hence the **empirical efficiency factor** $\eta_{eff} \in [0.01, 0.10]$.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for explanation of energy conversion, 1 for citing the 1–10% range)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question is a Module Internal Choice. Students answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Marks are split **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

### Question A (Alternative 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: A 50 m³ closed process vessel contains a stoichiometric propane-air mixture. Estimate the maximum pressure that would develop during a deflagration if no venting is provided. Comment on the structural implications.**

**Given:**
- Initial pressure $P_1 = 1.013 \; \text{bar}$
- Initial temperature $T_1 = 298 \; \text{K}$
- Adiabatic flame temperature $T_{ad} = 2267 \; \text{K}$
- Ratio of specific heats for burned gas $\gamma_b = 1.25$

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1** — Apply the constant-volume deflagration pressure formula (NFPA 68 simplified):

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_1} = \frac{\gamma_b}{\gamma_b + 1} \cdot \frac{T_{ad}}{T_1}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the values:

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_1} = \frac{1.25}{1.25 + 1} \cdot \frac{2267}{298} = \frac{1.25}{2.25} \cdot 7.608$$

$$\frac{P_{max}}{P_1} = 0.556 \cdot 7.608 = 4.227$$

**Step 3** — Compute absolute maximum pressure:

$$P_{max,abs} = 4.227 \cdot 1.013 = 4.28 \; \text{bar absolute}$$

$$P_{max,gauge} = 4.28 - 1.013 = 3.27 \; \text{bar gauge}$$

**Step 4** — Structural Implications:

The vessel will experience a **gauge pressure of ~3.3 bar**. Typical atmospheric storage tanks are designed for only 0.05–0.5 bar gauge. **The vessel will rupture catastrophically** if not equipped with proper pressure relief (rupture disc or vent sized per NFPA 68 / API 521).

For a 50 m³ vessel with $P_{max} \approx 4.3 \; \text{bar abs}$, the **required vent area** (using NFPA 68 nomogram) is approximately:

$$A_v = K_v \cdot \frac{V^{2/3}}{P_{red,max}}$$

For $V = 50 \; \text{m}^3$, $P_{red,max} = 0.2 \; \text{bar gauge}$, $K_v$ for propane ≈ 4.4:

$$A_v \approx 4.4 \cdot \frac{50^{2/3}}{0.2} \approx 4.4 \cdot \frac{13.57}{0.2} \approx 298 \; \text{m}^2$$

This is enormous — highlighting why **prevention (inertion, gas detection)** is far better than reliance on vents alone.

*(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 1 for numerical answer, 2 for structural comment)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain with neat sketches the Cube Law (Hopkinson's Scaling) for blast wave propagation. Why is it central to blast damage modeling?**

**Model Answer:**

Hopkinson's Cube Law (also called Sachs' scaling law) states that for two explosions of different energy yields in the same atmosphere, **identical peak overpressures are observed at distances scaled by the cube root of the energy ratio**.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \left(\frac{E_1}{E_2}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{W_1}{W_2}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Scaled Distance:**

$$Z = \frac{R}{\sqrt[3]{W}} \quad \text{(units: m/kg}^{1/3}\text{)}$$

**Derivation Sketch:**

1. Consider two hemispherical surface bursts of explosive mass $W_1$ and $W_2$.
2. The blast wave at scaled distance $Z$ has the same peak overpressure.
3. This is because energy per unit area of the wavefront scales as $E/A \propto W/R^3$ — to keep this ratio constant, $R \propto W^{1/3}$.

**Why Central to Blast Damage Modeling:**

- **Single nomogram** can predict damage for any explosive yield.
- Standard **overpressure-vs-scaled-distance curves** (Baker–Westine, Kinney–Graham) are universally applicable.
- API RP 752/753 siting criteria are directly expressed in scaled-distance form.
- Computational tools (e.g., ALOHA, EFFECTS, PHAST) use the cube law as the underlying scaling.

**Implication for Process Plant Siting:**

For a 1000 kg TNT-equivalent deflagration, the **lethal radius** (where overpressure ≈ 1 bar) is at:

$$Z_{lethal} \approx 5 \; \text{m/kg}^{1/3} \implies R_{lethal} \approx 5 \cdot \sqrt[3]{1000} = 50 \; \text{m}$$

Hence a 50 m exclusion zone is required around the release point.

*(7 marks: 2 for law statement, 2 for mathematical form, 1 for derivation logic, 2 for siting implication)*

---

### Question B (Alternative 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: 200 kg of methane is accidentally released from a compressor station and forms a vapor cloud. After delayed ignition, a deflagration occurs. Estimate (i) the TNT equivalent, and (ii) the overpressure at a control room located 80 m from the cloud center.**

**Given:**
- $\Delta H_{c,\text{methane}} = 55,500 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $\eta_{eff} = 0.05$ (typical for methane)
- $\Delta H_{c,TNT} = 4680 \; \text{kJ/kg}$

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — TNT Equivalent:**

**Step 1** — Total chemical energy:

$$E_{chem} = 200 \; \text{kg} \times 55{,}500 \; \text{kJ/kg} = 1.11 \times 10^{7} \; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2** — Apply TNT equivalence formula:

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta_{eff} \cdot E_{chem}}{\Delta H_{c,TNT}} = \frac{0.05 \cdot 1.11 \times 10^7}{4680}$$

$$W_{TNT} = \frac{5.55 \times 10^5}{4680} = 118.6 \; \text{kg of TNT equivalent}$$

**Part (ii) — Overpressure at 80 m:**

**Step 3** — Scaled distance:

$$Z = \frac{R}{\sqrt[3]{W_{TNT}}} = \frac{80}{\sqrt[3]{118.6}} = \frac{80}{4.916}$$

$$Z = 16.27 \; \text{m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Step 4** — Read overpressure from Baker–Westine chart at $Z = 16.27$:

From the **Baker–Westine** (1975) correlation for hemispherical surface burst:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{808 \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}}$$

Substituting $Z = 16.27$:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{808 \cdot \left[1 + (3.616)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + (339.0)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (50.84)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (12.05)^2}}$$

$$\frac{\Delta P}{P_0} = \frac{808 \cdot 14.07}{18.42 \cdot 50.85 \cdot 12.13} = \frac{11{,}366}{11{,}371} \approx 1.00$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta P \approx 1.0 \; \text{bar (gauge)}\,}$$

**Interpretation:**

A control-room occupant at this overpressure will experience:
- ~50% probability of fatality (per TNO Green Book Probit equations)
- Total structural collapse of unreinforced structures
- Need for blast-resistant design (API RP 753) or relocation

*(7 marks: 2 for chemical energy, 2 for TNT calculation, 2 for scaled distance, 1 for overpressure read-out)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: With the aid of a sketch, describe the phenomenon of Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT). Under what conditions in a process plant is DDT most likely?**

**Model Answer:**

**Sketch Description:**

```
Duct/Pipe (length L >> diameter D)
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|  +-----------+  +-----------+  +-----------+              |
|  |           |  |           |  |  +-------+ |              |
|  |   Gas     |  |   Gas     |  |  |SHOCK  | |              |
|  | (unburned)|=>| (turbulent|=>|  |WAVE   | |=> DETONATION |
|  |           |  |  flame)   |  |  |detaches| |              |
|  +-----------+  +-----------+  |  +-------+ |              |
|                                +-----------+              |
|  [Ignition]    [Acceleration]    [Transition]              |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Mechanism (5 Sequential Stages):**

1. **Flame Initiation**: Spark ignites a small flame kernel.
2. **Laminar Propagation**: Flame travels at $S_u \approx 0.5$ m/s.
3. **Turbulent Acceleration**: Wall friction generates turbulence; eddy combustion increases effective flame area; $S_p$ rises sharply.
4. **Shock Formation**: Pressure pulses compress unburned gas ahead; these coalesce into a leading shock front.
5. **Detonation**: When the shock strength is sufficient to auto-ignite the unburned mixture within a few microseconds, the shock couples with the flame → **detonation wave** at $S_p > 1000$ m/s.

**Conditions Favoring DDT in Process Plants:**

- **Long ducts or pipelines** ($L/D > 5$)
- **Hydrogen-rich mixtures** (very high $\gamma$ and reactivity)
- **Acetylene, ethylene oxide** (highly reactive fuels)
- **High turbulence** from valves, orifices, fans
- **Strong confinement** (closed vessels with small relief)
- **Pre-heated unburned gas** (increases $a_u$ sensitivity)

**Process Plant Examples:**
- Hydrogen pipeline rupture with delayed ignition
- Reactor cooling failure with runaway → vent line DDT
- Acetylene compressor discharge line
- Vapor cloud entering a process building / turbine enclosure

**Mitigation:**
- Inertion (oxygen reduction below LOC)
- Flame arrestors (prevent flame propagation)
- Adequate vent sizing (NFPA 68)
- Explosion isolation valves (isolation of equipment)

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch, 2 for stages, 2 for conditions, 1 for mitigation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing units**: Students frequently use $H_c$ in J/kg instead of kJ/kg, yielding answers off by 1000×. Always declare units explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the cube root**: Scaled distance $Z = R / W^{1/3}$, not $R / W$. Marks deducted for wrong exponent.
> 3. **Confusing $\Delta H_c$ (LHV vs. HHV)**: Use LHV (lower heating value) for vapor clouds, as combustion products remain gaseous.
> 4. **Using $P_1 = 0$ (gauge)**: For absolute pressure rise formulas, always use **absolute** initial pressure.
> 5. **Not stating the efficiency factor's range**: $\eta_{eff}$ is not 100% — students who write $\eta = 1$ lose 1–2 marks instantly.
> 6. **Drawing a generic flame and calling it DDT**: KTU examiners expect the **5-stage mechanism** with shock-coupling explanation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Deflagration (PEPOT754, Module 2)**

- [ ] **Deflagration** = subsonic combustion ($S_p < a$), propagated by heat transfer.
- [ ] **Detonation** = supersonic combustion ($S_p > a$), propagated by shock wave.
- [ ] **Laminar burning velocity $S_u$** is a property of the fuel-oxidizer mixture; typically **0.3–3 m/s** for hydrocarbons.
- [ ] **Flame speed $S_p = S_u \cdot \varepsilon$**, where $\varepsilon \approx 6$–$8$ for hydrocarbon-air.
- [ ] **TNT Equivalence Formula**: $W_{TNT} = \eta_{eff} \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c / 4680$ (kg, kJ).
- [ ] **Efficiency factor $\eta_{eff}$** is **1%–10%** (NOT 100%).
- [ ] **Cube Law / Hopkinson Scaling**: $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$.
- [ ] **Maximum unvented vessel pressure** can be **8–10× atmospheric** for stoichiometric hydrocarbon-air.
- [ ] **NFPA 68** governs vent sizing; **API RP 752/753** governs blast-resistant building design.
- [ ] **DDT** is favored by long ducts, hydrogen-rich fuels, strong confinement, and turbulence.
- [ ] **Damage thresholds** (overpressure): 0.07 bar = window breakage, 0.35 bar = building damage, 1.0 bar = severe structural damage.
- [ ] **Three deflagration severity classes**: slow (< 5 m/s), fast (5–50 m/s), strong (50–300 m/s).
- [ ] Always use **absolute** pressure for $P_{max}$ calculations; always use **LHV** for $\Delta H_c$ of vapor clouds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Detonation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Detonation # 💥 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Detonation** is a *supersonic* autopropagating combustion wave in which the reaction zone is coupled to a **leading shock front**. The chemical reaction front propagates through the unreacted medium at a velocity greater than the **speed of sound** in the unreacted medium, generating a self-sustained overpressure pulse of typically **1 MPa – 2 MPa** (≈ 10 – 20 bar) for gas-phase detonations and up to **20 GPa** for condensed-phase explosives. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, detonation is treated as the **upper-bound catastrophic hazard** in process safety because: - The peak overpressure, $\Delta p$, propagates at velocity $D > c_1$ (where $c_1$ is the acoustic speed of the unburnt mixture). - A **detonation cannot be vented** by conventional rupture-disc or blow-off panels, unlike deflagration. - It is associated with **Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT)** in long pipelines, vapor clouds, and dust clouds. The fundamental detonation parameters that every safety engineer must know are: - **Detonation Velocity ($D$)** — typically **1,500 m/s – 2,000 m/s** for hydrocarbon–air, up to **9,000 m/s** for solid explosives. - **Cell Size ($\lambda$)** — typically **1 mm – 100 mm**, governing the *critical tube diameter*. - **Critical Initiation Energy ($E_c$)** — **$10^{-6}$ J – $10^{-3}$ J** for sensitive mixtures. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Whiplash Effect 🏎️** > Imagine a long, single-lane highway tunnel filled with bumper-to-bumper cars (the unreacted fuel-oxidizer mixture). If a heavy truck at the back slams the brakes while accelerating, the cars pile up into a *deflagration* (a slow shove, subsonic pressure wave). > > Now, picture the same tunnel but **a wall suddenly appears at the front** and begins moving *toward* the cars at **2 km/s**. The cars can't escape sideways. They are violently compressed, igniting *as they stack up*. This pile-up is the **leading shock front**; the fire that races through the compressed layer is the **reaction zone**; the wall is the **detonation front**. The wall-outruns the cars' sound, so this is *supersonic* — that is detonation. A second, more familiar analogy: **lightning + thunder**. Lightning is the shock; thunder is the energy release. The shock *leads*, the reaction *follows*, locked together by compression heating. ## 1.3 Deflagration vs. Detonation — The Critical Contrast | Parameter | Deflagration | Detonation | |---|---|---| | Flame speed | 1 – 100 m/s | **1,500 – 9,000 m/s** | | Mach no. ($M$) | $M < 0.1$ | $M = 5 - 10$ | | Pressure rise | 0.1 – 1 bar | **10 – 200 bar** | | Pressure wave | Acoustic, subsonic | Shock, **supersonic** | | Driver of propagation | Heat conduction, diffusion | **Shock compression heating** | | Effect of venting | Strongly mitigated | **Not effective** | | Typical event | Open pool fire, flare | Vapor cloud explosion in pipe, BLEVE pressure pulse | --- # 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Chapman–Jouguet (C-J) Theory — Theoretical Backbone The CJ theory (1889–1905) treats a detonation as a **steady, one-dimensional** wave with the following three conservation equations across the shock front (Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions). ### 2.1.1 The Three Conservation Laws Let subscript **1** denote the *unreacted* state and subscript **2** the *reacted* state. The wave speed (lab frame) is $D$. **Conservation of Mass:** $$\rho_1(D - u_1) = \rho_2(D - u_2)$$ **Conservation of Momentum:** $$p_1 + \rho_1(D - u_1)^2 = p_2 + \rho_2(D - u_2)^2$$ **Conservation of Energy (Rankine–Hugoniot):** $$h_1 + \frac{(D - u_1)^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{(D - u_2)^2}{2}$$ where $h = c_p T$ is the specific enthalpy for an ideal gas. ### 2.1.2 The Chapman–Jouguet Condition The CJ condition selects the unique physically stable point on the **Hugoniot curve** of the products: $$D = u_2 + c_2$$ where $c_2$ is the acoustic speed of the burned gases. Physically, this means **the detonation velocity equals the local sound speed relative to the burned products**, so no rarefaction wave from the rear can overtake the front — the detonation is *self-sustained*. ### 2.1.3 Derivation of C-J Detonation Velocity (Ideal-Gas, Constant-γ Model) Assume: - $u_1 \approx 0$ (unreacted gas at rest, $p_1 = p_0$). - $\gamma_1 = \gamma_2 = \gamma$ (constant specific-heat ratio). - $Q$ = chemical energy released per unit mass. From the Rankine-Hugoniot equations, after algebraic manipulation, the detonation velocity for a *high-strength* detonation ($p_2 \gg p_1$) reduces to the celebrated **C-J formula**: $$\boxed{D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)\,Q}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This formula is the **single most important equation** in detonation theory for KTU board exams. Notice that $D_{CJ}$ is **independent of the ignition mechanism** — it is a thermodynamic property of the explosive mixture. ### 2.2 The Zeldovich–von Neumann–Döring (ZND) Model — A More Realistic Picture The CJ model is a *lumped* model (one-step, instantaneous reaction). The **ZND model (1940s)** describes the internal structure: 1. **Incident Shock** — compresses the medium to the *von Neumann spike* state. 2. **Induction Zone** — finite-time chemical induction, no significant reaction. 3. **Reaction Zone** — exothermic reactions liberate $Q$, pressure drops to CJ state. 4. **Taylor Expansion Wave** — products expand as the wave passes. > [!NOTE] > The ZND model predicts a **spike pressure** $p_{VN}$ higher than the CJ pressure $p_{CJ}$. The ratio is approximately $p_{VN}/p_{CJ} \approx 2$ for strong gas detonations. ### 2.3 The "Why" — Engineering Significance - **Why is CJ important?** Because it gives the *minimum self-sustained* detonation velocity and pressure. Any slower wave decays. - **Why is cell size important?** Because $\lambda$ is the *characteristic transverse length scale* of the detonation structure and determines the **critical tube diameter** for propagation. - **Why is DDT feared?** Because pipelines $L > 5\,\text{m}$ with rough internals routinely transition from deflagration to detonation, multiplying blast damage by 50–100×. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following table verbatim. These are the **high-yield formulas** for any KTU question on detonation. | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)\,Q}$ | CJ detonation velocity (strong) | m/s | | 2 | $D_{CJ} = u_2 + c_2$ | CJ sonic condition | m/s | | 3 | $\dfrac{p_{CJ}}{p_1} = \dfrac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_1 D_{CJ}^2}{(\gamma + 1) p_1 + \dots}$ | CJ pressure ratio (use $p_{CJ} \approx \rho_1 D_{CJ}^2/(\gamma + 1)$ for strong detonations) | Pa | | 4 | $\dfrac{T_{CJ}}{T_1} = \dfrac{2\gamma(\gamma-1)}{(\gamma+1)^2}\cdot\dfrac{D_{CJ}^2}{R T_1}$ | CJ temperature ratio (using $Q \approx c_v(T_2-T_1)$) | K | | 5 | $d_{crit} = 13\,\lambda$ | Critical tube diameter for cylindrical propagation | m | | 6 | $L_{run-up} = 5\text{–}10 \cdot d_{crit}$ | DDT run-up distance in rough pipes | m | | 7 | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c}{\Delta H_{TNT}}$ | TNT-equivalent yield (note: $\Delta H_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg; $\eta \approx 0.02\text{–}0.05$) | kg TNT | | 8 | $\dfrac{E_c}{E_{c,ref}} = \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda_{ref}}\right)^3$ | Critical initiation-energy scaling with cell size | J | | 9 | $p_{VN} = \dfrac{2\gamma M^2 - (\gamma-1)}{\gamma+1}\,p_1$ | von Neumann spike pressure (normal shock) | Pa | | 10 | $Z = \dfrac{D_{CJ}}{c_1}$ | Detonation Mach number (typically 5 – 10) | — | | 11 | $K_{St}$ (bar·m/s) | **Dust explosion index** — *not* a detonation parameter but often confused; quoted in dust-deflagration tests only | bar·m/s | | 12 | $\Delta p_{det} \approx 2 \Delta p_{deflag}$ | Rule-of-thumb peak overpressure ratio (detonation vs deflagration) | Pa | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Domain | Why Detonation Matters | |---|---| | **Oil & Gas (offshore platforms)** | DDT in subsea flowlines and risers → unconfined vapor cloud detonation | | **Petrochemical crackers** | Ethylene decomposes exothermically; detonation in compressor piping is a documented cause of major losses | | **Pharmaceutical / Powder Handling** | Dust detonation in cyclones, spray driers, bag filters | | **Ammonia / LNG Storage** | Unconfined vapor cloud detonation (UVCE) — leading cause of process plant fatalities | | **Mining / Quarries** | ANFO detonation — design of blast patterns, fly-rock, ground vibration | --- # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation A — The Chapman–Jouguet Detonation Velocity (From First Principles) We will derive the expression $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)\,Q}$ step by step. ### Step 1 — Set up the conservation equations in the shock-fixed frame. Let the detonation be a 1-D steady wave in the lab frame with velocity $D$. Switch to the *shock-fixed frame*: a fluid particle enters with velocity $(D - u_1)$ and leaves with $(D - u_2)$. In this frame, the **mass flux** is $\rho_1(D - u_1) = \rho_2(D - u_2) = m$ (constant). ### Step 2 — Express the momentum equation as the *Rayleigh line*. The momentum equation can be written in the convenient form: $$p_2 - p_1 = \rho_1(D - u_1)^2\!\left(1 - \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\right)$$ Using $V = 1/\rho$ (specific volume) and $m = \rho_1(D-u_1)$: $$p_2 - p_1 = m^2 (V_1 - V_2)$$ This is the **Rayleigh line** — a straight line in the $p$–$V$ plane. ### Step 3 — Write the energy equation as the *Hugoniot curve*. For ideal gases with $h = c_p T$ and $Q$ = heat released per unit mass: $$h_2 - h_1 = \frac{(p_2 - p_1)(\rho_1 + \rho_2)}{2\rho_1 \rho_2} + Q$$ After substituting $\rho = 1/V$ and simplifying: $$h_2 - h_1 = \frac{1}{2}(p_2 + p_1)(V_1 - V_2) + Q$$ This is the **Hugoniot curve** of the products — the locus of all admissible end states. ### Step 4 — Tangency condition (the CJ postulate). The CJ condition $D = u_2 + c_2$ implies that the **Rayleigh line is tangent to the Hugoniot** at the CJ point. Algebraically, this yields: $$\left(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial V}\right)_{CJ} = \dfrac{p_2 - p_1}{V_1 - V_2}\bigg|_{CJ} = -\rho_2^2 c_2^2$$ ### Step 5 — Specialize to ideal gas (constant $\gamma$). For an ideal gas with constant $\gamma$: - $h_2 - h_1 = c_{p,2}(T_2 - T_1) = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\left(\dfrac{p_2}{\rho_2} - \dfrac{p_1}{\rho_1}\right)$ - $c_2^2 = \gamma p_2/\rho_2$ Substituting these into the Hugoniot + tangent condition and simplifying (algebra omitted for brevity but fully shown in standard texts): $$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{(\gamma + 1)\rho_2 - (\gamma - 1)\rho_1}{(\gamma + 1)\rho_1 - (\gamma - 1)\rho_2}$$ Solving simultaneously with the Rayleigh line slope $-\rho_1^2 (D - u_1)^2$ gives the **CJ density ratio**: $$\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \frac{\gamma + 1}{\gamma}$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute back to get $D_{CJ}$. With $u_1 = 0$ and $\rho_2/\rho_1 = (\gamma+1)/\gamma$: $$D_{CJ} - u_2 = \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}(D_{CJ}) = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma+1}D_{CJ}$$ Hence $u_2 = D_{CJ} - \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma+1}D_{CJ} = \dfrac{D_{CJ}}{\gamma+1}$. Using the energy equation with the above: $$Q = \frac{1}{2}(D_{CJ} - u_2)^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\right)^2\right]$$ After algebra: $$Q = \frac{D_{CJ}^2}{2(\gamma+1)^2}\left[(\gamma+1)^2 - \gamma^2\right] = \frac{D_{CJ}^2 (\gamma^2 - 1)}{2(\gamma+1)^2}$$ Solving for $D_{CJ}$: $$\boxed{D_{CJ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(\gamma+1)^2 Q}{\gamma^2 - 1}}}$$ > ⚠️ **Subtlety for KTU exams:** The "textbook-canonical" form $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)Q}$ applies in the limit $p_2 \gg p_1$ (strong detonation) where the term $p_1/\rho_1$ on the *Rayleigh line* is negligible compared with $D_{CJ}^2$. The full expression retains the $p_1$-correction: $$D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)Q + \frac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}\cdot\frac{p_1}{\rho_1}}$$ ### Step 7 — Sanity check. For stoichiometric methane–air at 1 atm: $\gamma \approx 1.3$, $Q \approx 2.7 \times 10^6$ J/kg. $$D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2 \times (1.3^2 - 1) \times 2.7 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.69 \times 2.7 \times 10^6}$$ $$D_{CJ} \approx \sqrt{3.726 \times 10^6} \approx 1{,}930\ \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark$$ Experimental value: ≈ 1,800 – 1,900 m/s. **Excellent agreement.** 🎯 --- ## 3.2 Derivation B — von Neumann Spike Pressure for a Normal Shock For a *frozen* shock (no reaction) compressing the unreacted gas, the Rankine-Hugoniot jump gives: $$\frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{2\gamma M^2 - (\gamma-1)}{\gamma+1}$$ where $M$ is the Mach number of the post-shock flow with respect to the upstream sound speed. **For a CJ detonation**, $M$ is the detonation Mach number $Z = D_{CJ}/c_1$. For strong detonations $Z \gg 1$, the leading term dominates: $$p_{VN} \approx \frac{2\gamma}{\gamma+1}\rho_1 D_{CJ}^2$$ **Numerical check.** Using $D_{CJ} = 1{,}930$ m/s, $\gamma = 1.3$, $\rho_1 = 1.2$ kg/m³: $$p_{VN} \approx \frac{2 \times 1.3}{2.3} \times 1.2 \times (1{,}930)^2 \approx 1.355 \times 1.2 \times 3.72 \times 10^6$$ $$p_{VN} \approx 6.05 \times 10^6 \ \text{Pa} = 60.5\ \text{bar} \quad \checkmark$$ The CJ pressure is typically ~½ of this (≈ 30 bar), and the reflected pressure at a wall can reach **150 bar** — hence the engineering rule: *detonation walls must be designed for $\geq 100$ bar impulsive loading*. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Two Models | Aspect | C-J Theory | ZND Model | |---|---|---| | Year / Originators | Chapman 1889, Jouguet 1905 | Zeldovich, von Neumann, Döring ~1940 | | Reaction rate | Infinite (instant) | Finite induction + reaction zone | | Structure | Single jump | Shock + induction + reaction + rarefaction | | Pressure profile | Square wave | Spike → CJ plateau → decay | | Use in KTU exam | Quick $D_{CJ}$ calculation | Discussion / qualitative sketch | | Limitation | Cannot predict $\lambda$ or $d_{crit}$ | Cannot predict $d_{crit}$ (needs experiment) | | Empirical supplements | — | $\lambda$ from soot-track experiments | --- # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ZND Pressure–Distance Profile (Block Diagram) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagrams the **ZND detonation structure** travelling from left (unreacted) to right (products). These three numbers answer nearly every KTU numerical on detonation propagation in pipes. --- # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Differentiate between deflagration and detonation. List any four distinguishing parameters.** **Model Answer:** Deflagration and detonation are two fundamentally different modes of combustion wave propagation. 1. **Propagation mechanism**: Deflagration propagates by *heat and mass diffusion* across a thin flame; detonation propagates by *shock compression* of the unreacted medium. 2. **Flame speed**: Deflagration velocity is in the range **1 – 100 m/s** ($M < 0.1$), whereas detonation velocity is **1,500 – 9,000 m/s** ($M = 5 - 10$). 3. **Peak pressure**: Deflagration peak overpressure is typically $\leq 1$ bar; detonation peak pressure is **10 – 200 bar**. 4. **Effect of venting**: Deflagration is *strongly mitigated* by vent panels; detonation is **not affected** by conventional vents. 5. **Direction of pressure wave**: In deflagration, the pressure pulse is subsonic relative to the unreacted gas; in detonation it is *supersonic* and outruns acoustic signals. 6. **Critical parameters**: Deflagration is characterized by $K_G$ / $K_{St}$; detonation is characterized by cell size $\lambda$ and critical diameter $d_{crit} = 13\lambda$. (Any 4 of the above. **3 marks** awarded for 4 correct points with proper classification.) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **What is the Chapman–Jouguet condition? Why is it physically important for detonation?** **Model Answer:** The **Chapman–Jouguet (CJ) condition** states that the detonation velocity $D$ equals the sum of the particle velocity $u_2$ and the local acoustic speed $c_2$ of the *burned* products behind the front: $$D = u_2 + c_2$$ **Physical significance**: - It guarantees that **no rarefaction wave from the rear can overtake the reaction zone**, so the detonation is *self-sustained* indefinitely in an ideal medium. - It uniquely selects the *minimum* admissible velocity on the Hugoniot curve that satisfies mass, momentum, and energy conservation. - The CJ point determines the **thermodynamic state of the products** immediately behind the front — used for blast-damage estimation. - It explains why detonations are essentially insensitive to the ignition source strength: only the mixture thermodynamics matter. (1 mark for formula, 2 marks for physical interpretation.) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Combustion Wave Theory* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** > **A(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Zeldovich–von Neumann–Döring (ZND) model** of a detonation wave. Identify the four principal zones and explain the role of each in the propagation mechanism. **[7 Marks]** > > **A(b)** Derive the **Chapman–Jouguet detonation velocity** for an ideal gas with constant specific-heat ratio $\gamma$, clearly stating the *Rankine–Hugoniot jump conditions* and the *CJ sonic condition*. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — A(a) **Sketch** (the student must draw a pressure-vs-distance profile with the following labeled zones — see §4.1): ``` p │ ┌────┐ │ │ VN │ ← von Neumann spike │ │spike│ │ │ └──────┐ │ │ reaction │ ← Taylor rarefaction │ │ zone │ │ │ │ │ │ └────── │ │ (products expand) │___________│________________________ shock distance ``` **The four principal zones** (left → right): 1. **Unreacted gas region** ($p_1$, $T_1$, $\rho_1$) — the medium ahead of the wave. 2. **Leading Shock (von Neumann Spike)** — non-reactive compression to state VN; $p_{VN} \approx 2 p_{CJ}$ for strong gas detonations; the *trigger* for chemistry. 3. **Reaction Zone** — finite-time exothermic reaction; pressure falls from $p_{VN}$ to $p_{CJ}$ as energy $Q$ is liberated. 4. **Taylor Expansion Wave** — post-CJ rarefaction; products expand isentropically to ambient. **Propagation mechanism**: The shock pre-heats and pre-compresses the gas. During the induction delay, radicals (H, O, OH) build up. Once chemistry ignites, the liberated energy feeds back into the shock via pressure waves, maintaining its strength — *self-sustaining*. #### Model Solution — A(b) [Use the complete derivation from §3.1 above. The student must explicitly write: - The three Rankine-Hugoniot equations (mass, momentum, energy). - The substitution $V = 1/\rho$. - The Rayleigh line equation $p_2 - p_1 = m^2(V_1 - V_2)$. - The Hugoniot relation $h_2 - h_1 = \frac{1}{2}(p_2 + p_1)(V_1 - V_2) + Q$. - The CJ tangency condition $D = u_2 + c_2$. - Use of $\rho_2/\rho_1 = (\gamma+1)/\gamma$. - Final boxed result: $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)\,Q}$. **Numerical Check** (recommended for full marks): For methane–air ($\gamma = 1.3$, $Q = 2.7 \times 10^6$ J/kg), $D_{CJ} \approx 1{,}930$ m/s.] --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Engineering Application* **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **B(a)** Explain the concept of **Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition (DDT)**. Describe three mechanisms by which DDT is initiated in process piping, and identify the *engineering parameters* that control the run-up distance. **[7 Marks]** > > **B(b)** A stoichiometric methane–air mixture ($\gamma = 1.3$, $Q = 2.7 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $\rho_1 = 1.2$ kg/m³) is released inside a $50$ m long process pipe of diameter $0.6$ m. The mixture has a measured cell size $\lambda = 30$ mm. Determine: > (i) The Chapman–Jouguet detonation velocity $D_{CJ}$. > (ii) The critical tube diameter $d_{crit}$. > (iii) Whether detonation can propagate in this pipe. > (iv) The peak overpressure likely at the pipe exit, using the rule $\Delta p \approx 2 \rho_1 D_{CJ}^2 / (\gamma + 1)$. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — B(a) **DDT Definition**: DDT is the *abrupt transition* of a subsonic, diffusion-driven deflagration into a supersonic, shock-coupled detonation, often occurring within a few metres of a flame-acoustic obstacle (orifice, bend, valve). **Three initiating mechanisms**: 1. **Turbulent flame acceleration** by pipe-wall friction and congestion — increases flame surface area and local $u'$ RMS velocity. 2. **Shock-flame interaction and auto-ignition** in the boundary layer — pre-compressed pocket of unreacted gas reaches the *induction time* and explodes. 3. **Wave reflection and focusing** at bends, orifices and closed ends — repeated shock reflections raise local $T$ and $p$ until spontaneous ignition. **Run-up distance control parameters**: - $L_{run-up} \approx 5\text{–}10 \cdot d_{crit} = 5\text{–}10 \cdot 13\lambda$ - *Lower* run-up occurs with: smaller $\lambda$ (more reactive), higher blockage ratio (more obstacles), higher initial $T$, higher initial $p$, longer unburned length. #### Model Solution — B(b) **(i) CJ detonation velocity**: $$D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)\,Q} = \sqrt{2 \times (1.3^2 - 1) \times 2.7 \times 10^6}$$ $$D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.69 \times 2.7 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{3.726 \times 10^6} \approx 1{,}930\ \text{m/s}$$ **(ii) Critical tube diameter**: $$d_{crit} = 13\lambda = 13 \times 0.030 = 0.39\ \text{m}$$ **(iii) Propagation check**: Pipe diameter $D_{pipe} = 0.6\ \text{m} > d_{crit} = 0.39\ \text{m}$. **YES**, detonation will propagate. **(iv) Peak overpressure at exit**: $$\Delta p \approx \frac{2 \rho_1 D_{CJ}^2}{\gamma + 1} = \frac{2 \times 1.2 \times (1{,}930)^2}{2.3}$$ $$\Delta p \approx \frac{2 \times 1.2 \times 3.72 \times 10^6}{2.3} = \frac{8.94 \times 10^6}{2.3} \approx 3.89 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta p \approx 3.9\ \text{MPa} \approx 39\ \text{bar}}$$ **Conclusion**: The 50-m pipe satisfies the propagation criterion. Blast walls downstream must be designed for $\Delta p \geq 30$ bar with a dynamic-load factor of 1.5 for impulsive detonation loading. --- > [!WARNING] > **🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Mixing up deflagration and detonation formulas.** $K_{St}$ applies to dust *deflagrations*, NOT detonations. Using $K_{St}$ in a detonation question is an instant **0-mark** answer. > > 2. **Forgetting the strong-detonation assumption.** The formula $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2-1)Q}$ is valid only when $p_2 \gg p_1$. For low-pressure systems ($< 1$ atm), the $p_1/\rho_1$ correction term MUST be included. > > 3. **Confusing cell size $\lambda$ with induction length $\delta$.** They are *not* the same. $\delta$ = induction-zone length (ZND); $\lambda$ = transverse cell width (experimental). The relation is $\lambda \approx 10 \text{ – } 100 \delta$. > > 4. **Ignoring the 13-$\lambda$ rule.** Many students write $d_{crit} = \lambda$ or $d_{crit} = 3\lambda$. The correct factor is **13** for cylindrical tubes and **10** for planar channels. > > 5. **Neglecting units in numericals.** $D_{CJ}$ must be in m/s, $Q$ in J/kg, $\rho_1$ in kg/m³. Mixing CGS and SI units is a top-3 mistake. > > 6. **Forgetting the CJ sonic condition** $D = u_2 + c_2$ when asked "Why is CJ the minimum self-sustained detonation?". --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - 🔥 **Detonation** = *supersonic* combustion wave, $D > c_1$, $M = 5-10$. - 🔥 **Deflagration** = *subsonic*, $D < c_1$, governed by $K_G$ / $K_{St}$. - 🧮 **Master formula**: $D_{CJ} = \sqrt{2(\gamma^2 - 1)\,Q}$. - 🧮 **CJ sonic condition**: $D_{CJ} = u_2 + c_2$. - 📏 **Critical diameter**: $d_{crit} = 13\lambda$ (cylindrical), $10\lambda$ (planar). - 🛣️ **Run-up distance**: $L_{run-up} \approx 5\text{–}10\,d_{crit}$. - 🌪️ **DDT** is the most feared event in long, obstructed pipes. - 💥 **Peak detonation overpressure** $\sim 10$–$20$ bar (gas); up to **200 bar** (solid explosives). - 🛑 **Venting does NOT work** for detonations — use flame arrestors, barricades, TNT-equivalent blast walls. - 📐 **TNT equivalent**: $W_{TNT} = \eta \cdot m_{fuel} \cdot \Delta H_c / 4680\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $\eta \approx 0.02\text{–}0.05$. - 🧫 **Cell size $\lambda$** is *measured* (soot-track / smoked foil); not predicted analytically. - 🌡️ **Von Neumann spike pressure**: $p_{VN} \approx 2 p_{CJ}$ for strong gas detonations. - 🚧 **Design loading**: blast walls for detonations are rated to $\geq 100$ bar impulsive load. - ⚙️ **Process safety takeaway**: *Prevent* DDT by (a) inerting, (b) eliminating obstructions, (c) limiting pipe length $L < L_{run-up}$, (d) using flame arrestors at every connection. > **Final Examiner's Tip**: In KTU 2024 Scheme, marks are awarded for **labeled sketches + boxed final answers + numerical sanity checks**. Always end a numerical answer with a comparison to experimental data (e.g., "$D_{CJ} = 1{,}930$ m/s vs. experimental 1,800 m/s → 7% error, acceptable"). This shows OBE-aligned **Apply / Analyze (L3–L4)** cognitive depth and fetches the top grade band. ---

topics.title: UVCE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** UVCE (Unconfined Vapour Cloud Explosion) --- # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unconfined Vapour Cloud Explosion (UVCE):** A UVCE is defined as a combustion event in which a large mass of flammable vapor (or mixed vapor–air cloud), formed by the accidental release of a hydrocarbon or hazardous gas into the open, unconfined atmosphere, is ignited and produces a rapidly propagating flame front. The associated **blast wave, overpressure, thermal radiation, and missile generation** cause catastrophic damage to personnel, equipment, and structures in the surrounding process plant. The vapor cloud is termed *unconfined* because, unlike a Confined Vapour Cloud Explosion (CVCE), the flame acceleration is driven only by **turbulence, partial confinement (process equipment, pipe racks, congested regions), and obstacle density**, not by full geometric enclosure. The benchmark historical incident that anchored the study of UVCEs in process safety is the **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK)** – a Nypro caprolactam plant where a 20-inch temporary bypass pipe failed, releasing ~50 tonnes of cyclohexane that ignited after ~45 seconds, causing 28 fatalities. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** A UVCE is NOT the same as a BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion) or a flash fire. In a UVCE, the **overpressure component is the dominant damaging mechanism**, whereas in a BLEVE it is the *pressure vessel rupture + fireball*, and in a flash fire it is the *thermal radiation* with negligible overpressure. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a pressure cooker with a faulty seal. A tiny amount of steam leaks and mingles with the kitchen air. If the room were a small, enclosed kitchen (confined), even a small leak could build up pressure dangerously. Now imagine the same leak in a **vast open cricket stadium at dawn (unconfined)** – the vapor spreads thinly. However, if **thousands of spectators are densely packed in a stand (congestion/obstacle density)**, and a spark occurs, the flame can rip through the crowd at supersonic speeds because the people act as turbulence generators, accelerating the flame. **Translation to process plant:** The "stadium" is the open plant, the "spectators" are the **distillation columns, heat exchangers, pipe racks, structural steel, and tanks**. A massive vapor release (e.g., 100 tonnes of LPG) finds this congestion, ignites, and the flame can accelerate from a few m/s to over **200–300 m/s**, generating destructive blast overpressures. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** UVCE = "Vapor Cloud meets Obstacles + Spark = Blast." The cloud itself is unconfined, but the **flame speed** becomes confined by the **obstacle field**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following numerical values are universally accepted in KTU- and internationally-validated blast calculations: | Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit | |--------|----------|-------|------| | $H_{TNT}$ | Heat of combustion of TNT | **4,684** | kJ/kg | | $P_0$ | Standard atmospheric pressure | **101,325** | Pa | | $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81** | m/s² | | $c_{air}$ | Speed of sound in air (15 °C) | **340.3** | m/s | | $M_{air}$ | Mean molecular mass of air | **28.97** | g/mol | > [!NOTE] > The **empirical yield factor ($\eta$)** for a UVCE typically lies between **1% and 10%**, with **3% to 5%** being the KTU-board-recommended value for a first-pass conservative estimate of *open-air* cloud explosions. In highly congested regions, $\eta$ may be higher. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of UVCE – Five Sequential Phases A UVCE is not a single event; it is a chain of physical and chemical transitions. Understanding each phase is critical because **mitigation strategies are phase-specific**. 1. **Phase I – Release (Source Term):** The pressurized liquefied or gaseous hydrocarbon escapes through a failed flange, ruptured pipe, or opened relief valve. The mass flow rate is governed by orifice equations (subsonic or sonic flow depending on pressure ratio $P_1/P_2$). 2. **Phase II – Dispersion & Vapor Cloud Formation:** The released material forms a denser-than-air or neutrally-buoyant cloud. It is transported by wind, spreads by **atmospheric turbulence (Pasquill–Gifford stability classes)**, and mixes with air until pockets within the cloud fall in the **flammability range** $[\text{LEL}, \text{UEL}]$. 3. **Phase III – Ignition:** An ignition source (static discharge, hot surface, electrical spark) triggers combustion. The flame initially propagates at the **laminar burning velocity** ($S_L \approx 0.4$–$0.5$ m/s for methane, up to 3–4 m/s for higher hydrocarbons). 4. **Phase IV – Flame Acceleration & Blast Generation:** As the flame front encounters process equipment, pipes, and structural obstacles, it generates **turbulence** and **flow-induced instabilities**. This can accelerate the flame to **300+ m/s**, even approaching detonation velocities (~1,800 m/s for hydrocarbon detonations). The rapid energy release creates a **high-pressure blast wave**. 5. **Phase V – Damage Propagation:** The blast wave radiates outward, **overpressure damages structures**, **thermal radiation causes secondary fires**, and **debris becomes missiles**. The damage radius can extend **several hundred meters** for large releases. ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" – Flame Acceleration Logic - **Why does the flame accelerate?** The flame area increases when it encounters an obstacle. The combustion rate (mass/time) = $\rho_u \cdot S_L \cdot A_f$ where $A_f$ is flame area. As $A_f$ grows, energy release rate $\dot{Q}$ grows, raising local pressure. The pressure gradient ahead of the flame further stretches and accelerates it — a positive feedback loop. - **How is the overpressure produced?** A flame moving at velocity $S_f$ into unburned gas at rest generates a pressure jump $\Delta P \approx \rho_u \cdot S_f^2 \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma - 1}{\sigma}\right)$, where $\sigma$ is the **expansion ratio** (typically 6–8 for hydrocarbons in air). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following are the **KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield formulas** tested in board examinations: | # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalence | $W_{TNT}$ in kg, $M$ in kg, $H_c$ in kJ/kg | | 2 | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance (Hopkinson) | $R$ in m, $W_{TNT}$ in kg | | 3 | $Z_s = \dfrac{R}{\left(E / P_0\right)^{1/3}}$ | Sachs scaled distance (TNO) | $E$ in J, $P_0$ in Pa | | 4 | $\dfrac{P_s}{P_0} = f(Z_s)$ | Sachs overpressure correlation | Dimensionless | | 5 | $E = M \cdot H_c$ | Total released combustion energy | $E$ in kJ | | 6 | $\Delta P = \rho_u \cdot S_f^2 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma - 1}{\sigma}$ | Acoustic overpressure estimate | Pa | | 7 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{P_0}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Thermodynamic efficiency | Dimensionless | | 8 | $t_+ = \dfrac{R}{c_{air}} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{P_0}{P_s}\right)^{-1/2}$ | Positive phase duration | seconds | | 9 | $i_s = \dfrac{A \cdot P_s^2 \cdot t_+}{2 \cdot m_{air}}$ | Specific impulse (drag loading) | Pa·s | > [!WARNING] > In **TNT-equivalence** formulas, the yield factor $\eta$ is unit-less but **numerically must be entered as a decimal (e.g., 0.05 for 5%)**, NOT as a percentage. This is a common KTU exam mistake. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Process Safety - **Plant Layout & Siting (CCPS Guidelines):** Determining safe separation distances between units. - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** Calculating individual and societal risk contours. - **Emergency Response Planning:** Defining evacuation zones and shelter-in-place radii. - **Insurance & Loss Prevention:** Estimating Maximum Foreseeable Loss (MFL) for property insurance. - **Regulatory Compliance:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM) and COMAH (UK) require UVCE analysis for any facility handling >10,000 lbs of flammable material. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — TNT Equivalence of a Vapor Cloud **Statement:** Derive the expression for the equivalent mass of TNT that would produce the same blast damage as a given mass of hydrocarbon released in a UVCE. **Step 1 — Define the energy content of the released hydrocarbon.** Let $M$ be the total mass of flammable vapor released (in kg), and $H_c$ be its lower (or net) heat of combustion (in kJ/kg). The total chemical energy available for blast generation is: $$ E_{chem} = M \cdot H_c $$ **Step 2 — Apply the empirical yield factor.** In a real UVCE, only a fraction of the chemical energy is converted to blast (the rest is dissipated as heat, slow deflagration, or vertical lift). The yield factor $\eta$ accounts for this: $$ E_{blast} = \eta \cdot E_{chem} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c $$ **Step 3 — Equate to the energy content of TNT.** The heat of combustion of TNT is a constant $H_{TNT} = 4{,}684$ kJ/kg. For an equivalent TNT mass $W_{TNT}$: $$ W_{TNT} \cdot H_{TNT} = E_{blast} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $W_{TNT}$.** $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}} $$ This is the **fundamental TNT equivalence formula** used in process safety globally. > [!NOTE] > **Why $\eta < 1$?** In a TNT detonation, the energy is released almost instantaneously with high mechanical efficiency ($\eta_{TNT} \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$). In a UVCE, the deflagration is slower and a large fraction of energy goes into heating the unburned gas rather than generating pressure. Hence $\eta$ for UVCE is typically only **0.01 to 0.10**. ## 3.2 Derivation II — Sachs Scaled Overpressure (TNO Multi-Energy) **Statement:** Derive the Sachs scaling law used in the TNO Multi-Energy method. **Step 1 — Define a characteristic length scale.** For an explosion of energy $E$ released in air at ambient pressure $P_0$, the volume of gas that would be at ambient pressure after expansion is $V \propto E / P_0$. A characteristic length is the cube root: $$ L \propto \left(\frac{E}{P_0}\right)^{1/3} $$ **Step 2 — Form a dimensionless scaled distance.** A point at physical distance $R$ from the explosion center has a Sachs scaled distance: $$ Z_s = \frac{R}{\left(E / P_0\right)^{1/3}} $$ **Step 3 — State the Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem result.** The overpressure ratio is a function only of the scaled distance (for ideal gas, constant $\gamma$): $$ \frac{P_s}{P_0} = \Phi(Z_s) $$ where $\Phi$ is determined empirically from laboratory blast tests (e.g., the **TNO Green Book** standard curves). **Step 4 — Verify dimensions.** $\left[E / P_0\right]$ has units of $\text{J} / \text{Pa} = \text{m}^3$, so its cube root is metres. Therefore $Z_s$ is dimensionless — correct. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):** > 12,000 kg of propane is accidentally released from a storage sphere and forms a vapor cloud that ignites. Using the TNT equivalence method, calculate: (i) the equivalent TNT mass, (ii) the scaled distance at $R = 400$ m, and (iii) the peak side-on overpressure (using the standard KTU chart lookup). **Given:** - $M = 12{,}000$ kg - $H_c$ (propane) = 46,000 kJ/kg - $H_{TNT} = 4{,}684$ kJ/kg - $\eta = 0.04$ (4%, typical congested open-air) - $R = 400$ m **Solution:** **Part (i) — Equivalent TNT mass:** $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}} $$ Substituting the values: $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{0.04 \times 12{,}000 \times 46{,}000}{4{,}684} $$ Compute numerator: $$ 0.04 \times 12{,}000 = 480 \quad;\quad 480 \times 46{,}000 = 22{,}080{,}000 \text{ kJ} $$ Divide by $H_{TNT}$: $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{22{,}080{,}000}{4{,}684} \approx 4{,}714 \text{ kg TNT} $$ **Part (ii) — Scaled distance:** $$ Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} $$ Compute the cube root: $$ W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 4{,}714^{1/3} \approx 16.76 \text{ m / kg}^{1/3} $$ Therefore: $$ Z = \frac{400}{16.76} \approx 23.87 \text{ m / kg}^{1/3} $$ **Part (iii) — Overpressure from the KTU standard chart:** Using the standard KTU/CCPS TNT overpressure correlation (Kingery–Bulmash polynomial, simplified form): For $Z \approx 24$, the peak side-on overpressure is: $$ P_s \approx 8{,}000 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.08 \text{ bar} \approx 1.16 \text{ psi} $$ > [!NOTE] > This corresponds to **"Light structural damage"** in standard damage-probability tables — well within the level that can shatter windows, injure personnel, and knock down light partitions. Beyond 600 m, overpressure drops below **0.5 psi**, the threshold for minor injuries. ## 3.4 Damage Criteria Matrix (KTU Standard) | Peak Side-on Overpressure | Effect | |---------------------------|--------| | **0.5 kPa (0.005 bar)** | Annoying noise (~135 dB); window rattle | | **2.0 kPa (0.02 bar)** | Glass cracking; personnel knocked down by surprise | | **7.0 kPa (0.07 bar)** | 10% window breakage; minor injuries from flying glass | | **14 kPa (0.14 bar)** | 50% window breakage; minor structural damage | | **35 kPa (0.35 bar)** | Wooden utility poles snap; severe structural damage | | **70 kPa (0.7 bar)** | Brick walls fail; fatalities common | | **140 kPa (1.4 bar)** | Reinforced concrete damaged; near-total destruction | | **280 kPa (2.8 bar)** | Total destruction of conventional buildings | > [!TIP] > Memorize **3 critical thresholds** for KTU exams: > 1. **0.7 bar** = Heavy damage to structures > 2. **0.1 bar** = Threshold for serious injury / fatality > 3. **0.01 bar** = Threshold for window damage --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 UVCE Event Sequence — Process Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ReleasePhase
High pressure
hydrocarbon leak] --> B[DispersionPhase
Vapor cloud forms
in atmosphere] B --> C[MixingPhase
Cloud concentration
passes through
flammability limits] C --> D[IgnitionPhase
Spark or hot surface
initiates combustion] D --> E[FlameAcceleration
Obstacles in plant
accelerate flame front] E --> F[BlastWaveGeneration
Rapid pressure rise
creates overpressure] F --> G[DamagePhase
Structures fail
Personnel injured
Missiles projected] style A fill:#ffe4b5 style B fill:#ffd1dc style C fill:#b0e0e6 style D fill:#ffb6c1 style E fill:#ffa07a style F fill:#ff7f50 style G fill:#dc143c ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of UVCE Damage Assessment ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputData P1[Mass of release M] P2[Heat of combustion Hc] P3[Yield factor eta] P4[Wind speed and direction] P5[Obstacle density in plant] end subgraph DispersionEngine Q1[Gaussian plume model] Q2[Vapor cloud footprint] end subgraph EnergyEstimation R1[Total chemical energy Echem] R2[Blast energy Eblast] R3[Equivalent TNT mass] end subgraph OverpressureCalculator S1[Scaled distance Z or Zs] S2[Empirical chart lookup] S3[Peak overpressure Ps] end subgraph DamageAssessment T1[Comparison with damage tables] T2[Risk contour mapping] T3[Final design recommendations] end P1 --> R1 P2 --> R1 P1 --> Q1 P2 --> Q1 P4 --> Q1 Q1 --> Q2 Q2 --> T2 R1 --> R2 P3 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 --> S1 P5 --> S2 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> T1 T1 --> T2 T2 --> T3 style InputData fill:#e0f7fa style DispersionEngine fill:#fff9c4 style EnergyEstimation fill:#ffe0b2 style OverpressureCalculator fill:#f8bbd0 style DamageAssessment fill:#c8e6c9 ``` ## 4.3 Comparison of Estimation Methods ```mermaid flowchart TD A[UVCE Blast Estimation
Method Selection] --> B{Is the cloud
truly open-air?} B -->|Yes| C[TNT Equivalence Method
Conservative screening] B -->|No, congested| D{TNO or BST?} D -->|E = constant,
simple geometry| E[TNO Multi-Energy Method
Sachs scaling] D -->|Detailed flame
speed data| F[Baker-Strehlow-Tang Method
3D blast curves] C --> G[Output: Peak overpressure Ps] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Damage contour mapping
Risk-based land use planning] style A fill:#e3f2fd style B fill:#fff59d style C fill:#c8e6c9 style D fill:#fff59d style E fill:#ffccbc style F fill:#ffccbc style G fill:#d1c4e9 style H fill:#b2dfdb ``` ## 4.4 Standard Overpressure vs. Scaled Distance Curve (Mermaid Plot Equivalent) ```mermaid xychart-beta title "Peak Side-on Overpressure vs Sachs Scaled Distance" x-axis "Sachs Scaled Distance Zs" [1, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 300] y-axis "Overpressure Ratio Ps/P0" 0 --> 100 line [80, 25, 12, 4, 1.5, 0.6, 0.3, 0.15] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The overpressure ratio falls **non-linearly** with scaled distance. Doubling the distance does NOT halve the overpressure — it typically reduces it by a factor of 5 to 10 for a TNT-equivalent blast, due to the **cubic geometric spreading** of the shock front. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Define UVCE. List any four factors that influence the severity of a UVCE.** **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** A UVCE (Unconfined Vapour Cloud Explosion) is the combustion of a large, dispersed flammable vapor cloud in the open atmosphere, producing a destructive blast wave and overpressure. **Four factors influencing severity:** 1. **Mass of fuel released** — Larger mass → larger energy → larger blast radius. 2. **Yield factor (η)** — Depends on congestion, confinement, and ignition timing. 3. **Flame speed / obstacle density** — Higher congestion accelerates the flame, raising peak overpressure. 4. **Distance from cloud center** — Overpressure falls with cube-root of energy and inversely with distance. *(Other valid factors: reactivity of fuel, wind speed/direction, atmospheric stability class, ignition delay time.)* ### Question 2 (3 marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Q: Differentiate between the TNT Equivalence method and the TNO Multi-Energy method for estimating UVCE blast effects.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | TNT Equivalence | TNO Multi-Energy | |--------|----------------|------------------| | Underlying principle | Energy equivalent to TNT detonation | Models flame-driven blast in turbulent/congested clouds | | Yield factor $\eta$ | Single empirical value (1–10%) | Varies with cloud strength class (1–10) | | Overpressure correlation | Single universal curve (Hopkinson) | Family of curves for different classes | | Conservatism | Generally **over-conservative** for open-air clouds | **More realistic** for partially confined/congested clouds | | Best use | Quick screening, simple geometry | Detailed QRA, congested plants | ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Choice is given between **Question A** and **Question B**. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — [7 marks] **Q: With the help of a neat schematic, explain the mechanism of a UVCE. Discuss the role of flame acceleration in determining the magnitude of the blast overpressure.** **Model Solution Outline:** 1. **Diagram** (2 marks): Draw a labelled block diagram showing the five phases (Release → Dispersion → Mixing → Ignition → Blast → Damage). Reference the schematic in Section 4.1. 2. **Phase-wise explanation** (3 marks): Briefly describe each phase with its governing physics (orifice flow, Pasquill dispersion, flammability limits, turbulence). 3. **Flame acceleration discussion** (2 marks): - Equation: $\dot{Q} = \rho_u \cdot S_L \cdot A_f \cdot \Delta H_c$ - As flame area $A_f$ increases through obstacles, $\dot{Q}$ rises → local pressure rises → flame stretches → auto-acceleration. - Peak overpressure scales as $S_f^2$ — so even a 10× increase in flame speed can yield **100× overpressure**. #### Part (b) — [7 marks] **Q: A 20,000 kg mass of n-butane is released and forms a UVCE in a moderately congested area. Compute (i) the equivalent TNT mass, (ii) the scaled distance at 600 m, and (iii) the peak overpressure in bar.** **Given:** $M = 20{,}000$ kg, $H_c$ (n-butane) = 45,700 kJ/kg, $H_{TNT} = 4{,}684$ kJ/kg, $\eta = 0.05$ (5%), $R = 600$ m. **Solution:** **(i) Equivalent TNT mass:** $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}} = \frac{0.05 \times 20{,}000 \times 45{,}700}{4{,}684} $$ Compute numerator: $$ 0.05 \times 20{,}000 = 1{,}000 \quad;\quad 1{,}000 \times 45{,}700 = 45{,}700{,}000 \text{ kJ} $$ Therefore: $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{45{,}700{,}000}{4{,}684} \approx 9{,}756 \text{ kg TNT} $$ **(ii) Scaled distance:** $$ W_{TNT}^{1/3} = 9{,}756^{1/3} \approx 21.40 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$ $$ Z = \frac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{600}{21.40} \approx 28.04 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$ **(iii) Peak overpressure:** Using the standard KTU/CCPS Kingery–Bulmash polynomial (or a published chart), for $Z = 28$: $$ P_s \approx 6{,}500 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.065 \text{ bar} \approx 0.94 \text{ psi} $$ **Conclusion:** This overpressure corresponds to **light-to-moderate damage** at 600 m. Beyond 1 km, the overpressure falls below the **0.5 psi minor-injury threshold**. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — [7 marks] **Q: Explain the TNO Multi-Energy method for UVCE blast estimation. How does it overcome the limitations of the TNT Equivalence method?** **Model Solution Outline:** 1. **Concept (3 marks):** The TNO method (van den Berg, 1985) models a UVCE as a *turbulent, hemispherical* blast source. The blast strength is characterized by a class number (1 = weak deflagration, 10 = detonation), selected based on the level of congestion and confinement observed in the plant. 2. **Sachs scaling (2 marks):** Define $Z_s = R / (E/P_0)^{1/3}$ and explain the dimensionless correlation $\Phi(Z_s)$. 3. **Advantages over TNT method (2 marks):** - TNT assumes point-source detonation → over-predicts blast for slow deflagrations. - TNO allows the engineer to select a blast class consistent with local congestion, giving a more realistic pressure profile. - Particularly suitable for open-air, congested process plants. #### Part (b) — [7 marks] **Q: A vapor cloud explosion in a refinery releases $E = 8 \times 10^{11}$ J of blast energy. Using the Sachs scaling method, determine the peak overpressure at 250 m.
