Human error is considered a *root cause* in approximately **70–80 %** of all major process industry accidents worldwide (per industry accident statistics). Classic KTU-case studies — Bhopal (1984), Texas City Refinery (2005), BP Texas City — all reveal human error as a multi-layered chain rather than a single operator's mistake.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Human Error Classification

The **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPOT754 – Module 3)** categorizes human errors into three broad orientations based on the **origin / level at which the error is generated**:

| # | Error Class | Origin / Source of Error | KTU Keyword |
|---|-------------|-------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **People-Oriented Errors** | The individual worker (skills, mind, body) | *Performance-shaping factors of the human* |
| 2 | **Situation-Oriented Errors** | The immediate environment / context around the worker | *Workplace & ambient conditions* |
| 3 | **System-Oriented Errors** | The organization, design, procedures, management | *Latent failures / system design* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three categories and not just "operator mistake"?**
> A single industrial accident rarely has a single human cause. The KTU taxonomy helps engineers and safety managers trace an error **upward** — from the operator → to the supervisor → to the management system. This is the essence of the **Swiss Cheese Model** (Reason, 1990), which KTU expects you to know.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Surgery" Metaphor

Imagine a surgeon performing a heart operation:

- **People-Oriented Error**: The surgeon is fatigued from a 24-hour shift and miscounts the stitches. (The *person* failed.)
- **Situation-Oriented Error**: The operation theatre is freezing cold, the lighting flickers, and the surgical instrument tray is placed on the wrong side — so the surgeon nicks an artery. (The *situation* failed the person.)
- **System-Oriented Error**: The hospital's protocol never specified the *exact* instrument layout; the procurement department bought cheap forceps that slip; the shift roster system forced a sleep-deprived doctor to operate. (The *system* failed.)

In a process plant, a valve mis-operation, a confined-space fatality, or a pump-room fire can almost always be traced to one or a combination of these three layers. The senior process safety engineer's job is to identify **which layer allowed the error to slip through**.

---

## 1.4 Why KTU Asks This Question

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2024 Scheme outcomes for this module expect students to:
> 1. Classify any given industrial incident into the correct human-error class (CO-3, Bloom Level: *Apply*).
> 2. Justify the layered nature of causation (CO-3, Bloom Level: *Analyse*).
> 3. Recommend control measures targeted at the *correct* layer (CO-3, Bloom Level: *Evaluate*).

This is a **high-weightage, high-frequency** topic in the ESE (End Semester Examination) of PEPOT754. Expect a 14-mark full question or a 7-mark sub-part in every KTU exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes

## 2.1 People-Oriented Errors (POE)

People-oriented errors are failures attributable **directly to the individual operator, technician, or engineer** executing the task. They are also called **operator-level errors** or **performance errors**.

### 2.1.1 Sub-Classification of People-Oriented Errors

The KTU syllabus requires the following exhaustive sub-classification:

1. **Slips and Lapses (Execution Failures)**
   - The person *intended* to do the correct thing but the action went wrong.
   - *Slip* = observable action error (e.g., pressing the START button instead of STOP).
   - *Lapse* = memory/cognitive failure that is not externally visible (e.g., forgetting to close a bleed valve before opening a process line).
   - **Cause**: attention lapse, distraction, fatigue, habit interference.

2. **Mistakes (Planning / Decision Failures)**
   - The person *intended* to do the wrong thing, or chose the wrong plan, even though execution was correct.
   - *Rule-based mistake* = wrong rule selected from memory (e.g., applying the startup procedure during shutdown).
   - *Knowledge-based mistake* = novel situation where the operator's mental model is incomplete (e.g., diagnosing a transient pressure surge with no training).
   - **Cause**: inadequate training, poor mental model, time pressure.

3. **Violations (Non-Compliance)**
   - Deliberate deviation from procedure, often for convenience or to "get the job done".
   - *Routine violation* = habitual shortcuts (e.g., not wearing hard hat in a known zone).
   - *Situational violation* = one-off deviation forced by the immediate context (e.g., bypassing a permissives interlock to keep production running).
   - *Optimising violation* = deliberate act for personal benefit or to meet a target.

4. **Physiological and Psychological Factors**
   - Fatigue, sleep deprivation, illness, drugs, alcohol, emotional stress.
   - **Cognitive overload** when too many alarms sound simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Line:**
> *Slips and mistakes together are called **errors**. Violations are intentional and are technically NOT "errors" in the strictest human-factors sense — but KTU 2024 lists them under human errors for engineering-control purposes.*

### 2.1.2 Engineering Control Measures for People-Oriented Errors

- Comprehensive **Training & Re-certification** (typically every 2 years in process plants).
- **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** design — colour-coded, fail-safe push buttons.
- **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** before non-routine tasks.
- **Behavioural-Based Safety (BBS)** observation programmes.

---

## 2.2 Situation-Oriented Errors (SOE)

Situation-oriented errors arise from the **physical, environmental, or task-context** conditions surrounding the worker. The *person* is competent and *intended* to act correctly, but the *situation* made correct action impossible or improbable.

### 2.2.1 Sub-Classification of Situation-Oriented Errors

1. **Physical Environment Factors**
   - **Lighting** — poor visibility leads to wrong valve operation. A KTU textbook example is misreading a 1/2-inch valve as 2-inch under low light.
   - **Noise** — alarms masked, verbal communication impossible. Threshold of safe communication is **≤ 85 dB**.
   - **Temperature & Humidity** — heat stress reduces cognitive performance; cold reduces dexterity.
   - **Vibration** — affects gauge reading and fine motor control.
   - **Vapours, dust, fumes** — sensory irritation, dizziness.

2. **Ergonomic / Workspace Design Factors**
   - Valve handles identical in shape and colour → wrong valve opened (a classic **Three Mile Island** contributing factor).
   - Control panel layout that violates **population stereotypes** (e.g., emergency stop on the LEFT instead of RIGHT in a culture that expects it on the right).
   - Insufficient workspace → operator takes improvised shortcuts.

3. **Task / Workload Factors**
   - **Cognitive overload** during plant upset — multiple simultaneous alarms.
   - **Monotony / under-stimulation** during long steady-state operation → vigilance loss.
   - **Time pressure** due to production deadlines.

4. **Procedural / Documentation Factors (Operating Level)**
   - Outdated Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
   - Ambiguous or multi-language procedures in a multi-lingual workforce.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguishing KTU Test Question:**
> *If you can fix the error by training the operator, it is People-Oriented.*
> *If you can fix the error by changing the plant, equipment, or workplace, it is Situation-Oriented.*

### 2.2.2 Engineering Control Measures for Situation-Oriented Errors

- **Engineering design improvements** — better lighting, noise insulation, vibration dampers.
- **Ergonomic redesign** — colour-coded valves by service, spatially separated critical valves, sight-glasses at operator eye-level.
- **Alarm rationalisation & Management of Change (MOC)** — to reduce alarm floods.
- **Standardisation of control rooms** using **ISA-101** guidelines.

---

## 2.3 System-Oriented Errors (SYSOE)

System-oriented errors (also called **organisational errors** or **latent failures**) are the deepest, most upstream, and most preventable layer of human error. They originate in **management decisions, organisational culture, design philosophy, regulatory frameworks, and supervision**.

### 2.3.1 Sub-Classification of System-Oriented Errors

1. **Management & Organisational Factors**
   - Production-vs-safety trade-off imposed by management.
   - Inadequate staffing, poor shift rotation.
   - Cost-cutting on maintenance, training, and protective equipment.
   - **Lack of Process Safety Management (PSM)** culture.

2. **Design and Engineering Errors**
   - Inherently unsafe plant layout (e.g., control room too close to hydrocarbon storage).
   - Inadequate **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** review.
   - Failure to apply **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** principles — minimisation, substitution, moderation, simplification.
   - Insufficient **safety instrumented functions (SIFs)** or wrong **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.

3. **Procedural / Supervisory Errors**
   - Missing or poorly written SOPs.
   - Inadequate Permit-to-Work (PTW) system.
   - Poor handover between shifts.
   - **Inadequate Management of Change (MOC)** — process modification without re-hazard analysis.

4. **Communication and Cultural Errors**
   - Vertical communication failure (operator → supervisor → manager).
   - Language barriers in multi-cultural workforce.
   - "Blame culture" preventing near-miss reporting.
   - Lack of safety leadership commitment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Anchor — James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model:**
> In every accident, the *active failure* (people-oriented) penetrates several barriers. Each barrier has a *latent hole* (system-oriented error). The accident occurs when all holes align.
> KTU expects you to draw and explain this model in any 14-mark question on human error.

### 2.3.2 Engineering Control Measures for System-Oriented Errors

- Implementation of **PSM (OSHA 1910.119)** and **Process Safety Code** (India — **PSC 2020** under OISD).
- Strong **safety culture** based on **Sutton's safety culture ladder** (pathological → calculative → proactive → generative).
- Independent **Process Safety Audit** programmes.
- Robust **MOC** and **PSI (Pre-Startup Safety Review)** systems.
- Adoption of **DuPont, ABB, and Shell** safety-management frameworks.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important** revision artefact for KTU board exams. Memorise the mapping, not the exact words.

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Error Class} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Cause Layer} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Control Layer}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

| Error Class | Cause Layer | Control Layer | KTU Memory Hook |
|-------------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **People-Oriented** | Individual (operator's mind & body) | Training, supervision, BBS | *"He made the mistake"* |
| **Situation-Oriented** | Workplace (physical, ergonomic, task) | Engineering design, ergonomics | *"The place made him do it"* |
| **System-Oriented** | Organisation (management, design, culture) | PSM, MOC, audits, safety culture | *"The company let it happen"* |

**Quantitative metric for human error (KTU often asks):**

$$
\text{Human Error Rate (HER)} = \frac{\text{Number of unsafe acts performed}}{\text{Total number of opportunities for error}}
$$

Units: dimensionless (fraction) or per-million opportunities (e.g., **THERP** method in CCPS guidelines).

**THERP (Technique for Human Error Rate Prediction) baseline values** (per KTU-recommended Swain & Guttmann handbook):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Skill-based error rate} &\approx 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ to } 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per opportunity} \\
\text{Rule-based error rate} &\approx 1 \times 10^{-2} \text{ to } 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per opportunity} \\
\text{Knowledge-based error rate} &\approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.1 \text{ per opportunity}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not state that *violations* have an error rate — violations are intentional, not error-rate based. The above rates apply only to **errors**, not violations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Classification Framework

## 3.1 Master Classification Matrix (KTU Board-Exam Ready)

This is the **single analytical tool** you will use to answer any KTU question on human error. Use it as a step-by-step decision tree.

### Step 1 — Identify the **Type of Action** that failed

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Did the person do the WRONG thing?} &\rightarrow \text{Action Failure} \\
\text{Did the person do the RIGHT thing BADLY?} &\rightarrow \text{Execution Failure} \\
\text{Did the person FAIL to do the required thing?} &\rightarrow \text{Omission Failure}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the **Source / Layer** that caused the failure

Apply the **Layered Causation Model (LCM)** used in KTU:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If cause is in the individual's training, mind or body} &\rightarrow \textbf{People-Oriented Error} \\
\text{If cause is in the workplace, environment or ergonomics} &\rightarrow \textbf{Situation-Oriented Error} \\
\text{If cause is in management, design, procedure or culture} &\rightarrow \textbf{System-Oriented Error}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Map to **Corrective Control**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{People-Oriented} &\rightarrow \text{Train, Re-train, Re-certify} \\
\text{Situation-Oriented} &\rightarrow \text{Redesign equipment / workplace} \\
\text{System-Oriented} &\rightarrow \text{Change management, PSM, MOC, Culture}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Worked Examples (KTU-Style)

### Example 1 — *Operator opens the wrong valve during a unit startup*

**Step 1: Identify Action Type**
- Operator intended to open the **reactor feed valve (FV-101)** but actually opened the **steam header isolation valve (FV-105)**.
- He *did the wrong thing* → **Action Failure / Slip**.

**Step 2: Identify Source Layer**
- Both valves were located adjacent to each other on the same manifold.
- The handles were of **identical shape, identical size, identical colour** (both green).
- There was **no nameplate or tag identifier** readable from the operator's stance.
- Lighting in that section of the plant was below the **300 lux** recommended for valve operations.
- *The competent operator was misled by the physical layout.* → **Situation-Oriented Error** (root cause is the **design of the workplace**).

**Step 3: Corrective Control**
- Apply **colour-coding by service** (API 570 / ANSI guidelines).
- Install **rotary-lock / latched handles** for critical service.
- Improve **lighting to 500 lux** in operational areas.
- Although *some* training reinforcement helps, the root cause is **not** a training gap. → Situation-Oriented error, not People-Oriented.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A question saying *"the operator was not properly trained"* forces you to answer **People-Oriented**. A question saying *"valves were identical, poorly lit, and had no tags"* forces **Situation-Oriented**. The keywords in the question word the layer for you.

---

### Example 2 — *Confined space asphyxiation fatality*

**Step 1: Identify Action Type**
- A maintenance worker entered a vessel without a valid **Confined Space Entry Permit**.
- He intended to inspect the interior but did not test the atmosphere, did not wear a harness, did not have an attendant.
- *He failed to apply a known procedure* → **Violation (situational)**.

**Step 2: Identify Source Layer**
- Investigation reveals:
  - The **supervisor** had issued only a verbal instruction, no written permit.
  - The **plant manager** had reduced the safety-officer strength from 3 to 1, so the safety-officer could not physically cover all confined-space jobs.
  - The **company PTW (Permit-to-Work) system** was outdated and did not match the OISD-105 standard.
  - There was no **Management of Change** when the vessel was reclassified from storage to process.
- *Root causes lie in the management system, supervision, and procedure design.* → **System-Oriented Error**.

**Step 3: Corrective Control**
- Revise and enforce PTW system.
- Deploy adequate **safety-officer manpower**.
- Conduct **MOC** for process reclassification.
- Institute **PSM audits** and **BBS**.

---

### Example 3 — *Reactor runaway with thermal explosion*

**Step 1: Identify Action Type**
- Operator added **excess catalyst** to the reactor despite an SOP saying "add 5 kg maximum".
- This is a **rule-based mistake** — wrong rule applied.

**Step 2: Identify Source Layer**
- Operator is **highly experienced (15 years)**, no fatigue, no drug issues, certified.
- The **reactor control panel** had the temperature and pressure alarms clustered and the catalyst-charge display hidden behind a maintenance tag.
- The **SOP for catalyst charging** was last revised in 2014, was in English only, but the operator's first language was Hindi.
- The **HAZOP** study had not been refreshed in 6 years.
- *Root causes are the panel design, language barrier, outdated HAZOP, and supervisory reviews.* → A **blend of Situation-Oriented (panel design) and System-Oriented (HAZOP, SOP revision, language policy)**.

**Step 3: Corrective Control**
- Redesign HMI (Situation-Oriented fix).
- Implement multi-lingual SOPs (System fix).
- Mandate **5-yearly HAZOP revalidation** (System fix).
- Install **independent high-temperature interlock (SIF with SIL-2)** (System-level engineering fix).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Ready-to-Draw in Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-marked table** in the KTU ESE. Reproduce it verbatim in your answer sheet.

| Parameter | People-Oriented | Situation-Oriented | System-Oriented |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| Synonym (KTU) | Operator error | Workplace error | Organisational / Latent error |
| Layer in Swiss Cheese | Active failure (sharp end) | Local conditions | Latent failure (blunt end) |
| Direct actor | Individual operator | Immediate environment | Management / Design team |
| Time of origin | Just before the event | Just before the event | Months/years before the event |
| Visibility | Highly visible | Moderately visible | Hidden / latent |
| Frequency in accidents | High | High | Very High (root) |
| Typical control | Training, supervision | Engineering design, ergonomics | PSM, MOC, culture, audits |
| Example | Operator mis-reads gauge | Poor lighting hides gauge | SOP never specified gauge reading |
| KTU textbook example | Bhopal operator error | Three Mile Island valve confusion | BP Texas City — cost-cut management |
| KTU recovery time | Hours | Days | Months/Years |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Hierarchy of Human Error (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete hierarchy of human error classifications** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 scheme. Each branch represents one of the three orientations, with all sub-classifications expanded for exam-readiness.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HUMAN ERROR IN PROCESS PLANTS] --> B[PEOPLE ORIENTED ERRORS]
    A --> C[SITUATION ORIENTED ERRORS]
    A --> D[SYSTEM ORIENTED ERRORS]

    B --> B1[Slips and Lapses]
    B --> B2[Mistakes Rule Based and Knowledge Based]
    B --> B3[Violations Routine Situational Optimising]
    B --> B4[Physiological Factors]
    B --> B5[Psychological Factors]

    C --> C1[Physical Environment Lighting Noise Temperature]
    C --> C2[Ergonomic Design Valve Layout Panel HMI]
    C --> C3[Task Workload Cognitive Overload Monotony]
    C --> C4[Documentation SOP Issues Language Ambiguity]

    D --> D1[Management and Organisational Factors]
    D --> D2[Design and Engineering Errors HAZOP SIL ISD]
    D --> D3[Procedural and Supervisory Errors PTW MOC Handover]
    D --> D4[Communication and Culture Blame Culture Language]

    B1 --> F1[CONTROL Training and Re Certification]
    B2 --> F1
    B3 --> F1
    C1 --> F2[CONTROL Engineering Redesign and Ergonomics]
    C2 --> F2
    C3 --> F2
    C4 --> F2
    D1 --> F3[CONTROL PSM MOC Safety Culture Audits]
    D2 --> F3
    D3 --> F3
    D4 --> F3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram (for your exam answer):**
> - The **top node (A)** is the global human-error concept.
> - The **three middle nodes (B, C, D)** are the three KTU classes.
> - The **bottom three nodes (F1, F2, F3)** are the engineering-control measures corresponding to each class.
> - **KTU-marker love:** When you draw this diagram, the *control-measure* column automatically signals the right *layer of fix* — a hallmark of full-mark answers.

---

## 4.2 The Swiss Cheese Model — Why All Three Layers Matter

The following diagram maps the **three KTU error classes onto James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model**. This is the single most-marked diagram in KTU's Module 3.

```mermaid
graph TD
    HAZ[HAZARD e.g. Hydrocarbon Release] --> S1[SLICE 1 Organisational Decisions SYSTEM ORIENTED]
    S1 --> S2[SLICE 2 Supervisory and Procedural Safeguards SYSTEM ORIENTED]
    S2 --> S3[SLICE 3 Engineering and Ergonomic Conditions SITUATION ORIENTED]
    S3 --> S4[SLICE 4 Operator Behaviour PEOPLE ORIENTED]
    S4 --> ACC[PROCESS ACCIDENT]

    S1 -. Hole: Poor PSM .-> S2
    S2 -. Hole: Weak PTW .-> S3
    S3 -. Hole: Poor Lighting .-> S4
    S4 -. Hole: Operator Slip .-> ACC

    style HAZ fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style ACC fill:#990000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Translation Table (use this in your answer):**

| Swiss Cheese Slice | Maps to KTU Error Class | Example Hole |
|--------------------|-------------------------|--------------|
| Slice 1 — Organisational | System-Oriented | No PSM culture |
| Slice 2 — Supervisory | System-Oriented | Bypassed PTW |
| Slice 3 — Engineering / Ergonomic | Situation-Oriented | Bad valve labelling |
| Slice 4 — Operator | People-Oriented | Slip / mistake / violation |

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow of an Industrial Incident

The following diagram shows the **functional flow** of a real industrial accident through the three KTU layers. Use it whenever a question asks *"Trace the root cause of this incident through the three layers."*

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[PROCESS PLANT] --> Q[Workplace Conditions SITUATION ORIENTED]
    Q --> R[Operator Action PEOPLE ORIENTED]
    R --> S[Incident Occurs Near Miss or Accident]
    S --> T[Investigation]
    T --> U[Root Cause in Management System SYSTEM ORIENTED]
    U --> V[Corrective Action Plan]
    V --> P

    P -. Hidden.-> U
    style P fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style U fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300
    style S fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The accident appears to begin at the operator (R), but the *true origin* is the *management system* (U) which designed the procedures, trained (or failed to train) the operator, and provided (or failed to provide) the workplace. The dashed line `P -. Hidden .-> U` is the **latent failure pathway** that KTU expects you to identify.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEPOT754]**  
> *Define human error. List any two classifications of human error as per the KTU 2024 scheme.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Human error is the failure of a person to perform a task within the limits of acceptable performance, where the failure is attributable to the human rather than to a hardware malfunction. As per the KTU 2024 scheme, human errors are classified into:
1. People-Oriented Errors (operator-level)
2. Situation-Oriented Errors (workplace-level)
3. System-Oriented Errors (organisational-level)

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the three classes.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEPOT754]**  
> *Distinguish between a "slip" and a "mistake" with one process-plant example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Slip | Mistake |
|--------|------|---------|
| Definition | Action error — correct intention, wrong execution | Planning error — wrong intention itself |
| Cognitive level | Skill-based | Rule-based / Knowledge-based |
| Example (KTU) | Operator presses START instead of STOP because buttons are adjacent | Operator charges catalyst at 8 kg instead of 5 kg because the SOP is outdated |

*(2 marks for distinction table, 1 mark for one example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEPOT754, 14 marks]**  
> *Explain the three classifications of human error — People-Oriented, Situation-Oriented, and System-Oriented — with two industrial examples for each. State the engineering control measures for each class.*

**Model Answer Outline (14 marks = 7 + 7):**

**Part (i) — Definitions and Characteristics (7 marks):**

1. **People-Oriented Errors** (2 marks)  
   - Errors attributable directly to the individual operator.  
   - Sub-classes: slips, lapses, mistakes (rule-based, knowledge-based), violations, physiological / psychological factors.  
   - Example 1: An operator forgets to open a bleed valve before pressuring a line (lapse).  
   - Example 2: A control-room operator misinterprets an alarm flood and presses the wrong shut-down button (mistake).

2. **Situation-Oriented Errors** (2 marks)  
   - Errors arising from the immediate workplace, environment, or ergonomic conditions.  
   - Sub-classes: physical environment, ergonomic design, task workload, documentation.  
   - Example 1: Three Mile Island — ambiguous valve labelling in a poorly-lit auxiliary building led to the wrong valve operation.  
   - Example 2: High noise (> 90 dB) in a compressor room caused a technician to miss a verbal warning.

3. **System-Oriented Errors** (2 marks)  
   - Errors embedded in management decisions, design philosophy, organisational culture, and procedural framework.  
   - Sub-classes: management, design, procedural, communication/cultural.  
   - Example 1: Bhopal 1984 — inadequate MOC, poor maintenance budget, weak PSM culture.  
   - Example 2: BP Texas City 2005 — cost-cutting on instrumentation and supervisor staffing.

4. **Interconnection statement (1 mark):**  
   These three classes are not independent; they interlock through the **Swiss Cheese Model** where active failures (people) align with latent failures (system) and local conditions (situation).

**Part (ii) — Engineering Control Measures (7 marks):**

| Error Class | Control Measures (with brief reasoning) |
|-------------|------------------------------------------|
| People-Oriented | Regular training & re-certification (2-yearly), Job Safety Analysis, Behavioural-Based Safety (BBS), competency matrix. |
| Situation-Oriented | Ergonomic redesign of valves and panels (colour-coding, separation), lighting upgrade to 500 lux, alarm rationalisation, ISA-101 HMI standards. |
| System-Oriented | Implementation of PSM (OSHA 1910.119 / OISD-PSC 2020), Management of Change (MOC), Permit-to-Work, HAZOP revalidation, safety culture transformation (Sutton's ladder), independent Process Safety Audits. |

---

> **Q3(b). [Internal Choice — Alternative 14-mark question]**  
> *With the help of the Swiss Cheese Model, explain how the three classes of human error combine to cause a process-plant accident. Take any one real industrial accident and analyse it layer by layer.*

**Model Answer Outline (14 marks = 7 + 7):**

**Part (i) — Swiss Cheese Model Theory (7 marks):**

1. Draw the **Swiss Cheese diagram** with 4 slices (use the Mermaid equivalent in your answer sheet). (2 marks)
2. Map each slice to a KTU error class: Slice 1 (Org) → System, Slice 2 (Supervisory) → System, Slice 3 (Engineering/Ergonomic) → Situation, Slice 4 (Operator) → People. (2 marks)
3. Explain that accidents occur when the **holes in all slices align**, allowing the hazard to pass through every defence. (2 marks)
4. State that the **deeper the system-oriented hole**, the higher the risk because it is *latent* and *invisible* until an active failure exposes it. (1 mark)

**Part (ii) — Real-Accident Case Study: BP Texas City Refinery Explosion, 23 March 2005 (7 marks):**

| Swiss Cheese Slice | KTU Layer | Real Defect at BP Texas City |
|--------------------|-----------|------------------------------|
| Slice 1 — Organisational | System-Oriented | Cost-cutting on instrumentation and maintenance; under-staffed safety team. |
| Slice 2 — Supervisory / Procedural | System-Oriented | Inadequate MOC when the raffinate splitter was re-classified; supervisor ignored previous near-miss reports. |
| Slice 3 — Engineering / Ergonomic | Situation-Oriented | Critical level-control instrumentation was bypassed; control-room alarm flood during start-up. |
| Slice 4 — Operator | People-Oriented | Operator over-filled the raffinate splitter tower (rule-based mistake). |

The explosion killed **15 workers, injured 180**, caused losses exceeding **$1.5 billion**, and became a textbook study of how **system-oriented errors** created the conditions for the **situation-oriented and people-oriented errors** to converge. (2 marks for conclusion / impact.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Pitfall Callouts

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU examiners commonly deduct marks on this topic:**

1. **Conflating *violations* with *errors*.** A violation is intentional, an error is unintentional. Mixing the two in a single answer will lose **at least 1 mark** per occurrence.
2. **Confusing Situation- and System-Oriented errors.** If a question describes a "managerial decision" (cost cut, under-staffing), the answer is *System* — not *Situation*. A situation is the *physical* workplace, not the *organisational* workplace.
3. **Recommending "training" as the fix for a design problem.** If the *valves are identical and poorly lit*, the answer is **engineering redesign**, not training. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks when the root-cause-control mismatch occurs.
4. **Forgetting the Swiss Cheese Model.** A complete answer to any 14-mark question on human error must mention the Swiss Cheese Model. Omitting it costs **2–3 marks**.
5. **Using absolute value `|x|` inside markdown tables.** Although this is a formatting error rather than a content one, students using KTU digital answer scripts that auto-parse markdown lose marks when the pipe symbol breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `absolute value` instead.
6. **Single-layer answer.** A 14-mark question requires a **multi-layer analysis**; an answer that lists only one class of error is incomplete. Examiners expect a comparison table.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Memorise this list — it is the KTU board's "minimum bar" for a 100 % answer on Module 3 — Human Errors.**

- [x] **Human Error Definition** — KTU 2024 wording: *failure to perform within acceptable performance limits, attributable to the human.*
- [x] **Three classes:** People / Situation / System.
- [x] **People-Oriented sub-classes:** Slips, Lapses, Mistakes (rule-based, knowledge-based), Violations, Physiological & Psychological factors.
- [x] **Situation-Oriented sub-classes:** Physical environment, Ergonomic design, Task workload, Documentation.
- [x] **System-Oriented sub-classes:** Management/Organisational, Design/Engineering, Procedural/Supervisory, Communication/Culture.
- [x] **Swiss Cheese Model** — 4 slices, latent vs active failures, Reason (1990).
- [x] **THERP Human Error Rates** — Skill $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$, Rule $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$, Knowledge $0.1$ to $0.5$ per opportunity.
- [x] **HER Formula:** HER = unsafe acts / total opportunities.
- [x] **Key engineering controls per layer:** Training (People), Ergonomic redesign (Situation), PSM + MOC + Culture (System).
- [x] **Real accidents to quote:** Bhopal 1984, Three Mile Island 1979, BP Texas City 2005, Texas City 2005, Flixborough 1974.
- [x] **Standards to mention:** OSHA 1910.119 (PSM), OISD-PSC 2020, ISA-101 (HMI), CCPS guidelines, ANSI / API 570, OISD-105 (PTW), Sutton's Safety Culture Ladder.
- [x] **KTU Golden Rule:** *The deeper the layer, the higher the prevention leverage. Always recommend the deepest-layer control first.*

> **End of KTU-Premier-Engine V10 Module 3 — Human Errors Notes.**
> **Course:** PEPOT754 — Safety Engineering of Process Plants | **Module:** 3 | **2024 Scheme.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HAZOP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HAZOP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a **structured, systematic, and rigorous qualitative risk analysis technique** used to identify potential process hazards and operability problems throughout the design, construction, operation, and modification stages of a process plant. It was originally developed in the **1970s by the Chemical Industries Association (CIA)** of the United Kingdom, primarily to address safety gaps identified during major industrial accidents in the chemical process sector.

The technique applies a **set of standardized "Guide Words"** to process parameters (like flow, pressure, temperature) at carefully chosen "study nodes" within a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). Each Guide Word creates a **deviation** from the intended design intent, and the HAZOP team then evaluates whether this deviation could lead to a hazard or operability problem and proposes protective or mitigating safeguards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Exam Vocabulary:** HAZOP is fundamentally a **brainstorming technique facilitated by a multidisciplinary team**, not a checklist or computational tool. It is the most widely used Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) method in the chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, and pharmaceutical industries worldwide.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are planning a long road trip from Kerala to Delhi. Before starting, you don't just check the tire pressure — you sit down and ask **"what if...?"** questions about every possible thing that could go wrong on the journey:

- *"What if the fuel runs out?"*
- *"What if the engine overheats?"*
- *"What if a tyre bursts on the highway?"*
- *"What if there's heavy rain?"*

For each scenario, you identify the **consequence**, check whether you already have a **safeguard**, and if not, recommend an **action**.

**HAZOP works exactly this way, but on a chemical plant.**

Instead of a car trip, you study a **P&ID line by line**. Instead of common sense questions, you use a **standard set of "Guide Words"** (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) combined with **process parameters** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level). Each combination produces a "deviation" (e.g., **NO FLOW**, **HIGH PRESSURE**). The team then brainstorms consequences and recommends safeguards.

| Everyday Trip Planning | Industrial HAZOP |
| :--- | :--- |
| Road map | Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) |
| "What if?" questions | Guide Words + Parameters |
| Identifying dangers | Hazard Identification |
| Backup plan | Safeguard / Recommendation |
| Group of friends advising | Multidisciplinary Team |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Think of HAZOP as **"Hazard + OP-erability"** — it identifies **both safety hazards AND operability issues** (like poor product quality, equipment damage, environmental releases).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in HAZOP

Although HAZOP is **qualitative**, the following standardized elements are universally applied:

- **Standard Guide Words:** 7 core guide words recognized globally (IEC 61882).
- **Study Node:** Typically a section of pipe containing 1 major process item (pump, vessel, heat exchanger, column).
- **Team Size:** Typically **5 to 8 members** (IEC 61882 recommends 4–10).
- **Study Duration:** Average of **1 to 4 hours per node**, depending on complexity.
- **Documentation:** Standard HAZOP worksheet columns — *Node, Intention, Deviation, Causes, Consequences, Safeguards, Actions, Responsibility*.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Positioning

This topic directly maps to the following Course Outcomes:

- **CO4:** Apply various hazard identification and risk assessment methodologies in process industries.
- **CO5:** Evaluate the suitability of safety review procedures for chemical process design and operation.

The expected cognitive level per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** is **Apply / Analyze (Level 3–4)**, with marks distributed across Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) as per KTU 2024 regulations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Foundational Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

Before applying HAZOP, the student must internalize the following **six foundational terms**. These appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Node:** A specific point or section in the process where process parameters are evaluated for deviations. A node is usually selected at the **inlet/outlet of a major equipment item** (vessel, pump, heat exchanger, column).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **2. Process Intention:** The **designer's expected operating condition** at a node — for example, *"transfer liquid hexane from Pump P-101 to Tank T-102 at a flow rate of 50 m³/h and pressure of 5 bar."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3. Guide Words:** A **predefined set of simple words** (NO, MORE, LESS, etc.) used in combination with process parameters to generate deviations. They act as a **"trigger"** for brainstorming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4. Parameter:** A **physical or chemical property** of the process (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Concentration, etc.) to which a guide word is applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5. Deviation:** The **departure from the process intention**, formed by combining a guide word with a parameter (e.g., *NO FLOW, HIGH TEMPERATURE*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **6. Safeguard:** An **existing protective measure** that prevents the cause, detects the deviation, or mitigates the consequence — for example, a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV), an alarm, an interlock, or a procedural check.

---

## 2.2 The HAZOP Guide Words — Definitive List

The **IEC 61882 international standard** for HAZOP recognizes the following **7 base guide words** plus utility-specific extensions:

| Guide Word | Meaning | Engineering Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **NO (NOT)** | Complete negation of the design intent | No flow, no pressure, no level |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | More flow, higher pressure, higher temperature |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Less flow, lower pressure, lower temperature |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite of intent | Reverse flow, reverse rotation |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity occurring | Two phases present, contamination |
| **PART OF** | Only part of the intent achieved | Partial composition, partial flow path |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | Wrong material, wrong phase |

**Extended Guide Words for Specific Applications:**

| Extended Guide Word | Application Context |
| :--- | :--- |
| **WHERE ELSE** | Multiple locations of deviation |
| **WHEN / HOW OFTEN** | Time / frequency related deviations |
| **BEFORE / AFTER** | Sequence-related deviations (Batch processes) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common Part A question is *"List the guide words used in HAZOP and explain any three with examples."* Memorize the table above verbatim.

---

## 2.3 Process Parameters Applied in HAZOP

| Parameter | Common Deviations Generated | Typical Process Concern |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Flow** | No Flow, Less Flow, More Flow, Reverse Flow | Pump failure, blocked line, valve closed |
| **Pressure** | High Pressure, Low Pressure, Vacuum | Overpressure, pump dead-heading, column collapse |
| **Temperature** | High Temperature, Low Temperature | Runaway reaction, freezing, material degradation |
| **Level** | High Level, Low Level, No Level | Vessel overflow, pump cavitation, dry running |
| **Concentration / Composition** | High/Low Concentration, Wrong Composition | Wrong mixing ratio, contamination |
| **Phase** | Two Phase, Vapor in Liquid Line | Cavitation, water hammer, slug flow |
| **Reaction / Mixing** | No Reaction, Too Much Reaction, Reverse Reaction | Reactor runaway, unreacted feed buildup |
| **Time / Sequence** | Too Soon, Too Late, Out of Order | Batch sequencing errors |
| **Start / Stop / Maintenance** | Failure to Start/Stop | Equipment damage, lost production |

---

## 2.4 HAZOP Methodology — Step-by-Step Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> HAZOP follows the **"What-If" + Guide Word + Parameter** logic. The **6-step procedure** below is the international standard adopted by KTU's 2024 scheme syllabus.

### Step 1 — Define Scope and Objectives
- Decide the boundaries of the study (entire plant / unit / specific section).
- Identify the phase of project life: design / operation / modification.
- Define physical, environmental, and operational limits.

### Step 2 — Assemble the Multidisciplinary Team

> [!TIP]
> **A good HAZOP team is small, senior, and cross-functional.** A solo HAZOP is **invalid** by IEC standards.

| Role | Typical Function | KTU-Exam Responsibility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Team Leader / Facilitator** | Experienced HAZOP leader | Guides discussion, ensures coverage |
| **Process Engineer** | Process design expert | Explains process intent and P&IDs |
| **Control/Instrumentation Engineer** | Controls and interlocks | Identifies instrument-based safeguards |
| **Operations / Plant Engineer** | Operating experience | Identifies practical operability issues |
| **Mechanical / Equipment Engineer** | Equipment integrity | Highlights equipment-based failures |
| **Safety Engineer** | Risk perspective | Ranks consequences and safeguards |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Documents the session | Fills the HAZOP worksheet |

### Step 3 — Obtain and Review Information
- **Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)**
- **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)**
- **Heat and Material Balance**
- **Cause & Effect (C&E) Matrix**
- **Operating Procedures**
- **Equipment Datasheets**
- **Layout Drawings**
- **Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS/SDS)**

### Step 4 — Divide the Plant into Study Nodes
- Nodes are typically defined at the **inlet and outlet of major equipment** (pumps, vessels, heat exchangers, columns).
- The plant is **physically divided** into sections like *Feed Section, Reaction Section, Separation Section*.

### Step 5 — Apply Guide Words and Analyze Each Node
For each node, every guide word is applied to every parameter, producing a deviation. For each deviation, the team identifies:
- **Causes** (what could make this happen?)
- **Consequences** (what happens if it does?)
- **Existing Safeguards** (what protects us now?)
- **Recommendations / Actions** (what should we add?)

### Step 6 — Documentation, Follow-Up, and Closure
- Complete the **HAZOP Worksheet** (mandatory deliverable).
- Assign each action a unique number, owner, and target date.
- Maintain an **Action Tracking Register**.
- Re-validate recommendations before implementation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical HAZOP Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Oil & Gas Refineries | Hydrocracker reactor loops, distillation columns |
| Petrochemicals | Ethylene cracking furnaces, polymerization reactors |
| Pharmaceutical | Batch reactors, solvent recovery, CIP systems |
| Power Plants | Boiler feedwater systems, condensate lines |
| Pulp & Paper | Digesters, chemical recovery boilers |
| Food Processing | Pasteurization, sterilization skids |

> [!WARNING]
> **Legal Requirement:** HAZOP is **legally mandated** in many countries (e.g., UK COMAH regulations, EU Seveso Directive) for handling hazardous materials above threshold quantities. In India, the **Factories Act, Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC), 1989**, implicitly requires HAZOP through the "On-Site Emergency Plan" and "Safety Audit" provisions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrices & Worked Examples

## 3.1 The HAZOP Deviation Generation Matrix — Analytical Derivation

The number of unique deviations studied at a single node is governed by the **Cartesian product** of Guide Words and Parameters. This is a formal combinatorial result, not an approximation.

$$
N_{\text{deviations}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} G_{i} \, \otimes \, P_{j}
$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{deviations}} &= \text{Total number of deviations analyzed at a node} \\
G_{i} &= \text{The } i\text{-th Guide Word from the standard list} \\
P_{j} &= \text{The } j\text{-th Process Parameter at the node} \\
\otimes &= \text{Combinatorial pairing (Cartesian product) operator} \\
n &= \text{Number of applicable guide words (typically 7 base)} \\
m &= \text{Number of applicable parameters at that node}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Derivation — Standard Case

For a **typical centrifugal pump suction line node**, the applicable process parameters are *Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level*. The standard 7 Guide Words apply.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad n &= 7 \quad (\text{Guide Words: NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN}) \\
m &= 4 \quad (\text{Parameters: Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{deviations}} &= \sum_{i=1}^{7} \sum_{j=1}^{4} G_{i} \otimes P_{j} \\
&= 7 \times 4 \\
&= 28 \text{ deviations per node}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Exam:** A simple centrifugal pump suction line generates **28 distinct deviation scenarios**. Each deviation must be individually evaluated for causes, consequences, safeguards, and actions. This is why HAZOP studies on large refineries often run for **several weeks** with multiple nodes.

### Extended Case — Distillation Column Node

A distillation column section involves parameters: *Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase* (6 parameters).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{deviations}} &= 7 \times 6 \\
&= 42 \text{ deviations per node}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Plant-Wide Estimation

If a refinery has $k$ nodes, total deviations studied:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = k \times N_{\text{deviations per node}}
$$

For $k = 200$ nodes with average $N = 30$ deviations per node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= 200 \times 30 \\
&= 6{,}000 \text{ deviation analyses}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Risk Ranking Within HAZOP — The Severity × Likelihood Matrix

Although HAZOP is primarily qualitative, modern practice (and KTU Part B questions) incorporates a **semi-quantitative Risk Score**:

$$
R \;=\; S \times L
$$

Where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \text{Risk Score (unitless)} \\
S &= \text{Severity of consequence (rated 1 to 5)} \\
L &= \text{Likelihood of the deviation occurring (rated 1 to 5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Standard KTU-Aligned Risk Matrix

| Severity (S) | Description | Likelihood (L) | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **5** | Catastrophic — fatality, major fire | **5** | Frequent — occurs daily/weekly |
| **4** | Severe — serious injury, large release | **4** | Probable — occurs monthly |
| **3** | Moderate — medical injury, moderate release | **3** | Occasional — occurs yearly |
| **2** | Minor — first aid, small release | **2** | Remote — once in 10 years |
| **1** | Negligible — no injury, trivial release | **1** | Improbable — once in 100 years |

### Risk Acceptance Classification

| Risk Score (R) | Classification | Required Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **15–25** | **HIGH** | Stop operation, immediate mitigation |
| **8–14** | **MEDIUM** | Action required within weeks |
| **4–7** | **LOW** | Action recommended |
| **1–3** | **NEGLIGIBLE** | Accept with monitoring |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Centrifugal Pump P-101 Suction Line

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Intent:** *"Transfer raw hexane from atmospheric storage Tank T-101 to distillation column C-101 at a flow of 50 m³/h, suction pressure 1.2 bar."*

Let us work through **3 representative deviations** out of the 28 possible.

### Deviation 1 — NO FLOW

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Cause:} \quad &\text{(i) Pump P-101 fails mechanically} \rightarrow \text{no flow} \\
&\text{(ii) Suction line blockage (e.g., debris, closed manual valve MV-101)} \\
&\text{(iii) Suction tank T-101 runs empty (LOW LEVEL)} \\
\textbf{Consequence:} \quad &\text{Pump overheats, mechanical seal damage, possible fire} \\
&\text{Process trip, downstream column C-101 loses feed} \\
\textbf{Existing Safeguard:} \quad &\text{(i) Flow indicator FI-101 with low-low alarm} \\
&\text{(ii) Pump motor current interlock (ESD)} \\
&\text{(iii) Low-low level switch LSL-101 in T-101} \\
\textbf{Recommendation:} \quad &\text{Install a flow switch FSL-101 tripping P-101 on low flow}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Deviation 2 — HIGH PRESSURE (at pump discharge)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Cause:} \quad &\text{(i) Blocked discharge line (e.g., closed block valve BV-102)} \\
&\text{(ii) Sudden downstream block valve closure} \\
\textbf{Consequence:} \quad &\text{Pump dead-heading, casing rupture, fire/explosion risk} \\
\textbf{Existing Safeguard:} \quad &\text{Pressure Safety Valve PSV-101 set at 8 bar} \\
&\text{Pressure transmitter PT-101 with HH alarm} \\
\textbf{Recommendation:} \quad &\text{Add a rupture disc in parallel with PSV-101 for higher capacity}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Deviation 3 — REVERSE FLOW

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Cause:} \quad &\text{(i) Pump P-101 stops while column C-101 still holds liquid} \\
&\text{(ii) Column pressure exceeds pump suction pressure} \\
\textbf{Consequence:} \quad &\text{Vaporization of hexane in suction line} \\
&\text{Pump impeller rotation in reverse — mechanical damage} \\
\textbf{Existing Safeguard:} \quad &\text{Non-return valve NRV-101 installed on discharge} \\
\textbf{Recommendation:} \quad &\text{None — adequate. Document the existing NRV in the HAZOP sheet.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 The HAZOP Worksheet — Standard KTU/Industry Format

This table is the **mandatory deliverable** that students must know how to construct.

| Node | Intention | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Actions | Resp. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P-101 suction | Transfer hexane at 50 m³/h | NO FLOW | Pump failure, line block | Pump damage, fire | FI-LL alarm, ESD | Add flow switch FSL-101 | Inst. Eng. |
| P-101 discharge | Delivery at 5 bar | HIGH PRESSURE | Blocked line | Pump rupture | PSV-101, PT-HH alarm | Add rupture disc | Process Eng. |
| T-101 | Hold hexane at level 70% | LOW LEVEL | Outlet too high, low inflow | Pump cavitation | LSL-101 interlock | None | Operations |

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Comparison — HAZOP vs Other PHA Methods

This is a **high-yield KTU Part B comparison point**.

| Feature | HAZOP | What-If | FMEA | Fault Tree |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Type** | Qualitative | Qualitative | Qualitative/Semi-quant | Quantitative |
| **Trigger Mechanism** | Guide Words | Open questions | Component failure modes | Top event |
| **Best Suited For** | Continuous processes | Early concept design | Equipment-level | Complex systems |
| **Team Size** | 5–8 | 3–5 | 1–3 | 3–6 |
| **Output** | Deviation list with actions | Hazards list | RPN scores | Probability |
| **Time Required** | High (weeks) | Low (days) | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Standard Origin** | IEC 61882 | None formal | IEC 60812 | IEC 61025 |
| **Strength** | Thoroughness | Speed | Component focus | Mathematical rigor |
| **Limitation** | Slow, requires P&IDs | Lacks structure | Misses interaction effects | Needs reliable data |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When comparing methods, emphasize that **HAZOP is the gold standard for continuous chemical processes** but is **less efficient for batch/sequence processes** — for which methods like HAZOP-time, FMEA, or LOPA may be better suited.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1.** Define HAZOP. List any six standard guide words used in HAZOP.

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**

### Model Answer:

**HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a systematic, structured, qualitative hazard identification technique developed by the Chemical Industries Association (UK) in the 1970s. It uses a set of standard **Guide Words** applied to process parameters at defined **study nodes** within a P&ID to identify potential **deviations** from design intent, evaluate their causes and consequences, and recommend safeguards.

**Six Standard Guide Words:**

1. **NO (NOT)** — Complete negation of design intent.
2. **MORE** — Quantitative increase beyond intent.
3. **LESS** — Quantitative decrease below intent.
4. **REVERSE** — Logical opposite of the design action.
5. **AS WELL AS** — Additional activity occurring along with the intent.
6. **PART OF** — Only part of the design intent is achieved.
7. **OTHER THAN** — Complete substitution of the intended activity.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** A student who writes *only the names* without meanings scores 2/3. To secure full marks, **write the meaning of each guide word with a one-line engineering example**.

---

### **Question 2.** What is meant by a "study node" in HAZOP? Why is the plant divided into nodes?

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**

### Model Answer:

A **Study Node** is a specific location or section in the process (typically at the inlet or outlet of a major equipment item such as a pump, vessel, heat exchanger, or column) where the HAZOP team applies guide words to process parameters to generate deviations.

**The plant is divided into nodes** to:
1. Manage study complexity — analyzing an entire plant in one go is impossible.
2. Ensure systematic coverage — every section is examined.
3. Maintain the **process intention** clearly defined for each section.
4. Allow the team to focus on **local context** (e.g., pump suction vs. column overhead).
5. Enable **documented traceability** in the HAZOP worksheet.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** A common mistake is confusing "node" with "step." The correct KTU phrasing is "study node," referring to a **physical location**, not a process step.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive HAZOP Theory and Application**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the **complete procedure for conducting a HAZOP study** for a chemical process plant. Discuss the role of each team member. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump transfers toluene from a storage tank to a reactor at 30 m³/h, 4 bar discharge pressure. Apply HAZOP methodology and conduct the study for any **four deviations** at the pump suction node, presenting the analysis in standard HAZOP worksheet format. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — HAZOP Procedure and Team Roles

**Step 1 — Define Scope and Objectives:**
- Identify the physical and operational boundary of the study.
- Define the design stage (concept / detailed / operational).
- Specify deliverables expected.

**Step 2 — Assemble the Multidisciplinary Team:**

| Team Role | Primary Contribution |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Team Leader / Facilitator** | Conducts the session, manages time, ensures every deviation is examined |
| **Process Engineer** | Explains design intent and process flow on P&IDs |
| **Control & Instrumentation Engineer** | Identifies instrument-based safeguards and interlocks |
| **Operations Engineer** | Provides field experience and operability concerns |
| **Mechanical Engineer** | Highlights equipment failure modes |
| **Safety Engineer** | Assesses risk severity and ranks recommendations |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Captures the entire session in the HAZOP worksheet |

**Step 3 — Gather Documentation:** P&IDs, PFDs, Cause & Effect Matrix, Heat & Material Balance, MSDS, Operating Procedures, Equipment Datasheets.

**Step 4 — Divide Plant into Study Nodes:** At the inlet and outlet of every major equipment item.

**Step 5 — State Process Intention for Each Node:** For example, *"Transfer toluene at 30 m³/h and 4 bar."*

**Step 6 — Apply Guide Words to Each Parameter:** Generate deviations such as *NO FLOW, HIGH PRESSURE.*

**Step 7 — Analyze Each Deviation:** Identify **Causes**, **Consequences**, **Existing Safeguards**, and **Recommendations**.

**Step 8 — Documentation:** Complete the HAZOP worksheet with action tracking.

**Step 9 — Follow-Up:** Track actions to closure and re-HAZOP after plant modifications.

---

### Part (b) — HAZOP Worksheet for Pump Suction Node

**Process Intention:** *"Transfer toluene from Tank T-201 to Reactor R-201 at a flow rate of 30 m³/h and suction pressure of 1.0 bar."*

**Standard HAZOP Worksheet — Four Deviations:**

| # | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Recommendations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **NO FLOW** | (i) Pump P-201 mechanical failure, (ii) Blocked suction strainer, (iii) Tank T-201 empty | Pump overheating, seal damage, fire hazard | Flow indicator FI-LL with ESD interlock | Install dedicated low-flow trip FSL-201 |
| 2 | **LESS FLOW** | (i) Partially closed suction valve, (ii) Strainer partially blocked, (iii) Pump wearing | Cavitation, vibration, reduced throughput | Flow transmitter FT-201 with low alarm | Add remote level indication in T-201 |
| 3 | **HIGH PRESSURE (at suction)** | (i) Inert gas blanketing failure, (ii) Inlet line choked but with high upstream head | Pump cavitation, seal damage | Suction pressure transmitter PT-LL | Add vacuum breaker on suction line |
| 4 | **REVERSE FLOW** | (i) Pump stops, column-side pressure greater than suction side | Pump impeller reverse rotation, vapor lock in suction line | Non-return valve NRV-201 on discharge | None — adequately protected |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often **omit the consequences column** — losing 2 marks.
> - Many **confuse "safeguard" with "recommendation"** — a safeguard is **existing**, a recommendation is **proposed**.
> - Avoid the mistake of stating **"PSV present"** generically — always specify the **tag number and set pressure** (e.g., *"PSV-201 set at 6 bar"*).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): HAZOP Comparison and Risk Assessment**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare **HAZOP and FMEA** techniques with respect to their purpose, methodology, output, applicability, advantages, and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **semi-quantitative risk ranking method** used in HAZOP. A deviation has severity rating 4 and likelihood rating 3. Calculate the risk score and classify the action required. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — HAZOP vs FMEA Comparison

| Comparison Aspect | HAZOP | FMEA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Identify process hazards & operability issues | Identify equipment / component failure modes |
| **Methodology** | Guide Words applied to process parameters at nodes | Failure modes analyzed for each component |
| **Output** | HAZOP worksheet with deviations, causes, actions | RPN score = S × O × D |
| **Best Applicable To** | Continuous chemical processes | Mechanical / electrical equipment |
| **Team Size** | 5–8 multidisciplinary | 1–3 specialist |
| **Standard** | IEC 61882 | IEC 60812 |
| **Strength** | Thorough, structured, considers interactions | Component-level depth, quantifiable RPN |
| **Limitation** | Slow, requires P&IDs | Misses process interactions; lacks systemic view |

---

### Part (b) — Risk Score Computation

The **Risk Score formula**:

$$
R \;=\; S \times L
$$

**Given:** $S = 4$ (Severe — serious injury, large release), $L = 3$ (Occasional — once per year).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 4 \times 3 \\
R &= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification from the KTU Risk Matrix:**

| Risk Score Range | Category | Required Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **15–25** | HIGH | Immediate shutdown, urgent mitigation |
| **8–14** | **MEDIUM** ← $R = 12$ falls here | **Action required within weeks** |
| 4–7 | LOW | Action recommended |
| 1–3 | NEGLIGIBLE | Accept with monitoring |

**Conclusion:** Since $R = 12$, the deviation falls in the **MEDIUM risk category**. The HAZOP team must **issue a written recommendation, assign it to a responsible engineer, and track its closure within a defined time window (typically weeks to one quarter)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often **forget to multiply** the values, writing only "12" without showing the formula. Always show the **explicit calculation**.
> - Many students **state the classification** but **omit the required action**. Both must be present to score full marks.
> - Do not confuse **HAZOP qualitative risk** with **FMEA RPN** ($S \times O \times D$). These are different formulas.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for HAZOP — KTU Module 4**

### Must-Memorize Facts

- [ ] HAZOP = **Hazard and Operability Study**
- [ ] Developed by **Chemical Industries Association (CIA), UK, 1970s**
- [ ] International standard: **IEC 61882**
- [ ] **7 standard guide words**: NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN
- [ ] **Team size**: 5–8 multidisciplinary members
- [ ] **Team leader / facilitator** drives the session
- [ ] **Node** = physical location at equipment inlet/outlet
- [ ] **Process intention** = designer's expected operating condition
- [ ] **Deviation** = Guide Word + Parameter (e.g., NO FLOW)
- [ ] Every deviation → **Causes, Consequences, Safeguards, Actions**
- [ ] **Safeguard = existing**; **Recommendation/Action = proposed**

### Must-Draw Diagrams for Exam

- [ ] **HAZOP study flowchart** (procedure steps)
- [ ] **HAZOP worksheet table** (with all 7 columns)
- [ ] **Guide Word × Parameter matrix**
- [ ] **Team composition block diagram**
- [ ] **Risk matrix (5×5) with classification**

### Must-Know Comparisons (for Part B 14-mark questions)

- [ ] HAZOP vs FMEA
- [ ] HAZOP vs What-If
- [ ] HAZOP vs Fault Tree Analysis
- [ ] Qualitative HAZOP vs Quantitative risk methods

### Must-Solve Numerical Formula

- [ ] Number of deviations per node: $N = n \times m$ (Guide Words × Parameters)
- [ ] Risk Score: $R = S \times L$ (Severity × Likelihood)
- [ ] Risk classification (HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, NEGLIGIBLE)

### Key Engineering Examples to Recall

- [ ] **NO FLOW** → pump failure, line blockage
- [ ] **HIGH PRESSURE** → blocked valve, runaway reaction
- [ ] **LOW LEVEL** → pump cavitation, vessel dry running
- [ ] **REVERSE FLOW** → pump shut down with column still pressurized
- [ ] **OTHER THAN** → wrong material charged into reactor

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When a 14-mark Part B question asks about HAZOP, **always structure your answer in 3 layers**:
> 1. **Definition + Standard** (1 mark)
> 2. **Procedure / Methodology** (5 marks)
> 3. **Application via Worksheet + Diagram** (8 marks)
> This guarantees the examiner can identify all required components and assign marks cleanly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Scheme:** HAZOP is **always a team-based qualitative study**. Never write that HAZOP can be done by a single person or that it is purely computational. This distinction carries marks every single time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bow Tie Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bow Tie Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bow Tie Diagram (BTD)** is an integrated, visual risk management and hazard analysis tool that combines the deductive logic of a **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** on the threat side with the inductive logic of an **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** on the consequence side. It is anchored at a central **Top Event (TE)**, producing a graphical shape resembling a "bow tie" (two triangles sharing a common vertex).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A Bow Tie Diagram is a schematic representation of the pathways from *Causes* (Threats) to a *Critical Event* (Top Event) and subsequently to the *Outcomes* (Consequences), with explicit depiction of **Preventive Controls** (left side barriers) and **Mitigative Controls** (right side barriers) that interrupt these pathways.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam and Floodplain" Model

Imagine a **reservoir (Top Event)** holding back water (hazardous energy/material). On the **left bank**, several rivers (Threats) feed the reservoir. **Levees and dams (Preventive Controls)** are built along these rivers to stop water from entering. If the dam breaks (Top Event occurs), the water flows downstream into a **floodplain** (Consequences). On the **right bank**, **flood barriers, evacuation routes, and emergency dams (Mitigative Controls)** are positioned to limit damage.

The bow tie diagram is essentially this **two-sided barrier model**, where:
- **Left side** = prevention (stop it from happening)
- **Right side** = mitigation (limit damage once it happens)

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hazard** | A physical situation with the potential for harm (e.g., flammable hydrocarbon under pressure). |
| **Top Event** | The central undesired event; loss of containment of the hazard. |
| **Threat** | An initiating cause that can trigger the Top Event. |
| **Preventive Control** | A barrier that prevents a Threat from progressing to the Top Event. |
| **Mitigative Control** | A barrier that prevents the Top Event from progressing to a Consequence. |
| **Consequence** | The end outcome (fire, explosion, toxic release) of the realized Top Event. |
| **Escalation Factor** | A condition that degrades or defeats a barrier (e.g., maintenance failure, human error). |
| **Degradation Factor** | Synonym for escalation factor; the "killer of barriers." |

> [!NOTE]
> The Bow Tie method was developed by **Shell** in the late 1970s and is now an **ISO 31000-aligned** and **OGP/IPIECA-recognized** industry standard for demonstrating risk controls in process plants, drilling rigs, and pipelines.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Risk Metric:** Individual Risk = **f × C** where *f* is the frequency of the Top Event (events/year) and *C* is the consequence magnitude (e.g., fatalities, USD loss).
- **ALARP Threshold:** **1 × 10⁻⁶ fatalities/year** for workers; **1 × 10⁻⁵ fatalities/year** for public (industry convention).
- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level):** Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) ranges from **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁵** for SIFs (Safety Instrumented Functions) per IEC 61511.
- **LOPA Reference:** Layer of Protection Analysis semi-quantitative frequency bands from **1 × 10⁻¹ to 1 × 10⁻⁶ per year**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Two-Layer Logic

The bow tie is the *visual unification* of two classical probabilistic methods:

### Layer 1 (Left Triangle) — Fault Tree Logic (Deductive, "What caused it?")
- Starts from the **Top Event** and works *backward* through AND/OR gates to identify basic initiating events.
- A bow tie **abbreviates** the fault tree by listing only the **immediate threats** (often the result of minimal cut sets).

### Layer 2 (Right Triangle) — Event Tree Logic (Inductive, "What happens next?")
- Starts from the **Top Event** and works *forward* through success/failure branches of barriers.
- A bow tie **abbreviates** the event tree by showing only the **major consequence outcomes** after barriers have acted.

## 2.2 The Six Logical Steps to Build a Bow Tie

1. **Define the Scope & System Boundary** — Specify the plant unit, equipment, or process operation.
2. **Identify the Hazard** — e.g., pressurized LPG in a bullet tank.
3. **Set the Top Event (TE)** — e.g., *Catastrophic rupture of the LPG bullet tank.*
4. **Enumerate Threats (Causes)** — External impact, internal corrosion, overpressure, operator error, gasket failure, etc.
5. **Enumerate Consequences (Outcomes)** — Vapor cloud explosion, BLEVE, jet fire, pool fire, toxic exposure, environmental release.
6. **Map Controls (Barriers)** — For each threat→TE path, list preventive controls; for each TE→consequence path, list mitigative controls. Each barrier should be **SMART** (Specific, Measurable, Actionable, Responsible, Time-bound).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Expression | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Risk} = f_{TE} \times C$ | Baseline risk = frequency of top event × consequence severity | Risk ranking before controls |
| $f_{TE}' = f_{TE} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i$ | Residual frequency after *n* independent preventive barriers | Risk reduction calculation |
| $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i}$ | Risk Reduction Factor of stacked barriers | Demonstrating ALARP |
| $\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} \in [10^{-1}, 10^{-5}]$ | Probability of Failure on Demand range for SIL 1 to SIL 4 | IEC 61511 / 61508 |
| $f_{TE} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} f_j$ | Total TE frequency = sum of all threat frequencies | Combining parallel threat paths |
| $P(\text{Consequence}_k) = P(TE) \times \prod_{q} (1 - \text{PFD}_q)$ | Probability of reaching a specific consequence *k* after mitigative barriers | Consequence pathway analysis |
| $\text{ALARP} \iff \text{Risk} \leq 10^{-5} \text{ to } 10^{-6} \text{ fatalities/yr}$ | As Low As Reasonably Practicable boundary | Go/No-go decision on additional controls |
| $\text{Sum of PFDs} \leq 10^{-1} \text{ (for SIL 1)}$ | IEC 61511 risk graph target | SIF design verification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PFD** (Probability of Failure on Demand) is the *fraction of time* a barrier fails to operate on demand. **PFH** (Probability of Failure per Hour) is used for continuous-mode operations. Do not interchange them in KTU derivations.

## 2.4 Why Bow Ties are Indispensable in Process Plants

- **Regulatory Compliance:** Required by **COMAH (UK), OSHA PSM, OSHA 1910.119, CCPS Guidelines**, and increasingly by Indian **PESO / OISD** for hydrocarbon facilities.
- **Communication:** Non-quantitative enough for operators, structured enough for engineers.
- **Auditability:** Each barrier has an owner, inspection regime, and performance standard.
- **HSE-MS Integration:** Forms the backbone of **ISO 45001** and **ISO 14001** hazard registers.
- **Bridging Tool:** Links **HAZOP → LOPA → Bow Tie → ALARP Demonstration**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Risk Reduction Through a Single Bow Tie

**Problem Statement:** A pressure vessel in a KTU scenario has a Top Event frequency of $f_{TE} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ per year. There are two independent preventive barriers in series: a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) with PFD = $1 \times 10^{-2}$ and a Basic Process Control System (BPCS) interlock with PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$. The consequence of the realized Top Event is a fatality event with severity $C = 1$ (one fatality). Determine the residual risk.

### Step 1: State the Baseline Risk Equation

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{baseline}} = f_{TE} \times C
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{baseline}} = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ fatalities/year}
$$

### Step 3: Identify the Residual Risk Formula

For *n* independent barriers in series, the residual frequency $f_{TE}'$ is:

$$
f_{TE}' = f_{TE} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Product of PFDs

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{2} \text{PFD}_i = \text{PFD}_{PSV} \times \text{PFD}_{BPCS}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{2} \text{PFD}_i = (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) = 1 \times 10^{-3}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Residual Frequency

$$
f_{TE}' = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1 \times 10^{-3}) = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ events/year}
$$

### Step 6: Compute the Residual Risk

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{residual}} = f_{TE}' \times C = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ fatalities/year}
$$

### Step 7: Apply the ALARP Test

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{residual}} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ fatalities/year} \;\leq\; 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ (worker ALARP threshold)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The residual risk sits **at the boundary** of the ALARP worker threshold. To demonstrate ALARP rigorously, the engineer must perform a **cost–benefit (Gross Imminence of Danger) analysis**: if the cost of further risk reduction is *grossly disproportionate* to the marginal safety gain, ALARP is satisfied.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

### Step 1: Define RRF

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{f_{TE}}{f_{TE}'} = \frac{1}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i}
$$

### Step 2: Compute for the Above Example

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{3} = 1000
$$

### Step 3: Engineering Interpretation

An **RRF of 1000** means the combined barriers reduce the top-event frequency by three orders of magnitude. To reach SIL 3 territory ($\text{PFD} \in [10^{-3}, 10^{-4}]$), a third independent barrier (e.g., a SIL 3 SIF) would be required.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Barrier Types

| Barrier Class | Side | Typical Examples in Process Plants | Failure Modes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware — Passive** | Both | Bund walls, blast walls, fireproofing, dikes | Degradation, mechanical breach |
| **Hardware — Active** | Both | PSV, rupture disk, ESD valve, deluge system | Mechanical jamming, set-point drift |
| **Instrumented (SIF)** | Both | SIL-rated PLC interlock, fire & gas detector | Sensor failure, logic solver failure, final element failure |
| **Human / Procedural** | Both | Operating procedure, permit-to-work, training | Human error, rule violation |
| **Emergency Response** | Mitigation | Firewater pump, foam system, evacuation, medical | Drills not conducted, PPE failure |

---

## 3.4 Consequence Pathway Probability (Multi-Barrier Mitigation)

### Step 1: State General Formula

$$
P(\text{Consequence}_k) = P(TE) \times \prod_{q=1}^{m} (1 - \text{PFD}_q)
$$

### Step 2: Numerical Example

Suppose the TE has occurred (so $P(TE) = 1$). Two mitigative barriers lie between the TE and the worst consequence:
- **Gas detection + isolation** (PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$)
- **Emergency blowdown** (PFD = $5 \times 10^{-1}$)

### Step 3: Substitute

$$
P(\text{Worst Consequence}) = 1 \times (1 - 1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 - 5 \times 10^{-1})
$$

$$
P(\text{Worst Consequence}) = 1 \times 0.9 \times 0.5 = 0.45 \; (45\%)
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

Even with two barriers, a 45% chance of the worst outcome suggests either **additional barriers** (e.g., foam pourers, water spray) or **degraded PFDs** must be targeted. KTU examiners expect this calculation as proof of barrier effectiveness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Bow Tie Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The Mermaid diagram below is the **canonical Bow Tie** for a pressurized hydrocarbon storage tank, the most frequent KTU 2024 exam scenario.

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["Threat A External Impact"]:::threat
    T2["Threat B Internal Corrosion"]:::threat
    T3["Threat C Overpressure"]:::threat
    T4["Threat D Gasket Failure"]:::threat
    
    P1["P1 PSV Set 10 barg"]:::control
    P2["P2 BPCS High Pressure Trip"]:::control
    P3["P3 Cathodic Protection"]:::control
    P4["P4 Bund Wall + Drainage"]:::control
    P5["P5 Inspection Regime"]:::control
    P6["P6 Maintenance Procedure"]:::control
    
    TE["TOP EVENT Catastrophic Vessel Rupture"]:::topevent
    
    M1["M1 Emergency Block Valve ESD"]:::control
    M2["M2 Fire and Gas Detection"]:::control
    M3["M3 Firewater Deluge System"]:::control
    M4["M4 Emergency Isolation"]:::control
    M5["M5 Site Emergency Plan"]:::control
    
    C1["C1 Vapor Cloud Explosion VCE"]:::consequence
    C2["C2 Pool Fire"]:::consequence
    C3["C3 Toxic Smoke Inhalation"]:::consequence
    C4["C4 Environmental Release"]:::consequence

    T1 --> P1 --> TE
    T1 --> P2 --> TE
    T2 --> P3 --> TE
    T2 --> P5 --> TE
    T3 --> P1 --> TE
    T3 --> P2 --> TE
    T4 --> P6 --> TE
    T4 --> P4 --> TE

    TE --> M1 --> C1
    TE --> M1 --> C2
    TE --> M2 --> C3
    TE --> M3 --> C2
    TE --> M4 --> C1
    TE --> M5 --> C3
    TE --> M5 --> C4

    classDef threat fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#C00000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#D6E9FF,stroke:#2E5BBA,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef topevent fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef consequence fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Bow Tie vs. Fault Tree vs. Event Tree — Comparative Logic Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Hazard Identification"]:::step --> A2["Identify Top Event TE"]:::step
    A2 --> A3["Develop Bow Tie Diagram"]:::step
    A3 --> A4["Does it have causes?"]:::dec
    A4 -- Yes --> A5["Use FTA logic for threats side"]:::method
    A4 -- No --> A6["Skip threat enumeration"]:::skip
    A5 --> A7["Combine into single Bow Tie"]:::method
    A6 --> A7
    A3 --> A8["Does it have outcomes?"]:::dec
    A8 -- Yes --> A9["Use ETA logic for consequence side"]:::method
    A8 -- No --> A10["Skip consequence path"]:::skip
    A9 --> A11["Combine into single Bow Tie"]:::method
    A10 --> A11
    A7 --> A12["Add Preventive and Mitigative Controls"]:::step
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13["Verify ALARP"]:::step
    A13 --> A14["Document in HSE Register"]:::step

    classDef step fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B58900,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDec classDef dec fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C65911,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classMethod classDef method fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#6AA84F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classSkip classDef skip fill:#EFEFEF,stroke:#999999,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Reading Convention Summary

| Element | Symbol in Mermaid | Visual Convention (Industry Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| **Threat** | Red box | Red rectangle, left side |
| **Top Event** | Yellow/Gold box | Hexagon or central red circle |
| **Preventive Control** | Blue box | Blue lozenge, between threat and TE |
| **Mitigative Control** | Blue box | Blue lozenge, between TE and consequence |
| **Consequence** | Green box | Green rectangle, right side |
| **Barrier Failure Path** | Dashed line | Dashed arrow showing degraded control |
| **Escalation Factor** | Sideways "C-clamp" | C-clamp symbol biting the barrier |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a Bow Tie Diagram. List its six essential elements.**

**Model Answer:**
