A Bow Tie Diagram is a graphical risk assessment tool that integrates fault tree and event tree analysis, showing the pathways from causes to consequences via a central Top Event. The six essential elements are:
1. **Hazard** (the source of energy/material)
2. **Threats** (causes on the left)
3. **Top Event** (central undesired event)
4. **Preventive Controls** (left-side barriers)
5. **Mitigative Controls** (right-side barriers)
6. **Consequences** (outcomes on the right)

**[3 marks — 1 mark definition + 2 marks for six elements @ 0.33 each, or 0.5 each for 4 critical ones]**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Preventive Controls and Mitigative Controls in a Bow Tie Diagram. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Control | Mitigative Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Between threat and top event (left) | Between top event and consequence (right) |
| **Purpose** | Stop the unwanted event from occurring | Limit damage once the event has occurred |
| **Timing** | *Before* the incident | *After* the incident initiates |
| **Example** | Pressure Safety Valve (PSV), BPCS high-pressure trip, cathodic protection | Firewater deluge system, emergency block valve, gas detection + isolation |

**[3 marks — 1 mark distinction + 1 mark timing + 1 mark example]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Q. Module 4 — Part (a) & (b)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Construct a Bow Tie Diagram for a **pressurized LPG bullet tank** in a refinery. Identify at least **four threats**, **one top event**, **three preventive controls**, **two mitigative controls**, and **three consequences**. Use a labeled schematic and explain the logic.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* If the frequency of the top event is $f_{TE} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per year, and there are three independent preventive barriers with PFDs of $1 \times 10^{-1}$, $5 \times 10^{-2}$, and $1 \times 10^{-2}$ respectively, calculate: (i) the residual frequency, (ii) the Risk Reduction Factor (RRF), and (iii) comment on whether the residual risk meets the ALARP threshold of $1 \times 10^{-6}$ fatalities/year (assume one fatality per event).

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Top Event:** *Catastrophic rupture of the LPG bullet tank releasing 200 tonnes of LPG.*

**Threats (Causes):**
- **T1** External impact (vehicle collision, dropped load)
- **T2** Internal corrosion under insulation (CUI)
- **T3** Overpressure due to blocked outlet / fire engulfment
- **T4** Mechanical fatigue at nozzle weld
- **T5** Foundation settlement

**Preventive Controls (Left-side Barriers):**
- **P1** Crash-resistant perimeter barrier (vehicle impact protection)
- **P2** Cathodic protection + scheduled UT thickness monitoring (corrosion)
- **P3** Pressure Safety Valve set at design pressure + BPCS high-pressure interlock
- **P4** API 620/650 design compliance + weld inspection + settlement survey

**Mitigative Controls (Right-side Barriers):**
- **M1** Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valve on inlet/outlet lines
- **M2** Water spray / deluge system to cool vessel during external fire
- **M3** Bund wall containment of pool fire + foam pourers
- **M4** Emergency response team + on-site fire brigade

**Consequences (Outcomes):**
- **C1** Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE) — far-field blast overpressure
- **C2** BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) of the bullet
- **C3** Pool fire within the bund
- **C4** Toxic smoke inhalation to personnel
- **C5** Environmental contamination

**Schematic:** (A bow tie with T1–T4 on the left feeding P1–P4 to the central TE, branching to M1–M4 and out to C1–C5.)

**[7 marks — 1 mark each for: 4 threats (2), 1 TE (1), 3 preventive (1.5), 2 mitigative (1), 3 consequences (1.5)]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- $f_{TE} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per year
- $\text{PFD}_1 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$, $\text{PFD}_2 = 5 \times 10^{-2}$, $\text{PFD}_3 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
- $C = 1$ fatality/event

**(i) Residual Frequency:**

$$
f_{TE}' = f_{TE} \times \prod_{i=1}^{3} \text{PFD}_i
$$

$$
\prod \text{PFD} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (5 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) = 5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
f_{TE}' = (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (5 \times 10^{-5}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ per year}
$$

**(ii) Risk Reduction Factor:**

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\prod \text{PFD}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2 \times 10^{4}
$$

**(iii) ALARP Check:**

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{residual}} = f_{TE}' \times C = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \times 1 = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ fatalities/year}
$$

Since $2.5 \times 10^{-8} \leq 1 \times 10^{-6}$ (worker ALARP threshold), the residual risk is **below ALARP** and considered **broadly acceptable**. No further risk reduction is mandated, but cost-benefit analysis confirms ALARP is satisfied.

**[7 marks — 2 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 3 marks (iii) with comparison]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the concept of **Escalation Factors** in a Bow Tie Diagram. List four typical escalation factors that defeat barriers in a process plant, with one example each. Discuss why barrier management is more critical than barrier installation.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A pump handling benzene (flammable, toxic) has a top event "Loss of Containment" frequency of $8 \times 10^{-3}$/year. Two independent preventive controls exist: Seal Pot System (PFD = $1 \times 10^{-1}$) and Gas Detection with Auto-Isolation (PFD = $5 \times 10^{-2}$). One mitigative control — Emergency Firewater — has PFD = $3 \times 10^{-1}$. Compute (i) the residual TE frequency, (ii) the probability of reaching the worst consequence (fire/explosion), and (iii) the overall Risk Reduction Factor.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Escalation Factor (EF) Definition:** An Escalation Factor (also called **Degradation Factor**) is a condition, action, or circumstance that *defeats* or *degrades* a barrier, allowing a threat to bypass a control and reach the Top Event, or allowing a Consequence to bypass a Mitigative Control.

**Four Typical Escalation Factors in Process Plants:**

1. **Maintenance Failure** — e.g., PSV not tested for 5 years, set-point drift undetected. Defeats the **Pressure Safety Valve** barrier.
2. **Human Error (Procedural)** — e.g., operator overrides BPCS interlock without management-of-change review. Defeats **BPCS High-Pressure Trip**.
3. **Harsh Environment** — e.g., corrosion under insulation (CUI) in coastal refinery. Defeats **Mechanical Integrity** barriers.
4. **Communication Failure** — e.g., shift handover omits critical alarm information. Defeats **Procedural/Situational Awareness** barriers.
5. **Impacts between plant units** — e.g., explosion in Unit A ruptures pipework in Unit B, defeating **Spatial Separation** barrier.

**Why Barrier Management > Barrier Installation:**
Installing a barrier is a *one-time* engineering decision; managing it is a *continuous lifecycle* commitment. Barriers degrade over time due to corrosion, fouling, software drift, procedural obsolescence, and human complacency. The **CCPS Process Safety Beacon** and **OGP Risk Assessment Data Directory** consistently show that barrier degradation is the root cause in **>60% of major incidents** (e.g., Texas City, 2005; Buncefield, 2005). A bow tie is only as strong as its weakest barrier.

**[7 marks — 1 mark definition, 2 marks for 4 EFs, 4 marks for management discussion]**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Given:**
- $f_{TE} = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ per year
- $\text{PFD}_{\text{Seal}} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$
- $\text{PFD}_{\text{GasDet}} = 5 \times 10^{-2}$
- $\text{PFD}_{\text{Firewater}} = 3 \times 10^{-1}$ (mitigative)

**(i) Residual Top Event Frequency:**

$$
f_{TE}' = 8 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (5 \times 10^{-2})
$$

$$
f_{TE}' = 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}
$$

**(ii) Probability of Reaching Worst Consequence:**

Since the Top Event has occurred, $P(TE) = 1$.

$$
P(\text{Fire/Explosion}) = 1 \times (1 - \text{PFD}_{\text{Firewater}})
$$

$$
P(\text{Fire/Explosion}) = 1 \times (1 - 3 \times 10^{-1}) = 0.7 = 70\%
$$

**(iii) Overall Risk Reduction Factor:**

Considering all three barriers (two preventive + one mitigative):

$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (5 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 - 3 \times 10^{-1})}
$$

$$
\text{RRF}_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(0.1)(0.05)(0.7)} = \frac{1}{0.0035} \approx 285.7
$$

So the combined barriers reduce the risk by a factor of approximately **286**, or just over 2.5 orders of magnitude.

**[7 marks — 2 marks (i), 2 marks (ii), 3 marks (iii)]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mixing up PFD and PFH** — Examiners will not award full marks if PFD is used where continuous-mode PFH is required.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Frequency must carry the explicit "per year" or "events/year" qualifier. Risk = fatalities/year, NOT just "fatalities."
> 3. **Incorrectly placing controls** — A firewater deluge is a **mitigative** control (acts after loss of containment), NOT preventive. Mark deduction: -1 per misplacement.
> 4. **Neglecting independence assumption** — When computing $f_{TE}'$, barriers MUST be independent. Common cause failure (CCF) reduces effective independence. State this assumption.
> 5. **Confusing ALARP and ALARA** — ALARP = *As Low As Reasonably Practicable* (UK/European). ALARA = *As Low As Reasonably Achievable* (US NRC). In KTU context, prefer **ALARP**.
> 6. **Missing the PFD product** — Many students add PFDs; the correct operation for series barriers is **multiplication**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Bow Tie = **FTA (left) + ETA (right)** joined at a Top Event.
- Six elements: **Hazard → Threats → Preventive Controls → Top Event → Mitigative Controls → Consequences**.
- **Preventive** controls act *before* the event; **Mitigative** controls act *after*.
- A bow tie is **abbreviated FTA/ETA** — it does not show AND/OR gates explicitly, only the most important cut sets and outcomes.
- Key risk equation: $\text{Risk} = f_{TE} \times C$, with residual risk $f_{TE}' = f_{TE} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$.
- **RRF** demonstrates how much the barrier stack reduces frequency.
- **ALARP** threshold for workers: $1 \times 10^{-6}$ fatalities/year.
- **Escalation factors** are the *silent killers* of barriers — barrier management is more critical than installation.
- Bow Ties must be **maintained dynamically**: review after every HAZOP, MOC, near-miss, or incident.
- Bow Ties are part of the **CCPS Risk Based Process Safety (RBPS)** and **OGP/IPIECA** frameworks.
- **Quantitative risk** can be expressed using **f-N curves** (frequency vs. number of fatalities) for spatial risk mapping.
- A **good Bow Tie** has at least **two independent** barriers on each critical path — *defense in depth*.
- **LOPA** (Layer of Protection Analysis) is the *quantitative backbone* of a Bow Tie; the diagram is the *visual representation*.
- PFD ranges per **IEC 61511**: SIL 1 (10⁻¹–10⁻²), SIL 2 (10⁻²–10⁻³), SIL 3 (10⁻³–10⁻⁴), SIL 4 (10⁻⁴–10⁻⁵).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Fault Tree Analysis — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive (top-down), logical, and graphical failure analysis methodology used to determine the combinations of component-level failures, human errors, and external conditions that can lead to an undesired top-level event (the "Top Event") in a complex engineering system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** FTA is a systematic, deductive analytical technique in which an undesired state of the system (Top Event) is postulated, and the system is then analyzed in the context of its immediate environment to find all credible ways in which the Top Event can occur. The analysis proceeds downward through the system using Boolean logic until the resolution of the Top Event is reduced to its constituent **Basic Events** (root causes).

The technique was pioneered by **H.A. Watson of Bell Telephone Laboratories** in **1962** for the U.S. Air Force **Minuteman Missile Launch Control System**. It is now codified under standards such as:
- **IEC 61025** — Fault Tree Analysis (International Standard)
- **NRC NUREG-0492** — Fault Tree Handbook (U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a doctor trying to find out **why a patient has a fever (the Top Event)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "Fever Diagnosis as Fault Tree Analysis"**
> - **Top Event:** Patient has high fever.
> - You trace causes deductively: Fever could come from **Infection OR Heatstroke OR Inflammatory disease** (these are joined by an **OR gate**).
> - For **Infection**, both bacteria AND a weakened immune system must be present simultaneously (**AND gate**).
> - You keep digging until you reach "irreducible" root causes: exposure to virus, lack of sleep, dehydration — these are your **Basic Events**.

In exactly the same way, an FTA starts from an undesired outcome (e.g., "**Reactor Vessel Rupture**", "**Toxic Gas Release**") and traces the event backwards through the plant using logical gates until it reaches root-cause basic events whose failure probabilities are already known from field data, manufacturer specs, or industry databases (e.g., **OREDA, IEEE Std 500**).

## 1.3 Why FTA Matters in Process Plant Safety

- **Compliance** with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (PSM) and CCPS guidelines.
- **Identification of single-point failures** (minimal cut sets of size 1).
- **Quantitative risk assessment (QRA)** — calculates the probability of the Top Event.
- **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** detection — visualizes shared root causes across subsystems.
- **Allocation of safety resources** — guides inspection, redundancy, and SIL decisions (per **IEC 61511**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between Fault Tree Analysis and Event Tree Analysis."* Remember — **FTA is DEDUCTIVE (backward, one Top Event)**, whereas **ETA is INDUCTIVE (forward, branching consequences from an initiating event)**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Elements of a Fault Tree

A fault tree is composed of **two classes of symbols**: (i) **Event symbols** (rectangles, circles, diamonds, ovals) and (ii) **Logic gate symbols** (AND, OR, etc.).

### 2.1.1 Event Symbols

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle | Event (Intermediate Event) | A fault event resulting from logical combination of lower events |
| Circle | Basic Event | A primary failure whose probability is known; no further development |
| Diamond | Undeveloped Event | A failure whose data is unavailable or considered insignificant |
| Oval | Conditional Event | A condition or external event (e.g., "Maintenance in progress") |
| House | House Event | A normal/expected condition used as boundary condition |
| Triangle | Transfer Symbol | Connects to another part of the tree on same/different page |

### 2.1.2 Logic Gates

| Gate | Symbol | Boolean Logic | Process Plant Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AND** | Flat D-shape | $A \cap B$ | Both pumps fail simultaneously → No flow |
| **OR** | Curved shield | $A \cup B$ | Pump fails OR Valve closes → No flow |
| **Exclusive OR** | Shield with double curve | $A \oplus B$ | Exactly one of two redundant sensors trips |
| **Inhibit** | Diamond AND | $A \cap B$ | $A$ occurs only if **condition** $B$ is true |
| **Priority AND** | AND with P | $A$ before $B$ | Alarm sounds before shutdown activates |
| **N-out-of-M (Voting)** | Ellipse with V | $\binom{M}{N}$ | 2-out-of-3 voting logic (IEC 61508) |

## 2.2 Construction Logic — Step-by-Step Deductive Process

1. **Define the Top Event** clearly and unambiguously (e.g., "Loss of cooling water to Reactor R-101").
2. **Identify the immediate causes** of the Top Event — these become the inputs to the first-level gate.
3. **Apply Boolean logic** (AND/OR) at each level based on whether all causes must occur together (AND) or any one alone is sufficient (OR).
4. **Continue decomposition** until every branch terminates at a **Basic Event**, **Undeveloped Event**, or **House Event**.
5. **Validate the tree** using consensus review with plant engineers, operations, and instrumentation specialists.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule for Gate Selection:**
> - If the Top Event occurs **only when ALL** lower events occur → use **AND gate** (parallel/redundant configuration).
> - If the Top Event occurs **if ANY ONE** of the lower events occurs → use **OR gate** (series/single configuration).

## 2.3 Minimal Cut Sets (MCS) — The Heart of Qualitative FTA

A **Cut Set** is a set of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the Top Event.
A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is a cut set where no event can be removed without losing the cut set property (i.e., all events are essential).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim from IEC 61025):** *"A minimal cut set is the smallest combination of component failures which, if they all occur, will cause the top event to occur."*

### 2.3.1 Significance of Minimal Cut Sets
- **Order of MCS** = number of basic events in the set.
- **MCS of order 1** → Single Point Failures (CRITICAL — must be eliminated).
- **MCS of order 2 or 3** → Double/Triple failures (manageable with redundancy, proof testing).
- **Higher-order MCS** → Negligible contribution to Top Event probability.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Top Event Probability (OR gate, $n$ independent events) | $P_{TE} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - q_i)$ | $q_i$ = failure probability of event $i$ |
| 2 | Top Event Probability (AND gate, $n$ independent events) | $P_{TE} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i$ | Valid for rare, independent failures |
| 3 | Approximation (rare event) | $P_{TE}^{OR} \approx \sum q_i$ | Used when $q_i \ll 1$ (typical $\leq 10^{-2}$) |
| 4 | Birnbaum Importance ($I_B$) | $I_B(i) = \frac{\partial P_{TE}}{\partial q_i}$ | Sensitivity measure |
| 5 | Fussell–Vesely Importance ($I_{FV}$) | $I_{FV}(i) = \frac{P(\text{Top Event via } i)}{P_{TE}}$ | Fractional contribution measure |
| 6 | Risk Reduction Worth (RRW) | $RRW_i = \frac{P_{TE}}{P_{TE}(q_i = 0)}$ | Improvement if event $i$ never fails |
| 7 | Risk Achievement Worth (RAW) | $RAW_i = \frac{P_{TE}(q_i = 1)}{P_{TE}}$ | Worsening if event $i$ is certain to fail |
| 8 | Average Probability (Demand) | $\bar{P} = \frac{\sum \lambda_i t_i}{N}$ | For non-repairable over mission time $t$ |
| 9 | Unavailability (Standby) | $Q(t) \approx \frac{\lambda_D t}{2} + \lambda_D \cdot T_{proof}/2$ | IEC 61511, PFDavg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas feed directly into **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** and **SIL (Safety Integrity Level)** determination per **IEC 61511**. The MCS order informs whether a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is required and at what integrity level.

## 2.5 Real-World Application Scenarios

- **Petrochemical:** Probability of overpressure in a distillation column → informs need for Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) and rupture disk.
- **Nuclear:** Station blackout (loss of off-site + on-site AC power) → drives design of Emergency Diesel Generators.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Batch reactor runaway → cross-checked against HAZOP findings.
- **Software Safety:** Decomposition of avionics software failure modes (DO-178C compliance).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Complete Worked Example: Pump-and-Valve Cooling System

### 3.1.1 System Description

A process cooling system consists of:
- **Two centrifugal pumps P1 and P2** in parallel (only one needed for adequate flow).
- **Two valves V1 and V2** in series (both must open for flow).

The **Top Event** is: **"No Flow to Heat Exchanger E-101"** (loss of cooling).

The corresponding logic equation is:
$$
T = (P_1 \cap P_2) \cup (\overline{V_1} \cup \overline{V_2})
$$

The first term represents "both pumps fail." The second term represents "either valve fails to open."

## 3.2 Derivation of Top Event Probability — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

Given data (assumed mission time, **per year**):

$$
q_{P_1} = 2 \times 10^{-2}, \quad q_{P_2} = 2 \times 10^{-2}, \quad q_{V_1} = 3 \times 10^{-2}, \quad q_{V_2} = 3 \times 10^{-2}
$$

### Step 1 — Evaluate the AND sub-tree (parallel pumps)

Both pumps must fail for the AND sub-event to occur. Assuming independence:
$$
P(P_1 \cap P_2) = q_{P_1} \cdot q_{P_2} = (2 \times 10^{-2})(2 \times 10^{-2}) = 4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the OR sub-tree (series valves)

At least one valve must fail to close the path. The probability that **at least one** valve fails:
$$
P(\overline{V_1} \cup \overline{V_2}) = 1 - (1 - q_{V_1})(1 - q_{V_2})
$$

Substituting numerical values:
$$
P(\overline{V_1} \cup \overline{V_2}) = 1 - (1 - 0.03)(1 - 0.03) = 1 - (0.97)^2
$$

Expanding the squared term:
$$
(0.97)^2 = 0.9409
$$

Therefore:
$$
P(\overline{V_1} \cup \overline{V_2}) = 1 - 0.9409 = 0.0591
$$

### Step 3 — Combine sub-trees under the top OR gate

$$
P_T = 1 - (1 - P(P_1 \cap P_2))(1 - P(\overline{V_1} \cup \overline{V_2}))
$$

Substitute the values obtained in Steps 1 and 2:
$$
P_T = 1 - (1 - 4 \times 10^{-4})(1 - 0.0591)
$$

Compute each factor:
$$
1 - 4 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9996
$$
$$
1 - 0.0591 = 0.9409
$$

Multiply:
$$
(0.9996)(0.9409) = 0.94052
$$

Final Top Event probability:
$$
\boxed{P_T = 1 - 0.94052 = 5.948 \times 10^{-2} \approx 0.0595 \;\; \text{per year}}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets

Apply Boolean algebra:
$$
T = (P_1 P_2) + (\bar{V_1} + \bar{V_2})
$$

After distribution and simplification (no further reduction possible without dependence assumptions):
- **MCS #1:** $\{P_1, P_2\}$ — order 2
- **MCS #2:** $\{\bar{V_1}\}$ — order 1 ⚠️ **SINGLE-POINT FAILURE**
- **MCS #3:** $\{\bar{V_2}\}$ — order 1 ⚠️ **SINGLE-POINT FAILURE**

> [!WARNING]
> **Single-Point Failure Alert:** The valves in series create two order-1 cut sets. This is the dominant contributor to Top Event probability. **Recommendation:** Reconfigure to two valves in parallel (or add a bypass with administrative control) and re-analyze.

### Step 5 — Verify with Rare-Event Approximation

Since all $q_i \ll 1$, the linear approximation holds:
$$
P_T \approx q_{P_1} q_{P_2} + q_{V_1} + q_{V_2}
$$

Compute each term:
$$
q_{P_1} q_{P_2} = 4 \times 10^{-4}
$$
$$
q_{V_1} + q_{V_2} = 0.03 + 0.03 = 0.06
$$

Therefore:
$$
P_T \approx 4 \times 10^{-4} + 0.06 = 0.0604 \;\; \text{per year}
$$

Compare with the exact value $0.0595$: the difference is **$9.4 \times 10^{-4}$**, which is the contribution of higher-order interactions neglected by the approximation. The approximation is acceptable for screening purposes.

### Step 6 — Calculate Fussell–Vesely Importance

For event $V_1$ (or $V_2$, by symmetry):
$$
I_{FV}(V_1) = \frac{P(\text{Top Event due to } V_1)}{P_T}
$$

Numerator = probability of all cut sets involving $V_1$:
$$
P_{V_1} = q_{V_1} + (1 - q_{V_1})q_{P_1} q_{P_2}
$$

$$
P_{V_1} = 0.03 + (0.97)(4 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.03 + 3.88 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.030388
$$

Therefore:
$$
I_{FV}(V_1) = \frac{0.030388}{0.0595} \approx 0.511
$$

By symmetry, $I_{FV}(V_2) \approx 0.511$. For pumps:
$$
I_{FV}(P_1) = I_{FV}(P_2) \approx \frac{q_{P_1} q_{P_2}}{P_T} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0595} \approx 0.0067
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The two valves together account for over **99 %** of the Top Event risk. Engineering attention should focus on improving valve reliability (better maintenance, higher-spec components, or moving to parallel redundancy).

## 3.3 General Analytical Procedure (Algorithm for Board Exams)

1. Draw the **fault tree** with the Top Event at the apex.
2. Write the **Boolean structure function** using logical operators.
3. Apply **Boolean algebra laws** (Distributive, Associative, Idempotent, Absorption) to obtain **disjoint minimal cut sets**.
4. Identify **Minimal Cut Sets** and rank by order.
5. Substitute **failure probability data** for each basic event.
6. Compute **Top Event probability** using the appropriate gate formulas.
7. Compute **importance measures** if quantitative ranking is required.
8. Recommend **design changes** (redundancy, diversity, derating, proof testing).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fault Tree Construction Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Define Top Event] --> B0[Identify Immediate Causes]
    B0 --> C0{Logic Type}
    C0 -- All required --> D0[AND Gate]
    C0 -- Any sufficient --> E0[OR Gate]
    D0 --> F0[Decompose Each Input]
    E0 --> F0[Decompose Each Input]
    F0 --> G0{Reached Root?}
    G0 -- No --> F0
    G0 -- Yes --> H0[Assign Basic Event Probabilities]
    H0 --> I0[Apply Boolean Algebra]
    I0 --> J0[Extract Minimal Cut Sets]
    J0 --> K0[Calculate Top Event Probability]
    K0 --> L0[Compute Importance Measures]
    L0 --> M0[Recommend Safety Improvements]
```

## 4.2 Fault Tree Diagram — Pump & Valve Cooling System (Top Event: No Flow to E-101)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TopEvent["TOP EVENT: No Flow to E-101"]
    OR1{{"OR GATE"}}
    AND1{{"AND GATE"}}
    OR2{{"OR GATE"}}
    Pump1(("Basic: P1 Fails to Run"))
    Pump2(("Basic: P2 Fails to Run"))
    Valve1(("Basic: V1 Fails to Open"))
    Valve2(("Basic: V2 Fails to Open"))

    TopEvent --> OR1
    OR1 --> AND1
    OR1 --> OR2
    AND1 --> Pump1
    AND1 --> Pump2
    OR2 --> Valve1
    OR2 --> Valve2
```

## 4.3 Boolean Reduction & MCS Extraction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Raw["Raw Structure Function: P1.P2 + V1 + V2"] --> Step1["Apply Idempotent Law"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Apply Absorption Law"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Identify MCS"]
    Step3 --> MCS1["MCS1: {P1, P2}"]
    Step3 --> MCS2["MCS2: {V1}"]
    Step3 --> MCS3["MCS3: {V2}"]
    Step3 --> Output["Order-1 SPF + Order-2 Redundancy"]
```

## 4.4 Integration with Other Safety Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HAZOP Study] --> B[Fault Tree Construction]
    C[Process Flow Diagram] --> B
    D[P&ID and Instrument Data] --> B
    B --> E[Minimal Cut Sets]
    E --> F[Quantitative Risk Assessment]
    F --> G[SIL Determination IEC 61511]
    G --> H[SIF Design and Validation]
    E --> I[Common Cause Failure Analysis]
    I --> J[Operating Procedure Review]
```

## 4.5 Gate Decision Matrix — Visual Logic Selector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does Top Event occur if ANY single cause alone occurs?}
    Q1 -- YES --> R1[Use OR GATE — Series Configuration]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Do ALL causes need to occur together?}
    Q2 -- YES --> R2[Use AND GATE — Parallel Configuration]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3[Apply Conditional / Inhibit Gate]
    Q3 --> R3[Consider EX-OR or INHIBIT gate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The flowcharts above represent the *algorithmic* procedure for fault tree construction — a block-level functional architecture suitable for KTU 14-mark problems where physical schematic drawing is not required.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Fault Tree Analysis. List any four symbols used in a fault tree.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Fault Tree Analysis is a deductive, top-down failure analysis technique used to identify combinations of component failures and human errors that can lead to an undesired Top Event in a system.

**Four Symbols:**

1. **Rectangle** — Intermediate Event (event resulting from logical combination of lower events).
2. **Circle** — Basic Event (a primary failure event whose probability is known).
3. **AND Gate** — Combines events such that the output occurs only if **all** inputs occur.
4. **OR Gate** — Combines events such that the output occurs if **any one** input occurs.

*(Full marks for clear definition plus correctly named symbols with brief purpose.)*

---

### **Q2. What is a Minimal Cut Set? Why is it important in process plant safety?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is the smallest combination of basic events which, if they all occur, will cause the Top Event to occur. Removing any one event from the set prevents the Top Event.

**Importance:**
1. Identifies **Single-Point Failures** (order-1 MCS) requiring immediate engineering action.
2. Provides a basis for **quantitative risk calculation** of the Top Event probability.
3. Guides **redundancy design** — high-order MCS indicate sufficient redundancy.
4. Supports **SIL allocation** in Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) design.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction procedure of a Fault Tree for any process plant hazard of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Top Event Chosen:** *Explosion in Atmospheric Distillation Column D-101.*

**Step 1 — Define Top Event:**
"Catastrophic rupture of D-101 due to internal overpressure."

**Step 2 — Immediate Causes (Level 1):**
The column can rupture if **(AND)**: (i) internal pressure exceeds design pressure AND (ii) the pressure relief system fails to operate.

**Step 3 — Decomposition of Input 1 (Overpressure):**
Overpressure can occur if **(OR)**:
- Excessive feed flow (control valve CV-101 fails open), OR
- Loss of reflux cooling (pump P-201 fails), OR
- Blocked overhead vapour line (block valve closed).

**Step 4 — Decomposition of Input 2 (Relief Failure):**
Relief fails if **(OR)**:
- Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) is stuck closed, OR
- Rupture Disc has pre-existing defect, OR
- Discharge header is blocked.

**Step 5 — Termination:**
Each branch ends at a **Basic Event** (e.g., CV-101 stuck, P-201 fails on demand) or a **House Event** (e.g., power available).

**Sketch:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP["TOP: Column Rupture D-101"]
    AND1{{"AND"}}
    OR1{{"OR (Overpressure Cause)"}}
    OR2{{"OR (Relief Failure Cause)"}}
    A(("CV-101 Fails Open"))
    B(("P-201 Fails"))
    C(("Overhead Line Blocked"))
    D(("PRV Stuck Closed"))
    E(("Rupture Disc Defective"))
    F(("Discharge Header Blocked"))
    TOP --> AND1
    AND1 --> OR1
    AND1 --> OR2
    OR1 --> A
    OR1 --> B
    OR1 --> C
    OR2 --> D
    OR2 --> E
    OR2 --> F
```

**Final Structure Function:**
$$
T = (A + B + C) \cdot (D + E + F)
$$

**Minimal Cut Sets** (after Boolean expansion):
- $\{A, D\}, \{A, E\}, \{A, F\}, \{B, D\}, \{B, E\}, \{B, F\}, \{C, D\}, \{C, E\}, \{C, F\}$ — all order-2 MCS.

---

**A. (b)** A fire water pumping system has two parallel pumps and a common discharge header with a single isolation valve. The failure probabilities per year are: $q_{P_1} = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$, $q_{P_2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-2}$, $q_V = 2 \times 10^{-2}$. Calculate the probability of "Fire water system failure on demand". **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Logic:**
- Both pumps in parallel → AND gate failure condition (both must fail).
- Single valve in series → OR gate condition (any valve failure kills flow).

Therefore:
$$
T = (P_1 \cdot P_2) + V
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of AND sub-event:**
$$
P(P_1 \cap P_2) = q_{P_1} \cdot q_{P_2} = (1.5 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of Top Event:**
$$
P_T = P(P_1 \cap P_2) + q_V - P(P_1 \cap P_2) \cdot q_V
$$

$$
P_T = 2.25 \times 10^{-4} + 2 \times 10^{-2} - (2.25 \times 10^{-4})(2 \times 10^{-2})
$$

$$
P_T = 0.020225 - 4.5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0202205
$$

**Result:**
$$
\boxed{P_T \approx 2.02 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{per year}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The single isolation valve dominates the failure probability (≈ **98.9 %** of $P_T$). Recommendation: install redundant isolation valves or change to a double-block-and-bleed configuration.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**B. (a)** Explain the difference between **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)**. Mention at least four differences. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direction of Analysis | Deductive (backward) | Inductive (forward) |
| 2 | Starting Point | Undesired **Top Event** | **Initiating Event** (e.g., pipe rupture) |
| 3 | Number of Outcomes | One (focuses on single Top Event) | Many branching outcomes (success, failure modes) |
| 4 | Logic Used | AND / OR gates (Boolean) | Binary branches at each safety function |
| 5 | Typical Output | Minimal Cut Sets, Top Event Probability | Event Sequence Diagram, Branch Frequencies |
| 6 | Application Stage | Detailed design, modification | Frequency estimation, consequence screening |
| 7 | Standards | IEC 61025 | NUREG/CR-2304, CCPS Guidelines |

**Conclusion:** FTA *quantifies causes*; ETA *quantifies consequences*. They are **complementary** — together they form the basis of Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA).

---

**B. (b)** A reactor cooling system requires both **pump A** and **pump B** to operate. Each pump has probability of failure $q_A = q_B = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ per demand. A standby pump **C** with similar reliability can be auto-started if both A and B fail. Calculate: **(i)** the failure probability of the system, **(ii)** the Fussell–Vesely importance of pump C. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) System Failure Probability:**

System fails only if **all three** pumps fail (since C auto-starts after A and B fail). Assuming independence:
$$
P_T = q_A \cdot q_B \cdot q_C = (5 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**(ii) Fussell–Vesely Importance of Pump C:**

Since pump C appears in **every** minimal cut set (only MCS = {A, B, C}):
$$
I_{FV}(C) = \frac{P(\text{Top Event involving } C)}{P_T}
$$

In this system, **every** failure path involves C, therefore:
$$
\boxed{I_{FV}(C) = 1.0}
$$

**Interpretation:** Pump C is **critically important** — without it, the system failure probability would rise from $1.25 \times 10^{-7}$ to $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ (an increase by factor **200**). Pump C must have high reliability, regular proof testing, and dedicated instrumentation.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing AND / OR gates** — remember, parallel redundancy = AND failure condition (both must fail); series components = OR failure condition (any one fails).
> 2. **Forgetting independence assumption** — if basic events share a Common Cause Failure (CCF), apply **β-factor model** and mention it.
> 3. **Adding probabilities naively** — when cut sets are **not disjoint**, use inclusion-exclusion or rare-event approximation.
> 4. **Skipping Minimal Cut Set identification** — many 7-mark questions specifically demand it; full marks require explicit list.
> 5. **No units in final answer** — always state "per year", "per demand", or "dimensionless" alongside numerical values.
> 6. **Drawing without logic gate labels** — examiners deduct marks if AND/OR shapes are unclear or unlabelled.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fault Tree Analysis:**

- ✅ **FTA is deductive** (Top → Basic), **ETA is inductive** (Initiating → Consequences).
- ✅ **Top Event** must be precisely defined and mutually exclusive with normal operation.
- ✅ **Gate Selection Rule:** Parallel components → AND for failure; Series components → OR for failure.
- ✅ **Minimal Cut Set** = smallest set of basic events whose joint failure causes the Top Event.
- ✅ **Order-1 MCS** = Single Point Failure → must be eliminated.
- ✅ **OR gate formula:** $P_T = 1 - \prod (1 - q_i)$ | **AND gate formula:** $P_T = \prod q_i$.
- ✅ **Rare-event approximation valid** when $q_i \leq 10^{-2}$.
- ✅ **Fussell–Vesely Importance** ranks components by fractional contribution to Top Event risk.
- ✅ **Birnbaum Importance** measures sensitivity of $P_T$ to component $q_i$.
- ✅ **Standards:** IEC 61025 (FTA), NUREG-0492 (Fault Tree Handbook), IEC 61511 (process SIL).
- ✅ **Pioneered by H.A. Watson, Bell Labs, 1962**, for Minuteman missile system.
- ✅ **Complementary tools:** HAZOP, FMEA, ETA, LOPA, Bow-Tie Analysis.
- ✅ **Always validate** the fault tree through peer review, walkdown, and operating procedure analysis.
- ✅ **Always quantify** via MCS list before drawing conclusions about risk.
- ✅ **Always recommend** engineering, procedural, or operational improvements in conclusion.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Fault Tree Analysis**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, PEPOT754 — Safety Engineering of Process Plants.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Mode and Effect Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Mode and Effect Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive, and structured analytical methodology used in process safety engineering to identify, evaluate, and prioritize potential failure modes of a system, component, process, or design. It examines **how** a system can fail (**Failure Mode**), **what** the consequences of that failure would be (**Effect**), and the **mechanisms** that caused the failure (**Cause**).

In the context of KTU's **PEPOT754 – Safety Engineering of Process Plants**, FMEA is treated as a cornerstone qualitative risk assessment tool that feeds into quantitative techniques such as **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** and **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**. The methodology was originally formalized in **MIL-STD-1629** (U.S. Military, 1949) and is now institutionalized through **IEC 60812** and **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** for process industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Within the 2024 scheme, FMEA is positioned as a *qualitative-to-semi-quantitative* hazard identification tool. You are expected to know its terminology (S, O, D, RPN), the standard worksheet columns, and how it links to **HAZOP** and **LOPA** in the broader safety lifecycle.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just bought a brand-new **car**. Before driving it daily, you mentally (or actually) run through a checklist:

| What could go wrong? | How bad would it be? | How often could this happen? | How quickly would I notice? | What do I do? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brake pad wears out** | Cannot stop — disastrous | Every 30,000 km | Easy — squealing sound | Service every 30k km |
| **Headlight bulb blows** | Cannot see at night — risky | Every 50,000 km | Easy — dark on one side | Carry a spare |
| **Engine seizes** | Total stop — catastrophic | Rare (1 in 100,000) | Hard to predict | Major overhaul |

That mental exercise is essentially **FMEA in plain English**. FMEA simply *formalizes* this thinking into a documented engineering worksheet so that nothing is left to memory and the team can rank which issues deserve the most engineering attention and budget.

- **S = Severity** (How bad?)
- **O = Occurrence** (How often?)
- **D = Detection** (How likely am I to catch it before it becomes a disaster?)
- **RPN = S × O × D** (Overall priority score)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Thresholds (KTU exam tip):** RPN values above **100** generally warrant corrective action. Critical items with high Severity (≥ 9) are often addressed regardless of RPN. These numeric triggers are *theoretically* common and frequently appear in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 1.3 When and Why FMEA is Used in Process Plants

FMEA is typically applied in the following lifecycle phases:

1. **Conceptual / Design Stage** – To choose between design alternatives by comparing their RPN profiles.
2. **Detailed Engineering** – To specify instrumentation, alarms, and interlocks.
3. **Existing Plant (Re-validation)** – To capture operating experience and update safety procedures.
4. **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** – Final verification before commissioning.
5. **Post-Incident Review** – To confirm whether a known failure mode was missed or inadequately controlled.

FMEA is the **building block** for advanced analyses:
- A **DFMEA** (Design FMEA) examines design-level failures.
- A **PFMEA** (Process FMEA) examines process deviations and human factors.
- A **System FMEA** examines interactions between subsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU Module 4, the **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** variant is the most relevant since it directly addresses process plant safety — chemical reactors, distillation columns, pumps, heat exchangers, storage tanks, and piping networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The FMEA Logic Flow (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

FMEA is fundamentally a **bottom-up, inductive** analysis — meaning it starts from individual components and propagates upward to the system level. The logic flow can be decomposed into the following sequential steps:

1. **Define the Scope and Boundaries**
   - Identify the system, subsystem, or process to be analyzed.
   - Define operational modes (startup, normal operation, shutdown, maintenance, emergency).
   - Identify the *level of analysis* — whether it is a piece of equipment, a unit operation, or a complete plant.

2. **Construct a Block Diagram / P&ID Review**
   - A process engineer must be intimately familiar with the **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** before starting FMEA.
   - Each block in the diagram becomes a candidate for failure analysis.

3. **Identify Functions of Each Component**
   - *What is this component supposed to do?*
   - Function should be expressed in **active** terms: "Pump P-101 shall deliver 50 m³/h of liquid at 4 bar gauge pressure."

4. **Identify Functional Failures**
   - Functional failure = *the component fails to perform its intended function.*
   - For a pump, the functional failures are: *no flow, low flow, high flow, reverse flow, no pressure, high pressure.*

5. **Identify Failure Modes**
   - The **specific manner** in which a functional failure manifests.
   - For a centrifugal pump *no flow* function failure, the failure modes include: impeller fouled, shaft broken, suction line blocked, motor failure, seal leak, vapor lock (NPSH loss).

6. **Identify Failure Effects (Consequences)**
   - What happens at the **component, subsystem, and system/mission level** when the failure mode occurs?
   - Example: *Shaft breaks* → *No flow from pump* → *Loss of cooling to exothermic reactor R-201* → *Runaway reaction* → *Vessel rupture* → *Plant shutdown, possible fatality.*

7. **Identify Failure Causes (Mechanisms)**
   - *Why* does the failure mode occur?
   - Example: Shaft breaks due to *fatigue from misalignment*, *bearing failure*, *over-torque*, or *corrosion fatigue.*

8. **Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) Ratings.**
   - These are integer scores on a **1–10 scale** (industry standard).

9. **Calculate Risk Priority Number (RPN)**
   - RPN = S × O × D.
   - Sort and prioritize RPNs from highest to lowest.

10. **Recommend Corrective Actions / Mitigation**
    - Reduce Severity, reduce Occurrence, or improve Detection.
    - Recalculate RPN after action implementation to verify improvement.

11. **Document and Update the FMEA Worksheet.**
    - The FMEA is a *living document*, revisited whenever:
      - Design changes.
      - Operating conditions change.
      - New failure modes are discovered through field experience or incidents.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' behind FMEA logic:** Bottom-up analysis complements top-down techniques (like **HAZOP** which starts at the system level) and bottom-up techniques (like **FTA** which propagates upward). FMEA is unique because it forces engineers to consider *each* component individually — no assumed minor part is ignored.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter / Symbol** | **Definition** | **Formula / Scale** | **Unit / Range** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | Severity — seriousness of the effect on the next higher level | Rated 1 (negligible) to 10 (catastrophic, death) | Integer, 1–10 | Drives design safety margins |
| **O** | Occurrence — likelihood that the failure mode will occur | Rated 1 (very unlikely) to 10 (very high, failure is almost inevitable) | Integer, 1–10 | Drives reliability / MTBF targets |
| **D** | Detection — probability that the cause/mechanism will be detected *before* the effect reaches the customer/patient/operator | Rated 1 (very high detection capability) to 10 (no detection, almost impossible to detect before effect) | Integer, 1–10 | Drives instrumentation, alarms, and inspection frequency |
| **RPN** | Risk Priority Number | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Integer, 1–1000 | Prioritizes corrective action |
| **Action Priority (AP)** | AIAG-VDA replacement for RPN since 2019 | Categorical: Low / Medium / High | Ordinal | Better than RPN but rarely asked in KTU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the 2024 KTU scheme:** Stick to the classical RPN = S × O × D formula in your exam. The newer Action Priority (AP) approach from the **AIAG-VDA 2019 Handbook** is more nuanced but examiners will explicitly ask for **RPN** calculations in numerical questions.

### 2.3 Detailed S, O, D Rating Tables (Exam-Ready)

#### Severity (S) — Effect on the System / Customer / Operator

| **Effect** | **Severity Level** | **Score (S)** |
|---|---|---|
| No effect, noticed only by the most discerning operator | None | **1** |
| Very minor effect, noticed by most operators; no injury | Very Minor | **2** |
| Minor effect; rework or minor repair needed | Minor | **3** |
| Low effect; some loss of production; no injury | Low | **4** |
| Moderate effect; significant loss of production | Moderate | **5** |
| High effect; loss of primary function; high rework | High | **6** |
| Very high effect; very high rework; possible minor injury | Very High | **7** |
| Major effect; major rework; major injury | Major with warning | **8** |
| Hazardous effect with warning; loss of safety margin; severe injury | Hazardous with warning | **9** |
| Catastrophic — death, total system loss, environmental catastrophe | Catastrophic | **10** |

#### Occurrence (O) — Likelihood of Failure Mode Manifesting

| **Failure Rate (per item / per year)** | **Probability of Occurrence** | **Score (O)** |
|---|---|---|
| ≤ 1 in 1,500,000 | Extremely unlikely | **1** |
| 1 in 150,000 | Very low | **2** |
| 1 in 30,000 | Low | **3** |
| 1 in 5,000 | Moderate-low | **4** |
| 1 in 800 | Moderate | **5** |
| 1 in 150 | Moderate-high | **6** |
| 1 in 50 | High | **7** |
| 1 in 15 | Very high | **8** |
| 1 in 5 | Extremely high | **9** |
| ≥ 1 in 2 | Almost certain | **10** |

#### Detection (D) — Ability of Controls to Detect the Failure *Before* the Effect

| **Detection Capability** | **Probability of Non-Detection** | **Score (D)** |
|---|---|---|
| Almost certain — control prevents the failure mode entirely | Very low | **1** |
| Very high | Low | **2** |
| High | Low-moderate | **3** |
| Moderately high | Moderate | **4** |
| Moderate | Moderate | **5** |
| Low | Moderate-high | **6** |
| Very low | High | **7** |
| Remote | Very high | **8** |
| Very remote | Very high | **9** |
| Almost impossible | Almost certain non-detection | **10** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Occurrence (O)** with **Detection (D)**. Remember: *Occurrence* is about **how often the failure happens**, while *Detection* is about **how well you catch it before it causes damage**. A failure can be very rare (low O) but if it is silent and catastrophic, the D score will still be 9 or 10.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

FMEA is applied across diverse engineering domains:

- **Automotive** (recall prevention, e.g., Toyota's quality system).
- **Aerospace** (FAA mandates for aircraft systems, e.g., DO-178C).
- **Pharmaceutical** (FDA requires process FMEA for drug manufacturing).
- **Semiconductor** (cleanroom yield improvement).
- **Chemical / Petrochemical** (CCPS guidelines, OSHA 1910.119 PSM).
- **Nuclear** (probabilistic safety assessments).
- **Healthcare** (Joint Commission requires FMEA for high-risk procedures).

In a **process plant**, the FMEA typically feeds directly into the **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** design, where the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** of an interlock is partly justified by the FMEA-identified risk.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed RPN Derivation and Worked Numerical Example

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is not a *natural law*; it is an engineering heuristic combining three ranked dimensions of risk into a single sortable index. The mathematical structure is:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where each factor is an integer between **1** and **10** inclusive.

**Boundary conditions:**
- **Minimum RPN** = $1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1$ (best case: low severity, rare, easily detected).
- **Maximum RPN** = $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$ (worst case: catastrophic, almost certain, undetectable).

Therefore, the RPN is bounded as:
$$
1 \leq \text{RPN} \leq 1000
$$

**Derivation logic (textual walkthrough):**

**Step 1** — A failure mode's *consequence* is rated by Severity (S). Higher S → more damaging consequence.

**Step 2** — The *frequency* of occurrence of the failure mode is rated by Occurrence (O). Higher O → more frequent failure.

**Step 3** — The *ease of detection* (or *probability of non-detection*) is rated by Detection (D). Higher D → harder to detect before damage.

**Step 4** — The product S × O × D aggregates all three dimensions into a single scalar metric. Multiplying (rather than adding) is justified because:
- Risk should increase **multiplicatively** — a high severity *and* high occurrence *and* low detection should yield a vastly larger priority than any single factor in isolation.
- Multiplication produces a wider dynamic range (1–1000), which makes prioritization more discriminative.

**Step 5** — Items with the highest RPN are scheduled for corrective action first. The threshold for action is typically **RPN ≥ 100** (or any item with S ≥ 9, regardless of RPN).

### 3.2 Worked Example: Centrifugal Pump in a Refinery

**Scenario:** A centrifugal pump **P-101** transfers naphtha from storage to a hydrotreater. You are the process safety engineer tasked with performing an FMEA on the pump.

**Given data (extracted from maintenance records and design specs):**

| **Item / Function** | **Failure Mode** | **Effect (Local)** | **Effect (System)** | **S** | **O** | **D** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pump shall deliver 50 m³/h @ 4 bar | Impeller fouled | Reduced flow to 30 m³/h | Hydrotreater throughput loss | 7 | 5 | 4 |
| Pump shall deliver 50 m³/h @ 4 bar | Shaft seizure | No flow | Loss of feed → emergency shutdown | 9 | 2 | 3 |
| Pump shall deliver 50 m³/h @ 4 bar | Mechanical seal leak | Flammable naphtha release to atmosphere | Fire/explosion hazard | 10 | 4 | 6 |
| Pump shall deliver 50 m³/h @ 4 bar | Suction strainer plugged | Cavitation, low flow | Loss of production | 6 | 6 | 5 |
| Pump shall deliver 50 m³/h @ 4 bar | Motor winding short | Pump stops | Emergency shutdown | 8 | 3 | 4 |

**Step 1 — Calculate RPN for each failure mode:**

For the first row (Impeller fouled):

$$
\text{RPN}_1 = S \times O \times D = 7 \times 5 \times 4 = 140
$$

For the second row (Shaft seizure):

$$
\text{RPN}_2 = 9 \times 2 \times 3 = 54
$$

For the third row (Mechanical seal leak):

$$
\text{RPN}_3 = 10 \times 4 \times 6 = 240
$$

For the fourth row (Suction strainer plugged):

$$
\text{RPN}_4 = 6 \times 6 \times 5 = 180
$$

For the fifth row (Motor winding short):

$$
\text{RPN}_5 = 8 \times 3 \times 4 = 96
$$

**Step 2 — Rank the failure modes by RPN (descending order):**

| **Rank** | **Failure Mode** | **RPN** | **Action Threshold?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mechanical seal leak | **240** | YES (Critical) |
| 2 | Suction strainer plugged | **180** | YES |
| 3 | Impeller fouled | **140** | YES |
| 4 | Motor winding short | **96** | Borderline |
| 5 | Shaft seizure | **54** | NO |

**Step 3 — Identify special cases (Severity-dominant items):**

The failure mode "Mechanical seal leak" has Severity = 10. Even if its RPN were lower than 100, it would still warrant immediate corrective action because a Severity-10 event can cause fatalities.

**Step 4 — Recommend corrective actions for the top three RPN items:**

| **Failure Mode** | **Current RPN** | **Recommended Action** | **Target S** | **Target O** | **Target D** | **New RPN** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical seal leak | 240 | Install dual mechanical seal with API Plan 53B; add flammable gas detector | 10 | 4 | 2 | 80 |
| Suction strainer plugged | 180 | Install differential pressure indicator with high alarm; schedule routine cleaning | 6 | 3 | 2 | 36 |
| Impeller fouled | 140 | Add online condition monitoring; install suction filtration; periodic cleaning | 7 | 3 | 3 | 63 |

**Step 5 — Verify improvement:**

For the seal leak after mitigation:

$$
\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} = 10 \times 4 \times 2 = 80
$$

Reduction:

$$
\Delta \text{RPN} = 240 - 80 = 160 \quad \text{(66.7% reduction)}
$$

This validates the corrective action as effective.

### 3.3 Critical Comparison: FMEA vs. HAZOP vs. FTA

This comparison matrix is **high-yield** for KTU Part A questions and viva voce.

| **Dimension** | **FMEA** | **HAZOP** | **FTA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Approach Direction** | Bottom-up (inductive) | Top-down (deviation-based) | Top-down (deductive) |
| **Starting Point** | Component failure | Process intent (design) | Unwanted top event |
| **Output** | RPN-ranked list of failure modes | Causes and consequences of deviations | Boolean cut sets of causes |
| **Quantification** | Semi-quantitative (RPN) | Qualitative | Quantitative (failure probability) |
| **Best For** | Component-level reliability, mechanical failures | Process chemistry, parameter deviations | Complex accident scenarios |
| **Limitation** | Misses combinations of failures | Time-consuming for large plants | Requires accurate failure rate data |
| **Standards** | IEC 60812, AIAG-VDA 2019 | IEC 61882 | IEC 61025 |
| **Usage in PEPOT754** | Module 4 | Module 3 | Module 4/5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU Module 4:** The syllabus pairs FMEA with FTA in the same module. Be prepared to write comparative answers explaining *why* FMEA alone is insufficient (it misses failure combinations) and *why* FTA alone is insufficient (it requires you to already know the top event). The two are *complementary*, not alternatives.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Construction of a Standard FMEA Worksheet

The standard FMEA worksheet contains the following columns (in order):

1. **Item / Component ID** — Reference number from P&ID.
2. **Function** — Intended purpose of the component.
3. **Failure Mode** — *How* it can fail.
4. **Effect(s)** — Consequence on system.
5. **Severity (S)** — 1–10 score.
6. **Cause / Mechanism** — *Why* the failure occurs.
7. **Occurrence (O)** — 1–10 score.
8. **Current Controls** — Existing detection / prevention methods.
9. **Detection (D)** — 1–10 score.
10. **RPN** — Computed value (S × O × D).
11. **Recommended Action** — Mitigation.
12. **Action Owner / Target Date** — Accountability.
13. **Action Taken** — Post-mitigation status.
14. **Revised RPN** — Post-mitigation re-score.

**Construction logic (textual walkthrough):**

**Step 1** — Populate columns 1–4 from the P&ID and operating manual. This is the *factual* part of FMEA.

**Step 2** — Apply the S, O, D tables (Section 2.3 above) and assign scores. This is the *judgment* part of FMEA — it requires cross-functional input from process, mechanical, instrumentation, and operations engineers.

**Step 3** — Compute RPN for each row. Sort in descending order. Identify the top critical items.

**Step 4** — For each top item, recommend a corrective action targeting at least one of S, O, or D. The classical engineering principle is:

$$
\text{Reduction in D} \rightarrow \text{improve detection (e.g., add alarms, sensors)}
$$

$$
\text{Reduction in O} \rightarrow \text{improve reliability (e.g., better material, redundancy)}
$$

$$
\text{Reduction in S} \rightarrow \text{reduce consequence (e.g., relief valve, containment, dikes)}
$$

**Step 5** — Re-score after action and re-compute RPN. Compare to the threshold and close the loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In 14-mark questions, the model answer always shows the **worksheet structure** explicitly. Drawing a table with all 14 columns earns you 2–3 marks even if your scores are imperfect. Examiners award marks for *methodology*, not just numerical accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the FMEA Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Define System Scope and Boundaries"] --> B["Review P and ID and Construct Block Diagram"]
    B --> C["Identify Component Functions"]
    C --> D["Identify Functional Failures"]
    D --> E["Identify Specific Failure Modes"]
    E --> F["Identify Failure Effects on System"]
    F --> G["Identify Failure Causes and Mechanisms"]
    G --> H["Assign Severity Score S 1 to 10"]
    H --> I["Assign Occurrence Score O 1 to 10"]
    I --> J["Identify Current Detection Controls"]
    J --> K["Assign Detection Score D 1 to 10"]
    K --> L["Compute RPN equals S times O times D"]
    L --> M{"RPN greater than 100 OR Severity greater than 8"}
    M -- YES --> N["Recommend Corrective Action"]
    M -- NO --> O["Document and Monitor"]
    N --> P["Implement Action and Recompute RPN"]
    P --> Q["Verify RPN Reduction Achieved"]
    Q --> O
    O --> R["Update FMEA Worksheet as Living Document"]
    R --> S["Feed into SIS Design or LOPA"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The diamond `M{"RPN greater than 100 OR Severity greater than 8"}` represents the decision gate. If *either* condition is true, corrective action is mandatory. Otherwise, the item is documented for periodic review. This is the standard FMEA triage logic.

### 4.2 RPN Risk Categorization Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RPN 1 to 50 LOW PRIORITY"] --> A1["Document and Routine Maintenance"]
    B["RPN 51 to 100 MODERATE PRIORITY"] --> B1["Review in Next Plant Shutdown"]
    C["RPN 101 to 200 HIGH PRIORITY"] --> C1["Corrective Action Within 6 Months"]
    D["RPN 201 to 500 VERY HIGH PRIORITY"] --> D1["Immediate Corrective Action Required"]
    E["RPN 501 to 1000 CRITICAL PRIORITY"] --> E1["Halt Operations and Implement Mitigation"]
    F["SEVERITY 9 or 10 SPECIAL CASE"] --> F1["Mandatory Action Regardless of RPN"]
```

### 4.3 FMEA Worksheet Schematic (Conceptual Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs["INPUT DATA"]
        P1["P and ID"]
        P2["Operating Manual"]
        P3["Maintenance Records"]
        P4["Failure History Database"]
    end
    subgraph Processing["FMEA WORKSHEET"]
        W1["Item and Function Column"]
        W2["Failure Mode Column"]
        W3["Effect Column"]
        W4["S O D Scoring Block"]
        W5["RPN Calculation Block"]
    end
    subgraph Outputs["OUTPUT DECISIONS"]
        O1["RPN Ranked List"]
        O2["Critical Items Report"]
        O3["Corrective Action Register"]
        O4["Feed to SIS and LOPA"]
    end
    P1 --> W1
    P2 --> W1
    P3 --> W2
    P4 --> W2
    W1 --> W2 --> W3 --> W4 --> W5
    W5 --> O1
    W5 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** Inputs (left) feed the worksheet (middle), which produces prioritized outputs (right). This is the same architecture used in real FMEA software tools (e.g., **IQ-FMEA**, **APIS IQ-RM**, **Blickfeld FMEA**).

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Risk Reduction Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Inherent Safety Design Phase 1"] --> B["Engineering Controls Phase 2"]
    B --> C["Administrative Controls Phase 3"]
    C --> D["PPE and Mitigation Phase 4"]
    A --> A1["Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify"]
    B --> B1["Relief Valves, Alarms, Interlocks, SIS"]
    C --> C1["Procedures, Training, Inspection"]
    D --> D1["Last Line of Defense"]
    A1 --> A2["FMEA targets the LEFTMOST layer"]
    B1 --> A2
    C1 --> A2
    D1 --> A2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** This diagram illustrates the **hierarchy of controls** from the **CCPS Guidelines for Inherently Safer Design**. A well-executed FMEA will prefer reducing **Severity (S)** through inherent safety *over* merely adding detection (which only reduces **D**). Examiners often ask: *"Which is more effective — reducing S or D?"* The answer: **S reduction (inherent safety)** is fundamentally superior.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]**

**Define Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA). What is a Risk Priority Number (RPN)?**

**Model Answer:**

> **FMEA** is a systematic, bottom-up, inductive engineering methodology used to identify potential failure modes of a system or component, evaluate the consequences (effects) of those failures, and prioritize corrective actions based on risk.
>
> The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a semi-quantitative metric computed as the product of three ranked scores — Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D) — each on a scale of 1 to 10:
>
> $$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$
>
> where:
> - **S (Severity)** = seriousness of the failure's effect (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic).
> - **O (Occurrence)** = likelihood that the failure mode will occur (1 = rare, 10 = almost certain).
> - **D (Detection)** = probability that the failure will be detected before causing damage (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = undetectable).
>
> The RPN ranges from **1 to 1000**, and higher RPN values indicate greater priority for corrective action. Items with RPN > 100, or with S ≥ 9, are typically addressed first.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Pattern]**

**Differentiate between FMEA and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) in process safety.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **FMEA** | **FTA** |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Bottom-up, inductive | Top-down, deductive |
| **Starting point** | Individual component failure mode | Unwanted top event |
| **Output** | Ranked list of failure modes (RPN) | Boolean cut sets of causes |
| **Quantification** | Semi-quantitative (RPN) | Quantitative (failure probability) |
| **Strength** | Easy to perform, systematic | Identifies combinations of failures |
| **Limitation** | Misses failure combinations | Requires known top event and accurate failure data |
| **Standard** | IEC 60812 | IEC 61025 |

> Both are complementary: FMEA generates the failure modes that FTA can then analyze in combination.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Note:** As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks) and offers an internal choice. The two questions below are independent alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step methodology of conducting an FMEA for a process plant. Discuss the significance of each step. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A heat exchanger **HX-201** in a chemical plant is used to cool a process stream. The following failure data has been collected from operating experience. Conduct a complete FMEA and recommend corrective actions for items with RPN > 100.

| **Failure Mode** | **Severity (S)** | **Occurrence (O)** | **Detection (D)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tube leak | 9 | 3 | 6 |
| Shell-side fouling | 6 | 7 | 4 |
| Channel cover gasket failure | 8 | 4 | 5 |
| Cooling water pump failure (external) | 9 | 3 | 3 |
| Air binding on water side | 5 | 6 | 7 |

(7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Step-by-Step FMEA Methodology:**

1. **Define the scope and boundaries** of the system to be analyzed (e.g., the heat exchanger HX-201 and its immediate piping).

2. **Review the P&ID and construct a block diagram** identifying all components: tubes, shell, channel cover, gaskets, nozzles, instruments, and supporting utilities (cooling water supply).

3. **Identify the functions** of each component — e.g., "Tubes shall transfer heat from hot process to cooling water without leakage."

4. **Identify functional failures** — e.g., *no heat transfer*, *process leaks to water side*, *water leaks to process side*.

5. **Identify specific failure modes** — *tube leak, fouling, gasket failure, pump failure, air binding*.

6. **Identify the effects** at the local, subsystem, and system levels — e.g., tube leak causes *mixing of process and water*, leading to *contamination*, *loss of product*, *corrosion in the water system*, and *potential environmental release*.

7. **Identify failure causes** — e.g., tube leak caused by *corrosion, erosion, vibration, or thermal fatigue*.

8. **Assign S, O, D scores** (1–10 each) using the standard rating tables.

9. **Compute RPN** = S × O × D for each row.

10. **Rank and prioritize** the failure modes; flag items with **RPN > 100** or **S ≥ 9** for action.

11. **Recommend corrective actions** that reduce S, O, or D. Re-score and re-compute RPN.

12. **Document and circulate** the FMEA worksheet to all stakeholders; update it as a living document whenever the design or operating conditions change.

**Significance:** Each step is a logical filter. Skipping a step risks missing the most catastrophic failure mode (Bhopal-like scenarios often arose from incomplete FMEA in the original design). The methodology provides a **defensible, auditable trail** for regulatory inspections (OSHA PSM, EPA RMP).

**(b) — Numerical FMEA Solution:**

Compute RPN for each row:

For **Tube leak**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 9 \times 3 \times 6 = 162
$$

For **Shell-side fouling**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 6 \times 7 \times 4 = 168
$$

For **Channel cover gasket failure**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 8 \times 4 \times 5 = 160
$$

For **Cooling water pump failure**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 9 \times 3 \times 3 = 81
$$

For **Air binding on water side**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 5 \times 6 \times 7 = 210
$$

**Ranked list (descending RPN):**

| **Rank** | **Failure Mode** | **RPN** | **Action Required?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air binding | **210** | YES (Critical) |
| 2 | Shell-side fouling | **168** | YES |
| 3 | Tube leak | **162** | YES (also S = 9) |
| 4 | Channel cover gasket failure | **160** | YES |
| 5 | Cooling water pump failure | **81** | NO (but monitor) |

**Recommended Corrective Actions:**

| **Failure Mode** | **Recommended Action** | **Target S** | **Target O** | **Target D** | **New RPN** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Air binding | Install automatic air vent at high points; add differential pressure alarm | 5 | 3 | 3 | **45** |
| Shell-side fouling | Install online tube cleaning system; schedule periodic descaling | 6 | 3 | 3 | **54** |
| Tube leak | Implement eddy current testing annually; install leak detection in drain | 9 | 2 | 2 | **36** |
| Channel cover gasket failure | Replace with spiral-wound gasket; torque monitoring | 8 | 2 | 2 | **32** |
| Cooling water pump failure | Add standby pump with auto-start; add flow switch | 9 | 2 | 2 | **36** |

**Verification of improvement:** All RPNs are reduced below **100**, and the new RPN for "Tube leak" is well below threshold even though S remains 9 (Severity-dominant items now have improved D and O controls).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the FMEA procedure used in process plant safety. Explain the concept of RPN and its significance in ranking failures. (7 Marks)

**(b)** In a distillation column, the reflux pump **P-301** is used to return condensed distillate. The following failure modes have been identified. Calculate the RPN for each and recommend actions for items with RPN ≥ 100.

| **Failure Mode** | **Effect** | **S** | **O** | **D** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pump bearing failure | Loss of reflux; column flooding | 8 | 4 | 5 |
| Impeller corrosion | Reduced reflux flow | 6 | 6 | 4 |
| Mechanical seal leak | Flammable vapor release | 10 | 3 | 6 |
| Coupling misalignment | Vibration, eventual failure | 7 | 5 | 4 |
| Suction line vapor lock | Loss of NPSH, pump cavitation | 8 | 4 | 5 |

(7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — FMEA Procedure and RPN Significance:**

The **FMEA procedure** follows a defined sequence (visualized in the block diagram from Section 4.1). The methodology begins with **system scope definition**, proceeds through **function and failure mode identification**, and culminates in **S, O, D scoring** and **RPN computation**. Each block in the P&ID is independently analyzed for its function, how it could fail, and what would happen.

The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is computed as:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

It is a **single composite metric** that allows the engineering team to compare failure modes that differ in nature. Without RPN, a team might argue endlessly about whether a "minor" frequent failure is more important than a "rare" catastrophic one. RPN provides an objective ranking basis.

**Significance of RPN:**
- **Prioritization:** Focuses resources on the highest-risk items.
- **Communication:** A single number is easy to convey to management.
- **Benchmarking:** RPN reductions can be tracked across project phases.
- **Limitations:** RPN does not capture S-only criticality, and is being progressively replaced by Action Priority (AP) in newer standards (AIAG-VDA 2019).

**(b) — Numerical FMEA Solution for Distillation Column Reflux Pump:**

Compute RPN for each failure mode:

For **Pump bearing failure**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 8 \times 4 \times 5 = 160
$$

For **Impeller corrosion**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 6 \times 6 \times 4 = 144
$$

For **Mechanical seal leak**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 10 \times 3 \times 6 = 180
$$

For **Coupling misalignment**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 7 \times 5 \times 4 = 140
$$

For **Suction line vapor lock**:
$$
\text{RPN} = 8 \times 4 \times 5 = 160
$$

**Ranked List (descending RPN):**

| **Rank** | **Failure Mode** | **RPN** | **Action Required?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mechanical seal leak | **180** | YES (S = 10, Critical) |
| 2 | Pump bearing failure | **160** | YES |
| 2 | Suction line vapor lock | **160** | YES |
| 4 | Impeller corrosion | **144** | YES |
| 5 | Coupling misalignment | **140** | YES |

**Recommended Corrective Actions:**

| **Failure Mode** | **Recommended Action** | **New S** | **New O** | **New D** | **New RPN** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical seal leak | Install dual seal (API Plan 54) + vapor detector | 10 | 2 | 2 | **40** |
| Pump bearing failure | Install vibration monitoring + auto-shutdown | 8 | 2 | 2 | **32** |
| Suction line vapor lock | Add NPSH check, low NPSH alarm, cool reflux sub-cooling | 8 | 2 | 3 | **48** |
| Impeller corrosion | Use SS-316 impeller; periodic visual inspection | 6 | 3 | 3 | **54** |
| Coupling misalignment | Laser alignment during maintenance; condition monitoring | 7 | 2 | 3 | **42** |

**Outcome:** All failure modes reduced below RPN = 100, and the special-case Severity-10 item (mechanical seal leak) has been mitigated by an order-of-magnitude improvement in D and O without altering S (the inherent severity of a flammable release cannot be designed away completely).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mark Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Confusing S, O, D scale direction:** The Detection (D) scale is *inverted* — a higher number means *worse* detection. Many students write 1 for "no detection" and 10 for "good detection," which is **backwards**. Mark loss: 1–2 per question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the severity special case:** Even if RPN is low, items with S ≥ 9 must be flagged for action. Examiners allocate at least 1 mark for recognizing this rule.
>
> 3. **Skipping the FMEA worksheet structure:** In 14-mark questions, the examiner expects an **explicit table** of at least 5–6 columns (Item, Function, Failure Mode, Effect, S, O, D, RPN). Drawing this earns 2 marks minimum.
>
> 4. **No corrective action after RPN computation:** Computing RPN is only half the answer. The second half is **recommending mitigation** and showing the **reduced RPN** after action.
>
> 5. **Writing "FMEA = FTA":** These are **different** methods. FMEA is bottom-up and inductive; FTA is top-down and deductive. Confusing them in a comparison question loses 3–4 marks instantly.
>
> 6. **Missing the link to LOPA/SIS:** Modern FMEA feeds the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** determination. Stating this connection earns bonus marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA):**
>
> - [x] FMEA is a **bottom-up (inductive)** qualitative-to-semi-quantitative risk assessment method.
> - [x] The core equation is $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, with each factor ranging from **1 to 10**, so RPN ranges from **1 to 1000**.
> - [x] **S (Severity):** Higher score = more serious consequence. 10 = catastrophic (fatality, environmental disaster).
> - [x] **O (Occurrence):** Higher score = more frequent failure. 10 = almost certain.
> - [x] **D (Detection):** Higher score = *worse* detection (INVERTED scale). 10 = undetectable.
> - [x] RPN > 100 or S ≥ 9 triggers mandatory corrective action.
> - [x] Corrective actions should preferably reduce **S** (inherent safety) over D (alarms).
> - [x] FMEA worksheet has 14 standard columns; the minimal exam version has 7–8 columns.
> - [x] FMEA standards: **IEC 60812**, **AIAG-VDA 2019** (newer AP method).
> - [x] FMEA complements **HAZOP** (process deviations) and **FTA** (failure combinations).
> - [x] FMEA is a **living document** — updated on design changes, operating changes, and post-incident.
> - [x] FMEA feeds directly into **SIS design** and **SIL determination** (Module 5 topic).
> - [x] **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** is the variant most relevant to PEPOT754 syllabus.
> - [x] Mitigation hierarchy: **Inherent safety → Engineering controls → Administrative → PPE**.
> - [x] The new **Action Priority (AP)** from AIAG-VDA 2019 categorizes risk as Low / Medium / High — *not* numerically equal to RPN but logically similar.
> - [x] Common exam traps: (a) inverted D scale confusion, (b) RPN-only thinking without considering S-special cases, (c) confusing FMEA with FTA, (d) failing to recommend mitigation.
> - [x] FMEA + FTA together = robust defense-in-depth safety analysis for any process plant.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Event Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is an **inductive (forward-looking)** probabilistic risk assessment technique used to evaluate the potential outcomes of a specified **initiating event** by mapping the sequential success or failure of **independent safety functions** (head-end events) along a binary branching logic tree.

In the context of process plant safety under the KTU 2024 scheme, ETA is the **mirror-image counterpart** to Fault Tree Analysis (FTA). Where FTA works **backward** from an undesired top event to its root causes using AND/OR gates, ETA works **forward** from a single initiating event through a chronological sequence of safety barriers, ultimately quantifying the frequency or probability of every plausible end-state outcome (e.g., safe shutdown, minor release, major fire, catastrophic explosion).

The fundamental goal of ETA is to answer the board examiner's pet question: *"If this initiating event occurs, what can happen next, and how likely is each scenario?"*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Signal Model

Imagine driving on a **NH-66 Kerala highway** approaching a series of three traffic signals.

- **Initiating Event**: Your car suddenly suffers brake failure (the head-starting accident scenario).
- **Signal 1 (Safety Function A)**: You steer onto the emergency escape lane. *Success → next signal. Failure → next event branch is a crash.*
- **Signal 2 (Safety Function B)**: The escape lane's gravel drag bed slows you down. *Success → controlled stop. Failure → collision with roadside barrier.*
- **Signal 3 (Safety Function C)**: Airbags deploy correctly upon barrier impact. *Success → minor injury. Failure → severe injury.*

Each signal acts as a **YES/NO branch point**. The car "travels" down one and only one final pathway, and the probability of ending in any specific outcome is the **product of all branch probabilities** along that pathway. The Event Tree is simply a structured diagram of all these pathways, making the invisible chain-of-events visible to the safety engineer.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Quantitative Parameters Used in ETA:**
> - **$f_I$** = Frequency of the Initiating Event (typically expressed in **events/year**).
> - **$P_i$** = Probability of success (or failure) of the *i*-th safety function, dimensionless, where $0 \leq P_i \leq 1$.
> - **$f_j$** = Frequency (events/year) of the *j*-th outcome pathway.
> - **Universal Boundary Condition:** $\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(\text{Outcome}_j) = 1$ — All outcome probabilities along an event tree must sum to unity.

## 1.4 Position of ETA in the KTU 2024 Safety Engineering Toolkit

| Technique | Logic Direction | Starting Point | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FTA** | Deductive (Backward) | Top Undesired Event | Find root causes |
| **ETA** | Inductive (Forward) | Initiating Event | Find all possible consequences |
| **FMEA** | Component-level | Equipment Item | Rank failure modes |
| **HAZOP** | Deviation-based | Process Node | Identify operability problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ETA is universally classified as a **consequence analysis tool**. It does **not** identify new initiating events — it assumes the initiating event is already known and asks "what happens next?".
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
