# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Building Blocks of Every Event Tree

A professionally constructed event tree (in line with IEC 62502 standards referenced in process safety literature) consists of the following six structural elements:

1. **Initiating Event (IE)** — Placed on the extreme left as the single root node. Every ETA must begin with exactly one initiating event.
2. **Safety Function / Head-End Event (HEE)** — Independent engineered or procedural barriers placed chronologically from left to right (e.g., relief valve, interlock, operator action, firewater system).
3. **Binary Branches** — Each safety function splits into exactly two outcomes:
    - **Upper branch** = Success / Yes / Function operates as designed.
    - **Lower branch** = Failure / No / Function does not operate.
4. **Outcome Pathway** — A unique right-hand terminus reached by traversing one branch at every safety function.
5. **Outcome Consequence Description** — The physical consequence (safe shutdown, toxic release, pool fire, jet fire, VCE) attached to each pathway.
6. **Branch Probabilities / Frequencies** — Quantitative values placed above each branch.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Logical Step

- **Why chronological order?** → Because safety functions act in time sequence. The relief valve opens *after* overpressure; the firewater pump starts *after* flame detection. Sequential ordering mirrors the **physics of emergency response**.
- **Why binary?** → Simplifies probability arithmetic. A multi-state branch (e.g., partial success) can be modeled by splitting it into two binary events (e.g., "valve partially open" vs "valve fully open").
- **Why assume independence?** → The classical ETA assumes safety functions are **statistically independent**. Dependent functions require conditional probabilities or **linked event trees** (an advanced KTU-level extension).
- **Why multiply along a pathway?** → Multiplication of probabilities is mathematically valid only for **independent sequential events**, which is precisely what ETA models.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` in place of plain `|` characters inside the markdown table cells to prevent rendering breakage.**

| Sl. No. | Formula / Equation | Meaning & Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_j \;=\; f_I \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_{i,j}$ | Frequency of outcome pathway *j* = (Initiating Event Frequency) × (Product of branch probabilities along pathway *j*) | events/year |
| 2 | $\sum_{j=1}^{N} P(\text{Outcome}_j) \;=\; 1$ | Total probability conservation — sum of all pathway probabilities = unity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P(\text{Failure of HE}_i) \;=\; q_i \;=\; 1 - p_i$ | Probability of failure (lower branch) is the complement of success (upper branch) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $f_{\text{Safe}} \;=\; f_I \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$ | Frequency of fully safe outcome (all safety functions succeed) | events/year |
| 5 | $f_{\text{Top Event}} \;=\; \sum_{j \in \{\text{undesired}\}} f_j$ | Frequency of overall undesired top event = sum of frequencies of all unsafe pathways | events/year |
| 6 | $\text{Avg. Consequence Magnitude} \;=\; \frac{\sum_j C_j \cdot f_j}{\sum_j f_j}$ | Risk-weighted consequence magnitude (used in risk matrix construction) | mixed units |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event Tree Analysis is the **backbone methodology** behind quantitative risk assessment (QRA) reports mandated by the **Oil Industry Safety Directorate (OISD)** in India, **Offshore Safety Case Regulations (UK)**, and **OSHA 1910.119 PSM** in the United States. Process industries routinely apply ETA for:

- **LPG/LNG storage terminal** consequence modeling.
- **Hydrocracker reactor** overpressure scenario analysis.
- **Ammonia refrigeration plant** toxic release dispersion studies.
- **Offshore platform** fire & explosion event sequence quantification.

The numerical outputs ($f_j$ values) feed directly into the **company risk matrix** to determine ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) classification of each accident scenario.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Outcome Frequency Equation

**Statement to Prove:** The frequency of occurrence of outcome pathway *j* in a generic event tree is given by the product of the initiating event frequency and the branch probabilities along that pathway.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Define the initiating event.**
Let $I$ be a rare random initiating event with annual frequency $f_I$ (events/year). The probability that event $I$ occurs in any given one-year window is approximately $P(I) \approx f_I$ when $f_I \ll 1$ (the standard rare-event approximation used throughout KTU process safety modules).

**Step 2 — Define safety functions sequentially.**
Let there be $n$ independent safety functions $\text{HE}_1, \text{HE}_2, \dots, \text{HE}_n$ arranged in chronological order. Each function $\text{HE}_i$ succeeds with probability $p_i$ and fails with probability $q_i = 1 - p_i$.

**Step 3 — Define outcome pathway *j*.**
A pathway $j$ is uniquely identified by an *n*-tuple of branch choices. Let the indicator $x_{i,j} \in \{S, F\}$ denote whether pathway *j* takes the Success or Failure branch at safety function *i*. The probability of traversing pathway *j* is:

$$
P(\text{Pathway}_j) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{Branch}_{i,j})
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute branch probabilities.**
If pathway *j* takes the success branch at function *i*, then $P(\text{Branch}_{i,j}) = p_i$. If it takes the failure branch, then $P(\text{Branch}_{i,j}) = q_i = 1 - p_i$. Hence:

$$
P(\text{Pathway}_j) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[\, x_{i,j}^{S} \cdot p_i \;+\; x_{i,j}^{F} \cdot (1 - p_i) \,\right]
$$

where $x_{i,j}^{S}$ and $x_{i,j}^{F}$ are mutually exclusive binary indicators (one equals 1, the other equals 0).

**Step 5 — Multiply by initiating event frequency.**
The annual frequency of outcome *j* is the joint probability that the initiating event occurs **and** the system follows pathway *j*:

$$
f_j \;=\; f_I \cdot P(\text{Pathway}_j) \;=\; f_I \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{Branch}_{i,j})
$$

**Step 6 — Verification by normalization.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{2^{n}} f_j &= f_I \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{2^{n}} \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{Branch}_{i,j}) \\
&= f_I \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( p_i + q_i \right) \\
&= f_I \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1) \\
&= f_I
\end{aligned}
$$

The total frequency of all possible outcomes equals the initiating event frequency — confirming conservation of probability. **Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Pump Overpressure Event Tree

**Problem Statement:**
A centrifugal pump handling naphtha is protected by two independent safety functions:
- **HE₁** = Block valve closes automatically upon high pressure (probability of success $p_1 = 0.99$).
- **HE₂** = Rupture disc relieves overpressure successfully (probability of success $p_2 = 0.95$).

The initiating event is pump dead-head overpressure, with annual frequency $f_I = 0.1$ events/year. Construct the event tree and compute the frequency of each of the four outcomes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Construct the tree structure.**
Four pathways exist ($2^2 = 4$).

**Step 2 — Compute branch probabilities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= 0.99, \quad q_1 = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01 \\
p_2 &= 0.95, \quad q_2 = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute frequency of Pathway 1 (Block valve succeeds AND Rupture disc succeeds):**

$$
f_1 = f_I \cdot p_1 \cdot p_2 = 0.1 \times 0.99 \times 0.95
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= 0.1 \times (0.99 \times 0.95) \\
&= 0.1 \times 0.9405 \\
&= 0.09405 \; \text{events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Outcome: **Safe shutdown** — system protected.

**Step 4 — Compute frequency of Pathway 2 (Block valve succeeds, Rupture disc fails):**

$$
f_2 = f_I \cdot p_1 \cdot q_2 = 0.1 \times 0.99 \times 0.05
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_2 &= 0.1 \times 0.0495 \\
&= 0.00495 \; \text{events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Outcome: **Minor leak** through failed rupture disc.

**Step 5 — Compute frequency of Pathway 3 (Block valve fails, Rupture disc succeeds):**

$$
f_3 = f_I \cdot q_1 \cdot p_2 = 0.1 \times 0.01 \times 0.95
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_3 &= 0.1 \times 0.0095 \\
&= 0.00095 \; \text{events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Outcome: **Controlled release** via successful rupture disc.

**Step 6 — Compute frequency of Pathway 4 (Block valve fails AND Rupture disc fails):**

$$
f_4 = f_I \cdot q_1 \cdot q_2 = 0.1 \times 0.01 \times 0.05
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_4 &= 0.1 \times 0.0005 \\
&= 0.00005 \; \text{events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Outcome: **Catastrophic pump rupture** — Worst-case scenario.

**Step 7 — Verification by normalization.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 + f_2 + f_3 + f_4 &= 0.09405 + 0.00495 + 0.00095 + 0.00005 \\
&= 0.10000 \; \text{events/year} \\
&= f_I \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The probability conservation law is satisfied — the analysis is arithmetically correct.

## 3.3 KTU Analytical Summary Matrix

| Pathway | Block Valve | Rupture Disc | Probability | Frequency (per year) | Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Success | Success | $0.9405$ | $0.09405$ | Safe shutdown |
| 2 | Success | Failure | $0.0495$ | $0.00495$ | Minor leak |
| 3 | Failure | Success | $0.0095$ | $0.00095$ | Controlled release |
| 4 | Failure | Failure | $0.0005$ | $0.00005$ | Catastrophic rupture |
| **Total** | — | — | **1.0000** | **0.10000** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Implication:** Although Pathway 4 has the lowest frequency (5 × 10⁻⁵/year, i.e., once in 20,000 years), its consequence magnitude is catastrophic. Under ALARP, additional protection (e.g., a third independent safety instrumented function) must be considered to push the frequency below the company **tolerability threshold** (typically 10⁻⁶/year for catastrophic events).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Event Tree Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical flow of an Event Tree Analysis system, mapping the interactions between the initiating event generator, the safety function library, the branching decision engine, and the outcome consequence matrix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IE[Initiating Event Module] --> HE1[Safety Function HE1]
    HE1 --> B1A[Upper Branch Success]
    HE1 --> B1B[Lower Branch Failure]
    B1A --> HE2[Safety Function HE2]
    B1B --> HE2B[Safety Function HE2 after Failure]
    HE2 --> B2A[Upper Branch Success]
    HE2 --> B2B[Lower Branch Failure]
    HE2B --> B2C[Upper Branch Success]
    HE2B --> B2D[Lower Branch Failure]
    B2A --> O1[Outcome 1 Safe Shutdown]
    B2B --> O2[Outcome 2 Minor Incident]
    B2C --> O3[Outcome 3 Controlled Event]
    B2D --> O4[Outcome 4 Catastrophic Event]
    O1 --> RM[Risk Matrix Mapping]
    O2 --> RM
    O3 --> RM
    O4 --> RM
    RM --> ALARP[ALARP Decision Module]
```

## 4.2 Numerical Worked Example — Event Tree Diagram (Naphtha Pump)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IE[Overpressure Initiating Event fI equals 0.1 per year] --> A
    A{Block Valve Closes}
    A -->|Yes p1 equals 0.99| B{Rupture Disc Relieves}
    A -->|No q1 equals 0.01| C{Rupture Disc Relieves}
    B -->|Yes p2 equals 0.95| O1[Outcome 1 Safe Shutdown f1 equals 0.09405 per year]
    B -->|No q2 equals 0.05| O2[Outcome 2 Minor Leak f2 equals 0.00495 per year]
    C -->|Yes p2 equals 0.95| O3[Outcome 3 Controlled Release f3 equals 0.00095 per year]
    C -->|No q2 equals 0.05| O4[Outcome 4 Catastrophic Rupture f4 equals 0.00005 per year]
```

## 4.3 Risk Categorization Matrix (Frequency vs Consequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F[Outcome Frequency Classification] --> F1[High Frequency greater than 1 per year]
    F --> F2[Medium Frequency 10 negative 2 to 10 negative 0 per year]
    F --> F3[Low Frequency 10 negative 4 to 10 negative 2 per year]
    F --> F4[Very Low Frequency less than 10 negative 4 per year]
    C[Severity Classification] --> C1[Slightly Harmful]
    C --> C2[Minor Harmful]
    C --> C3[Major Harmful]
    C --> C4[Catastrophic]
    F4 --> R4[ALARP Region Mandatory Risk Reduction]
    C4 --> R4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> - All node identifiers use the alphanumeric pattern (e.g., `IE`, `HE1`, `O1`) — none use reserved Mermaid keywords as standalone node IDs.
> - All node labels are written in **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** without bold, italic, or HTML tags to prevent rendering failures.
> - Quotation marks have been deliberately omitted from labels since they sometimes interfere with Mermaid's parser in certain rendering engines.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Definition of Event Tree Analysis
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU-2024-Set-B]**

**Q:** Define Event Tree Analysis. State any **two** differences between Event Tree Analysis (ETA) and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA).

**Model Answer:**

Event Tree Analysis (ETA) is an inductive, forward-looking probabilistic safety assessment technique that begins with a defined initiating event and traces the sequential success or failure of independent safety functions through a binary branching logic tree to determine the frequency of all possible outcome consequences.

| Parameter | Event Tree Analysis (ETA) | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) |
|---|---|---|
| Logic Direction | Inductive (Forward) | Deductive (Backward) |
| Starting Point | Initiating Event | Top Undesired Event |
| End Result | Frequency of all outcomes | Causes of single top event |
| Use of Gates | None (binary branches only) | AND, OR, NOT gates |

**(3 Marks — Awarded for: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two valid differences.)**

### Question 2 — Identifying Event Tree Components
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper KTU-2024-Set-A]**

**Q:** List any **six** elements/components that must be present in a properly constructed Event Tree.

**Model Answer:**

1. Initiating Event (IE)
2. Safety Function / Head-End Event (HEE)
3. Branching logic (binary success/failure)
4. Outcome pathway (terminus on right side)
5. Outcome consequence description
6. Branch probability values

**(3 Marks — Awarded for: ½ mark per correctly identified element × 6 elements.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Full Event Tree Construction & Frequency Calculation (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Question Paper PEPOT754-M4-Q8]**

**Q:** A pressurized ammonia storage vessel is protected by **three** independent safety functions arranged in the following order:

- **HE₁** = Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) opens on overpressure. Probability of successful opening $p_1 = 0.98$.
- **HE₂** = Emergency Isolation Valve (EIV) closes on detection of ammonia release. Probability of successful closure $p_2 = 0.97$.
- **HE₃** = Water Spray Curtain neutralizes ammonia cloud. Probability of successful operation $p_3 = 0.90$.

The initiating event (vessel overpressure due to external fire impingement) has an annual frequency $f_I = 0.05$ events/year.

**(a)** Draw the complete Event Tree showing all eight possible outcome pathways. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Calculate the frequency of each outcome and identify which pathway corresponds to the worst-case catastrophic release. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Tree Construction (7 Marks)

The event tree contains $2^3 = 8$ pathways. The construction proceeds chronologically:

**Tree Layout (described in textual form for clarity):**

- **Initiating Event** → branches into HE₁ (PSV).
- **HE₁ Success branch** (probability 0.98) → proceeds to HE₂ (EIV).
- **HE₁ Failure branch** (probability 0.02) → also proceeds to HE₂ (the EIV still has a chance to act).
- At **HE₂**, the success branch (probability 0.97) leads to HE₃ (Water Curtain).
- At **HE₂**, the failure branch (probability 0.03) also leads to HE₃.
- At **HE₃**, the success branch (probability 0.90) terminates in a controlled outcome.
- At **HE₃**, the failure branch (probability 0.10) terminates in an uncontrolled outcome.

The eight outcome pathways are:
| Pathway Code | PSV | EIV | Water Curtain |
|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | Success | Success | Success |
| O2 | Success | Success | Failure |
| O3 | Success | Failure | Success |
| O4 | Success | Failure | Failure |
| O5 | Failure | Success | Success |
| O6 | Failure | Success | Failure |
| O7 | Failure | Failure | Success |
| O8 | Failure | Failure | Failure |

*(7 Marks: 2 marks for identifying 8 pathways, 3 marks for correct sequential layout, 2 marks for labeling all branch probabilities.)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Frequency Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_I &= 0.05 \;\text{events/year} \\
p_1 &= 0.98,\; q_1 = 0.02 \\
p_2 &= 0.97,\; q_2 = 0.03 \\
p_3 &= 0.90,\; q_3 = 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute all eight pathway frequencies using $f_j = f_I \cdot \prod (\text{branch probabilities})$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= 0.05 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.90 = 0.05 \times 0.85554 = 0.042777 \\
f_2 &= 0.05 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.10 = 0.05 \times 0.09506 = 0.004753 \\
f_3 &= 0.05 \times 0.98 \times 0.03 \times 0.90 = 0.05 \times 0.02646 = 0.001323 \\
f_4 &= 0.05 \times 0.98 \times 0.03 \times 0.10 = 0.05 \times 0.00294 = 0.000147 \\
f_5 &= 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.97 \times 0.90 = 0.05 \times 0.01746 = 0.000873 \\
f_6 &= 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.97 \times 0.10 = 0.05 \times 0.00194 = 0.000097 \\
f_7 &= 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.03 \times 0.90 = 0.05 \times 0.00054 = 0.000027 \\
f_8 &= 0.05 \times 0.02 \times 0.03 \times 0.10 = 0.05 \times 0.00006 = 0.000003
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verification by sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{8} f_j &= 0.042777 + 0.004753 + 0.001323 + 0.000147 \\
&\quad + 0.000873 + 0.000097 + 0.000027 + 0.000003 \\
&= 0.050000 \;\text{events/year} \\
&= f_I \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Worst-case identification:**
Pathway **O8** (PSV fails ∧ EIV fails ∧ Water Curtain fails) gives $f_8 = 3 \times 10^{-6}$ events/year and corresponds to **unmitigated catastrophic ammonia release**. Despite its low frequency, the consequence magnitude is maximum.

*(7 Marks: 4 marks for correct eight frequency calculations, 2 marks for verification, 1 mark for identifying O8 as the worst-case pathway.)*

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice on Event Tree Theory & ALARP (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Question Paper PEPOT754-M4-Q9]**

**Q:**
**(a)** Explain the step-by-step methodology of constructing an Event Tree. Discuss how an Event Tree Analysis differs from a Fault Tree Analysis in terms of logic direction, outcome multiplicity, and computational approach. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Using a suitable example of a reactor cooling water failure scenario, construct a simplified Event Tree and demonstrate how the **ALARP principle** is applied to the highest-consequence outcome pathway. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Methodology & Comparison (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step ETA Construction Methodology:**

1. **Identify the Initiating Event (IE)** — Select a single, well-defined starting event (e.g., loss of cooling water).
2. **Identify Safety Functions in Chronological Order** — List all independent barriers (pumps, valves, interlocks, operator actions) in the order they would respond.
3. **Define Success/Failure Criteria** — Establish what constitutes successful operation of each safety function.
4. **Construct the Binary Tree** — Draw branches left-to-right; upper branch = success, lower branch = failure.
5. **Assign Branch Probabilities** — Use failure rate databases (OREDA, IEEE 500) or operational records to assign $p_i$ values.
6. **Identify Outcomes** — Describe the physical consequence of each terminal pathway.
7. **Calculate Pathway Frequencies** — Apply $f_j = f_I \cdot \prod P(\text{branch}_{i,j})$.
8. **Map to Risk Matrix** — Plot each pathway on a frequency-versus-consequence matrix to determine ALARP classification.

**ETA vs FTA Comparison:**

| Aspect | ETA | FTA |
|---|---|---|
| Logic Direction | Inductive (forward) | Deductive (backward) |
| Outcome Multiplicity | Multiple outcomes from one initiating event | Single top event traced to multiple causes |
| Computational Approach | Probability multiplication | Boolean algebra with AND/OR gates |
| Starting Information Required | Initiating event frequency | Top event probability |
| Typical Output | Frequency of each outcome | Cut-set ranking of basic events |

*(7 Marks: 3 marks for ETA methodology, 4 marks for ETA-vs-FTA comparison.)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Cooling Water Failure Example (7 Marks)

**Scenario:** A jacketed exothermic reactor suffers loss of cooling water (CWS failure). The initiating event frequency is $f_I = 0.2$ events/year. Three safety functions exist:

- **HE₁** = Backup cooling pump auto-start, $p_1 = 0.95$
- **HE₂** = Emergency dump valve opens to quench tank, $p_2 = 0.90$
- **HE₃** = Operator manually injects inhibitor, $p_3 = 0.80$

**Simplified Event Tree (text description):**

$$
\text{Loss of CW} \rightarrow \text{Backup Pump} \rightarrow \text{Dump Valve} \rightarrow \text{Operator Action}
$$

**Calculation of catastrophic pathway (all three fail):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{catastrophic}} &= f_I \cdot q_1 \cdot q_2 \cdot q_3 \\
&= 0.2 \times (1-0.95) \times (1-0.90) \times (1-0.80) \\
&= 0.2 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.20 \\
&= 0.0002 \;\text{events/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

This equals **once every 5,000 years**.

**ALARP Application:**
Under the typical company risk matrix, a runaway reaction with potential fatality has a **tolerability threshold of $10^{-5}$/year**. Since $f_{\text{catastrophic}} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$/year exceeds this threshold by a factor of 20, the risk is in the **intolerable region** (above the ALARP line). Required actions:

1. Install a fourth independent safety function (e.g., a rupture disc with $p_4 = 0.99$).
2. Re-compute: $f_{\text{new catastrophic}} = 0.2 \times 0.05 \times 0.10 \times 0.20 \times 0.01 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$/year → now **below** the tolerability line.
3. Cost-benefit analysis confirms the additional protection is grossly disproportionate to the residual cost, hence ALARP is satisfied.

*(7 Marks: 2 marks for tree description, 2 marks for frequency calculation, 3 marks for ALARP reasoning and decision.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in ETA Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Probability Conservation Check** — Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for verifying $\sum f_j = f_I$. Students who skip this lose easy marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $p$ and $q$** — Confusing the success and failure branches is the single most common arithmetic error. Always state: *"Upper branch = Success = $p_i$; Lower branch = Failure = $1 - p_i$."*
> 3. **Treating ETA as a single-tree conclusion** — Examiners expect students to identify and discuss the **worst-case pathway** by name and consequence. Simply listing frequencies without identifying the catastrophic scenario loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the "chronological order" rule** — Safety functions MUST be drawn in time sequence. Reversing the order of HE₁ and HE₂ is a structural error.
> 5. **Failing to connect ETA outputs to ALARP** — In 14-mark questions, the examiner almost always expects a final paragraph on risk reduction or ALARP justification. Stopping at frequency calculations forfeits 2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Event Tree Analysis**

- ☐ ETA is **inductive (forward)**; FTA is **deductive (backward)**.
- ☐ Start every ETA with exactly **one initiating event** placed on the left.
- ☐ Safety functions (Head-End Events) are arranged **chronologically from left to right**.
- ☐ Branches are **binary**: upper = Success ($p_i$), lower = Failure ($1 - p_i$).
- ☐ Each safety function is assumed **statistically independent** of the others (classical ETA assumption).
- ☐ Pathway frequency formula: $f_j = f_I \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{branch}_{i,j})$.
- ☐ Total frequency conservation: $\sum_{j=1}^{2^n} f_j = f_I$ (mandatory self-check).
- ☐ Number of possible outcomes in an ETA with $n$ safety functions = $2^n$.
- ☐ ETA quantifies **frequencies** (events/year), not just probabilities.
- ☐ The **worst-case pathway** is identified by lowest frequency × highest consequence — not lowest frequency alone.
- ☐ Output of ETA feeds into the **Risk Matrix** to determine ALARP classification.
- ☐ ALARP principle: reduce risk until the cost of further reduction is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained.
- ☐ Common application domains: LPG/LNG terminals, ammonia plants, hydrocrackers, offshore platforms.
- ☐ **Examiner loves asking:** "Identify the worst-case outcome and suggest one risk-reduction measure." Always end your answer with this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layer of Protection Analysis. Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT754 - SAFETY ENGINEERING OF PROCESS PLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Layer of Protection Analysis. Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (CCPS / ISA 84.91-Aligned)**
> **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** is a *semi-quantitative* risk assessment methodology that evaluates the risk of a process hazard scenario by examining the **frequency of an initiating event** against the **probability of failure on demand (PFD)** of each **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** that mitigates it. The output is a numerical estimate of the *mitigated event frequency*, which is compared against a *risk tolerance criterion* to determine whether additional protection layers (such as a Safety Instrumented Function or SIS) are required.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, LOPA sits between qualitative tools (HAZOP, What-If, FMEA) and fully quantitative tools (Fault Tree Analysis, Event Tree Analysis, QRA). It is the **bridge method** recommended by CCPS *Guidelines for Process Safety* and forms the basis of **IEC 61511 / ISA 84.00.02 SIL determination**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swiss Cheese Model"

Imagine you are driving a car and want to prevent a serious accident. You do **not** rely on just one safety measure. You have:

| Layer (Analogy) | Process Plant Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Your eyesight and attention | Operator Training & Procedures |
| Brake pedal | Basic Process Control System (BPCS) |
| Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) | Safety Instrumented System (SIS / SIF) |
| Car crumple zone | Pressure Relief Valve / Rupture Disc |
| Seatbelt and airbag | Dike, Bund wall, Catch basin |
| 911 emergency call | Emergency Response & Firefighting |
| Padded dashboard | Active fire suppression / deluge |

Each layer is a "slice of Swiss cheese" — it has *holes* (failures). An accident occurs only when the holes in **all** slices momentarily align. LOPA counts these holes numerically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept — Independent Protection Layer (IPL):**
> A device, system, or action that is capable of **preventing a scenario from proceeding to its undesired consequence**, is **independent** of both the initiating event and other IPLs, is **auditable**, and has a known **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**.

---

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary (KTU High-Frequency Exam Terms)

- **Scenario** — A specific unplanned event (e.g., tank overfill leading to pool fire).
- **Initiating Event (IE)** — The first event in the sequence that starts the scenario. Examples: pump failure, valve passing, instrument failure, operator error.
- **Consequence** — The final undesired outcome (e.g., fire, explosion, toxic release, fatality).
- **IPL (Independent Protection Layer)** — A barrier, device, system, or human action that stops the scenario.
- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** — The probability that an IPL will *not* perform its intended function when demanded. Expressed as a fraction (0.1) or order of magnitude (10⁻¹).
- **RRF (Risk Reduction Factor)** — The inverse of PFD: $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD}$.
- **Mitigated Frequency** — The expected frequency of the consequence after all IPLs operate (or fail).
- **Risk Tolerance / Target Frequency (f_t)** — The maximum *acceptable* frequency of the consequence, defined by company policy or industry standards (e.g., 10⁻⁶/year for fatality).

---

## 1.4 When LOPA is Used in the Process Lifecycle

LOPA is typically applied **after** HAZOP or FMEA identifies significant scenarios, in the following situations:

1. **SIL Determination** for Safety Instrumented Functions (per IEC 61511).
2. **Decision-making** for installing additional engineered protection (e.g., new relief valve, new SIS).
3. **Documentation** of risk reduction claims made for a process facility.
4. **Independence verification** — proving that two claimed protections are not both vulnerable to the same root cause.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Hook:** *IE × ∏PFDᵢ ≤ f_target* — If the Initiating Event frequency multiplied by the product of all IPL PFDs is **less than or equal** to the target frequency, the scenario is acceptably safe.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental LOPA Equation

The heart of LOPA is a single multiplication chain:

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i
$$

Where:
- $f_{\text{mitigated}}$ = frequency of the consequence occurring (per year)
- $f_{IE}$ = frequency of the initiating event (per year)
- $PFD_i$ = probability of failure on demand of the *i-th* IPL
- $n$ = number of valid IPLs claimed

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a product?** Because for the consequence to occur, the IE must happen **AND** IPL-1 must fail **AND** IPL-2 must fail … **AND** IPL-n must fail. These are independent events, so probabilities multiply.

---

## 2.2 Risk Tolerance / Target Frequency Tiers

Most chemical process companies adopt a tiered structure (CCPS 2009 guidelines):

| Severity Category | Consequence Description | Typical $f_{\text{target}}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Catastrophic | ≥ 3 fatalities, or public impact, or > \$10 M loss | **10⁻⁶ / yr** |
| Serious | 1–2 fatalities, or permanent disability, or major environmental | **10⁻⁵ / yr** |
| Significant | Lost-time injury, or significant release | **10⁻⁴ / yr** |
| Minor | First-aid injury, minor release | **10⁻³ / yr** |

The acceptable frequency **drops by a factor of 10 for each step up in severity** (a 10× penalty).

---

## 2.3 Standard Initiating Event Frequencies (Industry Consensus)

These values come from CCPS *Guidelines for Process Safety* and are widely cited in KTU reference books:

| Initiating Event | Frequency $f_{IE}$ (per year) | Source/Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure vessel catastrophic rupture | 10⁻⁶ | API 581 / CCPS |
| Piping full-bore rupture (>150 mm) | 10⁻⁴ | CCPS |
| Piping leak (10 % section) | 10⁻³ | CCPS |
| Gasket / flange leak | 10⁻² | CCPS |
| Pump deadhead (dead-end blockage) | 1 / yr = 10⁰ | Operating data |
| Pump seal failure | 10⁻¹ | CCPS |
| BPCS instrument loop failure | 10⁻¹ | ISA / industry average |
| Cooling water failure | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻² | CCPS |
| Power failure | 10⁻¹ to 1 / yr | Site-specific |
| Operator error (routine task) | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻² | Human factors data |
| Operator error (non-routine) | 10⁰ to 10⁻¹ | Human factors data |
| External fire (pool fire, jet fire) | 10⁻² to 10⁻³ | Site-specific |
| Lightning strike | 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴ | Site-specific |
| Static discharge (filling operation) | 10⁻² | API RP 753 |

---

## 2.4 Standard IPL / PFD Values

| IPL Type | Typical PFD | Typical RRF | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passive IPLs** | | | |
| Dike, bund wall, containment | 10⁻² to 10⁻³ | 100–1000 | Holds 100 % of inventory |
| Fireproofing | 10⁻² | 100 | 2-hour rated, certified |
| Inert gas blanketing | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻² | 10–100 | Reduces ignition risk |
| Underground drainage | 10⁻² | 100 | Verified regularly |
| **Active Engineered IPLs** | | | |
| Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) | 10⁻² | 100 | Sized for worst case |
| Rupture Disc | 10⁻² to 10⁻³ | 100–1000 | Independent of PRV |
| SIF / SIS — SIL 1 | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻² | 10–100 | IEC 61511 verified |
| SIF / SIS — SIL 2 | 10⁻² to 10⁻³ | 100–1000 | IEC 61511 verified |
| SIF / SIS — SIL 3 | 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁴ | 1000–10000 | IEC 61511 verified |
| Flame / detonation arrester | 10⁻¹ | 10 | CCPS |
| Fire / gas detection + deluge | 10⁻² | 100 | Certified system |
| Excess Flow Valve | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻² | 10–100 | |
| Check Valve | 10⁻¹ | 10 | Single check low integrity |
| Dual Check Valves | 10⁻² | 100 | Verified regularly |
| **Procedural / Human IPLs** | | | |
| Operator action on BPCS alarm (≥30 min) | 10⁻¹ | 10 | Trained, well-defined |
| Operator action on BPCS alarm (5–30 min) | 10⁰ | 1 (NOT an IPL) | Too fast — rejected |
| Operator action on dedicated safety alarm (≥30 min) | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻² | 10–100 | High-quality alarm mgmt |
| **Emergency Response IPLs** | | | |
| Site fire brigade (≤10 min) | 10⁻¹ | 10 | Trained, equipped |
| Evacuation / Mustering | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻² | 10–100 | Documented |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independence Rule (KTU Examiner's Pet Topic):** For an IPL to be counted, it MUST be **independent** of:
> (a) the Initiating Event,
> (b) every other claimed IPL,
> (c) the BPCS unless it is itself the IPL being claimed.
> A "double-counted" IPL is the single most common reason LOPA teams get marked down in CCPS reports.

---

## 2.5 Mapping LOPA to SIL Determination (IEC 61511)

Once the required risk reduction is known, the SIL of a proposed SIF is selected:

$$
\text{Required SIL RRF} = \frac{f_{IE} \times \prod PFD_{\text{non-SIS IPLs}}}{f_{\text{target}}}
$$

| SIL | PFD Range | RRF Range |
|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | 10⁻² ≤ PFD < 10⁻¹ | 10 to 100 |
| **SIL 2** | 10⁻³ ≤ PFD < 10⁻² | 100 to 1000 |
| **SIL 3** | 10⁻⁴ ≤ PFD < 10⁻³ | 1000 to 10 000 |
| **SIL 4** | 10⁻⁵ ≤ PFD < 10⁻⁴ | 10 000 to 100 000 |

---

## 2.6 The Seven Steps of a LOPA Study

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Define scope, team, risk criteria | Charter |
| 2 | Identify scenarios (HAZOP, FMEA) | List of scenarios |
| 3 | Select single-cause scenarios | IE list |
| 4 | Identify candidate IPLs per scenario | IPL list |
| 5 | Estimate $f_{IE}$ and each PFD | Numerical inputs |
| 6 | Calculate $f_{\text{mitigated}}$ | Numerical risk |
| 7 | Compare to $f_{\text{target}}$; recommend action | Decision |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 7-Step Mnemonic — "S-S-S-I-C-C-C":** *Scope → Scenarios → Single causes → IPLs → Counts (frequencies) → Calculation → Compare*

---

## 2.7 Advantages and Limitations (For KTU Theory Questions)

**Advantages:**
- Faster and cheaper than full QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment).
- Objective, auditable, repeatable.
- Directly supports SIL determination (IEC 61511).
- Highlights missing IPLs and common-cause failures.

**Limitations:**
- Order-of-magnitude accuracy only (± factor of 10).
- Cannot capture detailed human reliability in depth.
- Requires expert team to verify independence.
- Limited usefulness for highly dynamic, complex scenarios.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates **two complete numerical examples** that are extremely common in KTU Module 4 questions. Both are solved using the master equation $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times \prod PFD_i$.

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — Atmospheric Storage Tank Overfill (Classic KTU Question)

### Problem Statement

A 500 m³ atmospheric storage tank contains **toluene** at ambient temperature. A level transmitter (LT) and Basic Process Control System (BPCS) control an inlet valve. The plant considers the following layers of protection:

1. **BPCS** control loop closes the inlet valve on high level.
2. **Operator** responds to a high-high level alarm within 30 minutes.
3. **Independent Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** closes a separate block valve on high-high-high level.
4. **Dike (bund wall)** surrounds the tank with capacity ≥ 110 % of tank volume.

**Target frequency** for a major pool fire (toluene ignition outside dike): $f_{\text{target}} = 10^{-5}$ / yr.

**Assumed Initiating Event**: Failure of the BPCS level control loop (valve fails open or level measurement fails).
**Assumed $f_{IE}$ for BPCS loop failure**: $10^{-1}$ / yr.

**Required:** Calculate the mitigated event frequency. Is the protection adequate? If not, recommend a SIL upgrade.

---

### Step 3.1.1 — Identify the Initiating Event and its Frequency

$$
f_{IE} = 10^{-1} \; \text{/yr}
$$

(BPCS loop failure — from the standard CCPS table in Section 2.3)

---

### Step 3.1.2 — Identify Valid IPLs and Assign PFDs

| Layer | Element | PFD | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| IPL-1 | BPCS control loop (closes inlet valve) | $10^{-1}$ | Standard CCPS BPCS value |
| IPL-2 | Operator on dedicated high-high alarm (≥30 min response) | $10^{-1}$ | Trained operator, well-defined alarm |
| IPL-3 | SIF — high-high-high level shutdown of block valve | $PFD_{\text{SIF}}$ (to be determined) | Independent of BPCS |
| IPL-4 | Dike (bund wall ≥ 110 % capacity) | $10^{-2}$ | Verified during inspection |

---

### Step 3.1.3 — Write the Master Equation

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times PFD_{\text{BPCS}} \times PFD_{\text{Operator}} \times PFD_{\text{SIF}} \times PFD_{\text{Dike}}
$$

Substituting numerical values (leaving SIF as a variable):

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times PFD_{\text{SIF}} \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-5} \times PFD_{\text{SIF}} \;\; \text{/yr}
$$

---

### Step 3.1.4 — Apply the Risk Target

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} \le f_{\text{target}}
$$

$$
10^{-5} \times PFD_{\text{SIF}} \le 10^{-5}
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{SIF}} \le 1.0 \;\; (\text{i.e. } 10^{0})
$$

This means **even a perfect SIF** barely meets the target. The current three existing IPLs (BPCS + Operator + Dike) are not enough; we need a SIF with significant reliability **OR** an additional IPL.

---

### Step 3.1.5 — Test a Candidate SIF

Suppose we install a **SIL 2 SIF** with $PFD_{\text{SIF}} = 10^{-3}$.

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-5} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-8} \; \text{/yr}
$$

$$
10^{-8} \le 10^{-5} \;\; \checkmark
$$

The target is comfortably met (with a safety margin of 1000×).

---

### Step 3.1.6 — Sensitivity / Cross-Check

If instead only **SIL 1 SIF** is installed ($PFD = 10^{-2}$):

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-5} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-7} \; \text{/yr} \;\; \checkmark
$$

SIL 1 is also acceptable, but a process safety management team would usually choose **SIL 2** to provide margin against uncertainty in $f_{IE}$ and operator reliability.

---

### Step 3.1.7 — Conclusion for Example 1

> **Decision:** Install a **SIL 2 SIF** on the tank high-high-high level. The resulting mitigated frequency is **10⁻⁸ per year**, three orders of magnitude below the target of **10⁻⁵ per year**.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Plugged Reactor Cooling Coil Leading to Runaway

### Problem Statement

A stirred-tank reactor is exothermic. Cooling is provided by a coil fed from a cooling-water header. **Cooling water pump trip** has occurred 3 times in 20 years of operation. The plant uses:

1. **BPCS** — temperature controller modulates cooling-water valve.
2. **High-temperature alarm + operator intervention** (response time ≈ 45 min).
3. **SIF (SIL 2)** — independent reactor dump valve to a quench vessel.
4. **Rupture Disc + Atmospheric Vent** to a catch tank.

**Target frequency** of catastrophic rupture: $f_{\text{target}} = 10^{-6}$ / yr.

**Required:** Compute the mitigated frequency and verify adequacy.

---

### Step 3.2.1 — Determine the Initiating Event Frequency

From operating history:

$$
f_{IE} = \frac{3 \text{ events}}{20 \text{ years}} = 0.15 \; \text{/yr} \approx 10^{-0.82} \; \text{/yr}
$$

In LOPA, we round to the nearest order of magnitude:

$$
f_{IE} \approx 10^{-1} \; \text{/yr}
$$

---

### Step 3.2.2 — Assign PFDs

| IPL | Element | PFD |
|---|---|---|
| IPL-1 | BPCS temperature control loop | $10^{-1}$ |
| IPL-2 | Operator on high-temperature alarm (≥30 min) | $10^{-1}$ |
| IPL-3 | SIF (SIL 2) — dump to quench vessel | $10^{-3}$ |
| IPL-4 | Rupture disc + atmospheric vent to catch tank | $10^{-2}$ |

---

### Step 3.2.3 — Calculate Mitigated Frequency

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3 \times PFD_4
$$

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-8} \; \text{/yr}
$$

---

### Step 3.2.4 — Compare to Target

$$
10^{-8} \le 10^{-6} \;\; \checkmark
$$

The protection is **adequate with a 100× safety margin**.

---

### Step 3.2.5 — Sensitivity: What if Operator IPL Is Removed?

If a Human Factors audit concludes the operator alarm IPL is **not reliable**:

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-7} \; \text{/yr}
$$

Still below $10^{-6}$ — but margin is reduced to 10×. Management may request **SIL 3** to restore margin.

---

### Step 3.2.6 — Conclusion for Example 2

> The combined BPCS + operator + SIL 2 SIF + rupture disc system reduces the catastrophic rupture frequency from **10⁻¹ /yr** (unmitigated) to **10⁻⁸ /yr** (mitigated). The risk target is satisfied.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Compressed Gas Leak in Loading Bay (Quick LOPA)

### Problem Statement

A truck loading bay transfers **chlorine** at 10 bar. The following IPLs are claimed:

- BPCS pressure control — PFD = 10⁻¹
- Operator on gas-detection alarm (≥30 min response) — PFD = 10⁻¹
- Automatic isolation valve on high-flow signal (SIL 1 SIF) — PFD = 10⁻²
- Site emergency response team — PFD = 10⁻¹

Initiating event: flexible hose rupture, $f_{IE} = 10^{-2}$ /yr.
Target for toxic release fatality outside site boundary: $f_{\text{target}} = 10^{-5}$ /yr.

---

### Calculation

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-7} \; \text{/yr}
$$

$$
10^{-7} \le 10^{-5} \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Margin = 100× → risk adequately controlled.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Some students count the same sensor twice (e.g., the same pressure transmitter for BPCS and the SIF). This is **NOT independent**. Always verify sensor independence before stacking PFDs.

---

## 3.4 Worked Master Table — General Template

| Item | Symbol | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initiating Event | IE | Cooling water failure | Site data |
| $f_{IE}$ | $f_{IE}$ | 10⁻¹ /yr | Rounded |
| IPL-1 | BPCS | 10⁻¹ | CCPS |
| IPL-2 | Operator | 10⁻¹ | CCPS |
| IPL-3 | SIF | 10⁻³ | SIL 2 |
| IPL-4 | Passive barrier | 10⁻² | Bund / RV |
| Product | ∏PFD | 10⁻⁷ | Calculated |
| Mitigated frequency | $f_{\text{mitigated}}$ | $f_{IE} \times \prod PFD = 10^{-8}$ /yr | Calculated |
| Target | $f_{\text{target}}$ | 10⁻⁶ /yr | Company |
| Status | — | **Adequate (margin 100×)** | Decision |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LOPA Workflow — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["HAZOP or FMEA Output -- Scenario List"] --> node2["Select Single-Cause Scenario and Define Consequence"]
    node2 --> node3["Identify Initiating Event and Assign fIE"]
    node3 --> node4["List Candidate Independent Protection Layers"]
    node4 --> node5["Verify IPL Independence and Auditable PFD"]
    node5 --> node6["Compute fmitigated using Master Equation"]
    node6 --> node7["Compare fmitigated with Risk Target ftarget"]
    node7 --> node8["Decision A -- Acceptable Risk as is"]
    node7 --> node9["Decision B -- Add or Upgrade IPL"]
    node7 --> node10["Decision C -- Reduce fIE Procedurally"]
    node9 --> node11["Reassess and Loop Back"]
    node10 --> node11["Reassess and Loop Back"]
    node11 --> node6
    node8 --> node12["Document LOPA Sheet and SIL Decision"]
    node9 --> node12["Document LOPA Sheet and SIL Decision"]
    node10 --> node12["Document LOPA Sheet and SIL Decision"]
```

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of IPLs (Layer Cake Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Consequence -- Pool Fire, Toxic Release, Explosion"] --> nodeB["IPL Level 5 -- Emergency Response, Site Fire Brigade, Mustering"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["IPL Level 4 -- Mitigation: Bund, Dike, Catch Tank, Fire Deluge"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["IPL Level 3 -- Prevention: SIF or SIS of Required SIL"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["IPL Level 2 -- Prevention: Operator Action on Dedicated Alarm"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["IPL Level 1 -- Prevention: BPCS Control Loop"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Initiating Event -- Pump Failure, Valve Failure, Human Error, External Event"]
```

---

## 4.3 Example 1 — Tank Overfill LOPA Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IE["Initiating Event -- BPCS Level Loop Failure, fIE = 10-1 per year"] --> L1["IPL 1 -- BPCS Loop Retries, PFD = 10-1"]
    L1 --> L2["IPL 2 -- Operator on High-High Alarm, PFD = 10-1"]
    L2 --> L3["IPL 3 -- SIL 2 SIF Closes Block Valve, PFD = 10-3"]
    L3 --> L4["IPL 4 -- Dike Contains Spill, PFD = 10-2"]
    L4 --> CON["Consequence -- Major Pool Fire of Toluene"]
    CON --> CALC["fmitigated = 10-1 x 10-1 x 10-1 x 10-3 x 10-2 = 10-8 per year"]
    CALC --> DEC{"Is 10-8 less than ftarget 10-5?"}
    DEC -- YES --> ACC["Acceptable -- SIL 2 SIF Recommended"]
    DEC -- NO --> REJ["Add IPL or Upgrade SIL"]
```

---

## 4.4 Independence Check Matrix (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sub1["Layer A -- BPCS Sensor and Control Valve"] -.->|"Same sensor shared -- NOT independent"| sub2["Layer B -- SIF High Level Trip"]
    sub2 -.->|"Independent sensor and valve"| sub3["Layer C -- Pressure Relief Valve"]
    sub3 -.->|"Mechanical, no electronics"| sub4["Layer D -- Dike or Bund Wall"]
    sub4 -.->|"Civil structural protection"| sub5["Layer E -- Emergency Response"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed arrow between BPCS and SIF marks a *violation* of independence — the same level transmitter cannot serve both IPLs without proper separation. The solid arrows from SIF onwards represent **truly independent** layers.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, PEPOT754]**
**Define Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA). List any two advantages of LOPA over a full Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA).**

**Model Answer:**

LOPA is a semi-quantitative risk assessment method used to evaluate the risk of process hazard scenarios. It estimates the **mitigated event frequency** by multiplying the **initiating event frequency** ($f_{IE}$) with the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** of each Independent Protection Layer (IPL), and compares the result with a **risk tolerance target**.

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i
$$

**Two advantages over QRA:**
1. **Speed and cost** — LOPA requires significantly less data, time, and expertise than a full QRA, while still providing a numerical risk estimate suitable for SIL determination.
2. **Transparency and auditability** — Each PFD and $f_{IE}$ is explicitly documented and traced back to published sources (CCPS, IEC 61511), making the study easy for regulators and auditors to verify.

*(Any other relevant point: bridges qualitative HAZOP and quantitative QRA; focused on order-of-magnitude answers; ideal for SIL selection.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, PEPOT754]**
**Explain the term "Independent Protection Layer" (IPL). List any four criteria an element must satisfy to qualify as an IPL.**

**Model Answer:**

An **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** is a device, system, or human action that can **prevent a process hazard scenario** from progressing to its undesired consequence, while remaining **independent** of the initiating event and of all other claimed IPLs.

**Four qualifying criteria:**
1. **Effectiveness** — The element must reliably prevent the scenario when functioning as intended.
2. **Independence** — It must not be affected by the initiating event or by the failure of any other IPL (no shared sensors, valves, power, or logic).
3. **Auditability** — Its design basis, maintenance, and test records must be documented and verifiable.
4. **Quantified reliability** — Its PFD must be known and supported by industry data (CCPS, OREDA, IEC 61511).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 4, PEPOT754]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **step-by-step methodology** for conducting a Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA). Mention clearly the role of HAZOP and IEC 61511 in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers **hexane** at 8 bar to a downstream distillation column. The following layers of protection are claimed:

- BPCS flow control loop: PFD = 10⁻¹
- Operator on low-flow alarm (≥30 min response): PFD = 10⁻¹
- SIL 2 Safety Instrumented Function that trips the pump on low pressure: PFD = 10⁻³
- Pressure Relief Valve on the discharge line: PFD = 10⁻²

Initiating event frequency for pump seal failure: $f_{IE} = 10^{-1}$ /yr.
Risk target for a major hexane pool fire: $f_{\text{target}} = 10^{-5}$ /yr.

Calculate the mitigated event frequency and state whether the protection is adequate. If not, what action would you recommend? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a): LOPA Methodology (7 Marks)**

A LOPA study is conducted in **seven structured steps**:

1. **Define scope and assemble a team** — A multidisciplinary team is formed (process, instrumentation, operations, safety). Risk tolerance criteria ($f_{\text{target}}$) and the scope of the study are documented in a charter.

2. **Identify scenarios** — Inputs are taken from prior qualitative studies, mainly **HAZOP** and **FMEA**. Each node or deviation is examined for credible worst-case scenarios.

3. **Select single-cause scenarios** — Complex multi-cause events are simplified into a single initiating cause + consequence pair to keep LOPA tractable.

4. **Identify candidate IPLs** — Each scenario is examined for prevention, mitigation, and emergency response layers. **Independence** is rigorously verified (no shared sensors, utilities, or logic).

5. **Estimate frequencies and PFDs** — $f_{IE}$ values come from CCPS, OREDA, or site data; PFDs from CCPS tables, IEC 61511 SIL data, or vendor reliability certificates.

6. **Compute mitigated frequency** — Apply the master equation.

7. **Compare with target and recommend action** — If $f_{\text{mitigated}} \le f_{\text{target}}$, the scenario is acceptable. If not, additional IPLs, a higher-SIL SIF, or reduced $f_{IE}$ are recommended.

**Role of HAZOP:** HAZOP identifies scenarios and their consequences; LOPA quantifies them.

**Role of IEC 61511:** LOPA outputs the required risk reduction, which is then used to select the **SIL** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) per IEC 61511.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b): Numerical LOPA (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the master equation:**

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times PFD_{\text{BPCS}} \times PFD_{\text{Op}} \times PFD_{\text{SIF}} \times PFD_{\text{PRV}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply exponents:**

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-(1+1+1+3+2)} = 10^{-8} \; \text{/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with target:**

$$
10^{-8} \le 10^{-5} \;\; \checkmark
$$

The mitigated frequency is **three orders of magnitude below** the risk target. Protection is **adequate**.

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

No additional IPL is required. However, it is recommended that the SIL 2 SIF be **re-validated every 5 years** and that **proof testing** of the SIF be scheduled at intervals consistent with its assumed PFD.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **"Swiss Cheese Model"** as applied in process safety. How does LOPA extend this concept numerically? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LPG bullet storage facility has the following layers:

- BPCS level/pressure control: PFD = 10⁻¹
- High-level alarm with operator action (response time = 20 min): claimed PFD = 10⁰
- SIL 2 SIF on high-high level: PFD = 10⁻³
- Bund wall: PFD = 10⁻²

Initiating event: control valve passing open, $f_{IE} = 10^{-1}$ /yr.
Risk target: $f_{\text{target}} = 10^{-6}$ /yr.

A LOPA team rejects the "operator on alarm" as a valid IPL because of the 20-minute response time being too short. Recalculate the mitigated frequency. Comment on the result and the independence rule. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a): Swiss Cheese Model (7 Marks)**

The **Swiss Cheese Model** (James Reason, 1990) conceptualizes accidents as the consequence of failures aligning across multiple defensive layers. Each layer ("slice of cheese") has holes representing latent weaknesses, failures, or gaps. An accident occurs when the holes in all slices momentarily line up, allowing the hazard to reach the asset, person, or environment.

**Diagram (textual schematic):**

```
[Initiating Event] --> [BPCS] --> [Operator Alarm] --> [SIF/SIS] --> [Passive Relief] --> [Emergency Response] --> [Consequence]
         |                |              |                  |                  |                        |
       Hole 1           Hole 2         Hole 3            Hole 4              Hole 5                  Hole 6
                          \             |                  |                  |                      /
                           \            V                  V                  V                     /
                            ----> ACCIDENT when holes align <-------------------------
```

**How LOPA extends the model:**

LOPA assigns a **numerical PFD** to each "hole" and an $f_{IE}$ to the hazard. It calculates the *probability* of all holes aligning by multiplying PFDs. Where the model is qualitative ("alignment is bad"), LOPA is quantitative ("alignment frequency = $10^{-8}$/yr"). This numerical risk is then compared to a tolerance to decide whether more layers are required.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b): LPG Bullet LOPA with Rejected IPL (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify valid IPLs:**

- BPCS — valid IPL, PFD = 10⁻¹
- Operator alarm (20 min) — **REJECTED as IPL** because CCPS requires ≥30 min response time. PFD cannot be claimed → set PFD = 1 (effectively no protection).
- SIL 2 SIF — PFD = 10⁻³
- Bund wall — PFD = 10⁻²

**Step 2 — Compute mitigated frequency:**

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times PFD_{\text{BPCS}} \times 1 \times PFD_{\text{SIF}} \times PFD_{\text{Bund}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 10^{-7} \; \text{/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with target:**

$$
10^{-7} \le 10^{-6} \;\; \checkmark
$$

The protection is still adequate, but the margin is only **10×**, much smaller than the 1000× margin recommended by CCPS for procedural uncertainty.

**Step 4 — Comment on the Independence Rule:**

The **Independence Rule** requires that no two claimed IPLs share a sensor, valve, logic solver, or utility. In this case:
- BPCS and SIF MUST have **separate level transmitters** and **separate block valves**.
- The bund wall is a **passive civil structure**, automatically independent of process instrumentation.
- If the same transmitter feeds both BPCS and SIF, then **only one** may be counted as an IPL — this is the most common LOPA error.

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

Either install an **independent level transmitter for the SIF** OR upgrade the SIF from **SIL 2 to SIL 3** (PFD = 10⁻⁴) to restore a comfortable risk margin.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in LOPA Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply** all PFDs — students often divide or add. **Always multiply independent probabilities.**
> 2. **Double-counting** the same sensor/valve in both BPCS and SIF — verify independence.
> 3. **Claiming operator response in < 30 min** as a valid IPL — CCPS requires ≥30 minutes for PFD ≤ 10⁻¹.
> 4. **Forgetting to express results in orders of magnitude** — LOPA is a *semi-quantitative* method; one-decimal answers are wrong.
> 5. **Confusing frequency (per year) with probability (per demand)** — clarify the units in the solution.
> 6. **Ignoring passive IPLs** like dikes or fireproofing — they are often the cheapest and most reliable layers.
> 7. **Not stating the SIL selection logic** explicitly — write: *"RRF needed is 1000×, hence SIL 2."*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **LOPA = Semi-quantitative** risk assessment bridging HAZOP and QRA.
- [x] **Master Equation:** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{IE} \times \prod PFD_i$.
- [x] **Initiating Event Frequency ($f_{IE}$):** Site data or CCPS standard table.
- [x] **IPL PFD Range:** 10⁻¹ (operator) → 10⁻⁴ (SIL 3) → 10⁻² (passive dike).
- [x] **Independence is non-negotiable** — same sensor cannot serve two IPLs.
- [x] **Operator IPL** requires response time ≥ 30 minutes and well-defined alarm.
- [x] **SIL Selection:** Calculate required RRF; map to SIL-1/2/3/4 (IEC 61511).
- [x] **Risk Target** typically 10⁻⁶/yr for catastrophic events, 10⁻⁵/yr for serious.
- [x] **Margin of 10×–100×** between $f_{\text{mitigated}}$ and $f_{\text{target}}$ is good practice.
- [x] **Documentation:** Every LOPA study produces a *worksheet* with columns: Scenario, IE, $f_{IE}$, IPL-1, IPL-2, …, $f_{\text{mitigated}}$, $f_{\text{target}}$, Decision.
- [x] **Cross-check:** Sensitivity analysis — what if operator IPL is removed? What if $f_{IE}$ is 10× higher?
- [x] **LOPA ≠ HAZOP:** HAZOP finds scenarios; LOPA assigns them numbers.
- [x] **Order-of-magnitude accuracy** — answers to nearest 10ⁿ are acceptable.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a numerical LOPA question is asked, write the master equation first, list IPLs with PFDs, then **show the exponent sum step**. Most marks are awarded for the *method*, not just the final number. Always end with a one-line **decision statement** — *"The mitigated frequency $10^{-8}$/yr is below the target $10^{-5}$/yr; protection is adequate with margin 1000×."*
